## Thread: strange output — 5 this month / 9 total

_Thread started [2001-09](2001-09.md#t-9f9af9df7a)._

##### ↳ ↳ Re: strange output

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-10-02T20:31:56+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BBA162C.39F0E2CA@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<b7bceb3e.0109300947.1ff4c76a@posting.google.com> <bg0frt0dj7kg1feef4hp82tb419e7p52pt@4ax.com>`

```
Binyamin Dissen wrote:
> 
> The record area in the FILE
> SECTION is not really part of your data area, it is a system area mapped to
> your program.

That depends on the implementation.  With MF Cobol I believe the record
areas _are_ part of the program.  YMMV.

> Most programmers would recommend having the detailed output record area in the
> WORKING-STORAGE SECTION and just an 01 level in the file section, and to use
> WRITE FROM.

That recommendation may be true of the programmers that you know.
Whether this is 'most' is indeterminate. Personally I see nothing wrong
with an output record being created and written in file section, just
make sure that a MOVE SPACES (or similar) to the record area is done
before the fields are populated. And if I do use a WS area, for example
page headings, I never use WRITE .. FROM, I use a MOVE followed by a
WRITE.  Using a decent editor allows me to duplicate the MOVE x to y
line and change this to WRITE y in less keystrokes than typing the FROM.

> I don't understand how the trailing zeroes are being suppressed while using
> either PIC 9(4)V99 or X(6).

They aren't 'being suppressed', they never existed (most likely), he is
doing an alphanumeric move and has the record misaligned so that he
misses getting the full data item when defined as PIC X(6), or a 163
error when defined as 9(4)V99.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: strange output

- **From:** pauldbq@yahoo.com (dbq)
- **Date:** 2001-10-03T20:12:08-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b7bceb3e.0110031912.74309508@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b7bceb3e.0109300947.1ff4c76a@posting.google.com> <9p84cm$r2k$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I want to thank all who helped me resolve this issue.  I particularly
want to thank John for the DEBUG command.  I added the following to my
process module:

          DISPLAY 'DEBUG~ TOTAL >' MONTHLY-SALARY-TOTAL '<'      
          DISPLAY 'DEBUG~ EMPLOYEES >' NUMBER-OF-EMPLOYEES '<'

The above lines allowed me to determine that my INPUT data was
corrupted.  William was right (see below) in that there must have been
some control characters at the end of each record.  I went to the end
of each record and deleted those invisible characters.  (Aside: 
anyway to make them visible?)

<snip>

> A) you probably want a numeric-edited rather than numeric field in your
> output (print) file, i.e. "." rather than "V" in the PICTURE.

William, good idea!  I used a PIC ZZZ9.99 for my output.

<snip>

> 1) Do you define your input (and probably output) files as "LINE SEQUENTIAL"
> in the Select/Assign clause?  If the input file is "delimited" by
> carriage-return and line-feed (i.e. a "normal" PC or Unix file) then this
> could cause you to get "odd" results and to seem to get non-numeric data in
> numeric input fields.

William hit the nail on the head!

<snip>

> HINT:  In the "real world" it is usually a VERY GOOD idea to check numeric
> fields with the "IF NUMERIC" test before trying to use them as numeric data.
> My guess is that you are very early in your COBOL training, but you will
> soon be required to deal with "invalid" input.

<snip>

William is right again.  The 'IF' stuff is coming up.  I'm certainly
not looking forward to dealing with 'invalid input' however.

Again, thanks to all who responded!

Paul
```

#### ↳ Re: strange output

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-01T00:53:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_7Pt7.10547$ev2.16955@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<b7bceb3e.0109300947.1ff4c76a@posting.google.com>`

```
HI Paul,

The unwanted A/N chars in the detail line is the heading line chars that have
not been overlaid by your detail moves. Try moving SPACES to PRINT-REC before
you move the detail data.

The only reason I can think of that could cause an error when using PIC 9(4)V99
is that the field sometimes contains SPACES or other A/N chars. My bet is
SPACES. Does the error occur on the 1st record? As Benjimin suggested DISPLAY
the field, but keep it as PIC X(6). Also write the display like this:

DISPLAY 'DEBUG~ SALARY >' MONTHLY-SALARY-IN '<'                   

This will delimit the display and you can determine if spaces or other non-
displayable chars are in the field.

You may want to check the lengths of the I/P fields before the salary to make
sure they match the field lengths in the I/P. This could be the cause of the
error too.

HTH, Jack. 



In article <b7bceb3e.0109300947.1ff4c76a@posting.google.com>, dbq says...
>
>I'm getting some strange output when I run MF Cobol.  I have Animator
…[77 more quoted lines elided]…
>ALPHANUMERIC field.
```

#### ↳ Re: strange output

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-01T02:51:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BB7DB64.51E08D10@home.com>`
- **References:** `<b7bceb3e.0109300947.1ff4c76a@posting.google.com>`

```


dbq wrote:

> I'm getting some strange output when I run MF Cobol.  I have Animator
> 2. V.8.2.75 on Windows.  Let me preface this by saying I think that I
> have a compiler directive set that I can't set back to the default.
> So my first question is how do you set all the compiler directives
> back to their default values?

Paul,

Can't think why you would want Directives for this one - just delete them
(the lines starting $set ... in column 7), and the Directives are re-set
to their defaults.

The following works, and I also show the ouput PrintFile. The only change
I've made is to show the PrintFile salary field as numeric edited.

You can give the files any extensions you want "dat", "dta", "doc" or
"text". The only one that M/F is fussy about is that you don't use "idx"
because that signifies the index file for an ISAM data file.

Compare below with what you've done to see if there is a difference. Check
your MOVEs to the print fields.

*>---------------------- payroll.cbl ----------------------------

INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.

     Select               Payroll-File
     assign               "c:\PayrollFile.dat"
     organization         line sequential
     access               sequential
     file status          FileStatus.

     Select               Print-File
     assign               "c:\PrintFile.dat"
     organization         line sequential
     access               sequential
     file status          FileStatus.


FILE SECTION.
*>00001SMITH               0101050000123456789012345678901234567890
*>00002JONES               0201100000987654321098765432109876543210
*>kkkkknnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnttoossssssiiiiiiiiiddjjuuuuuuuuuuuuuuuuu

FD  PAYROLL-FILE. *> don't need LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD.
01  PAYROLL-REC.
    05  EMPLOYEE-NUMBER-IN                PIC X(5).
    05  EMPLOYEE-NAME-IN                  PIC X(20).
    05  LOCATION-CODE-IN.
        10  TERRITORY-NUMBER-IN           PIC XX.
        10  OFFICE-NUMBER-IN              PIC XX.
    05  MONTHLY-SALARY-IN                 PIC 9(4)V99.
    05  SOCIAL-SECURITY-NUMBER-IN         PIC X(9).
    05  NUMBER-OF-DEPENDENTS-IN           PIC XX.
    05  JOB-CLASSIFICATION-CODE-IN        PIC XX.
    05  UNUSED-IN                         PIC X(22).


FD PRINT-FILE. *> Ditto -  LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD.
01 PRINT-REC.
   05                                PIC X(5).
   05  EMPLOYEE-NUMBER-OUT           PIC X(5) VALUE SPACES.
   05                                PIC XX.
   05  EMPLOYEE-NAME-OUT             PIC X(20) VALUE SPACES.
   05                                PIC X.
   05  TERRITORY-NUMBER-OUT          PIC XX VALUE SPACES.
   05                                PIC X(3).
   05  OFFICE-NUMBER-OUT             PIC XX VALUE SPACES.
   05                                PIC XX.
   05  MONTHLY-SALARY-OUT            PIC zzz9.99 Blank when zero.
   05                                PIC XX.
   05  SOCIAL-SECURITY-NUMBER-OUT    PIC X(9) VALUE SPACES.
   05                                PIC XX.
   05  NUMBER-OF-DEPENDENTS-OUT      PIC XX VALUE SPACES.
   05                                PIC XX.
   05  JOB-CLASSIFICATION-CODE-OUT   PIC XX VALUE SPACES.
   05                                PIC X(3).

Working-Storage Section.
01 FileFlag                        pic 9.
    88 FileNotFinished             value 1.
    88 FileFinished                value 0.
01 FileStatus                      pic x(02).

Procedure Division.
 Open INPUT  Payroll-File
 Open OUTPUT Print-File
 set FileNotFinished to true
 perform until FileFinished
   Read Payroll-File next record
      at end
          set FileFinished to true
      not at end
          perform A-PRINT-RECORD
   End-read
 End-Perform
 Close Payroll-File, Print-File
 STOP RUN.

A-PRINT-RECORD.

move EMPLOYEE-NUMBER-IN          to EMPLOYEE-NUMBER-OUT
move EMPLOYEE-NAME-IN            to EMPLOYEE-NAME-OUT
move TERRITORY-NUMBER-IN         to TERRITORY-NUMBER-OUT
move OFFICE-NUMBER-IN            to OFFICE-NUMBER-OUT
move MONTHLY-SALARY-IN           to MONTHLY-SALARY-OUT
move SOCIAL-SECURITY-NUMBER-IN   to SOCIAL-SECURITY-NUMBER-OUT
move NUMBER-OF-DEPENDENTS-IN     to NUMBER-OF-DEPENDENTS-OUT
move JOB-CLASSIFICATION-CODE-IN  to JOB-CLASSIFICATION-CODE-OUT
write PRINT-REC

*> Tip - reference above - check the syntax for MOVE CORRESPONDING.....
.
*>--------------------------------------------------------.
 And your PrintFile looks like :-

     00001  SMITH                01   01   500.00  123456789  01  23
     00002  JONES                02   01  1000.00  987654321  09  87
*>----------------------------------------------------------------------------

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: strange output

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-01T03:30:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BB7E4A0.AADF3F67@home.com>`
- **References:** `<b7bceb3e.0109300947.1ff4c76a@posting.google.com>`

```


dbq wrote:

Paul,

Further thoughts after my  last message. This *may* be what you are doing
incorrectly. Your print layout.

In working storage you want :-

01 HeaderRecord1 pic x(???)
01 HeaderRecord2 pic x(???)
01 DetailRecord .
           05                                  PIC X(5).
           05  EMPLOYEE-NUMBER-OUT             PIC X(5) VALUE SPACES.
           05                                  PIC XX.
           etc.

Then specify your File Print-Rec as pic x(??) for the LARGEST of the
above.

Write Print-Rec from HeaderRecord1
Write Print-Rec from HeaderRecord2
move spces to Print-Rec
write print-rec
Now do the perform read-next and for each incoming record
    MOVE x to y
    write print-rec from Detail-Record

Note you formatted your SSN :-

   987-65-4321 - this is ELEVEN characters not the pic x(09) you specified
for output.

Jimmy
```

## Thread: Info reqd about NEC mainframe — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Info reqd about NEC mainframe

- **From:** "Bhaskar Reddy" <reddy.bhaskar@in.bosch.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-01T14:24:06+05:30
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9p9b2a$hnp$1@proxy.fe.internet.bosch.com>`

```
Hello,

   I am looking for some information about the NEC mainframes but not able
to find much on the NEC site.

I know COBOL is the host language and so posting on this group hoping
someone knows about it.
The information I need is

1. IS there any other language apart from COBOL on this NEC mainframes ?
2. What is the OLTP system used, I mean equivalent of CICS on IBM mainframe
?
3. What is the file system used (If there is anything), I mean equivalent of
VSAM on IBM mainframes ?
4. What is the database used ?
5. Is the COBOL and JCL on NEC mainframe much different from the COBOL and
JCL of IBM mainframes ?

Any internet links will do.

TIA & REGARDS
Bhaskar Reddy.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Interview Questions

- **From:** "James" <mangogrower@telocity.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-01T13:51:08-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nG2u7.5272$gQ.3502946@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net>`
- **References:** `<3bb701cd_2@corp-news.newsgroups.com>`

```
Well, generally for COBOL some interview / test questions I've seen are:

describe 'COMP'
how many bytes does a PIC 9(05) COMP-3 occupy?
how many bytes does a PIC 9(10) COMP-1 item occupy?
what is the difference between 'SEARCH' and 'SEARCH ALL'?
how would you 'UPDATE' a record?
what are the four DIVISIONS in a COBOL program?
what are the types of 'variable' length records?

How much time do you think is needed to work?
(just starting out, as much as necessary to get the job done right, until
I'm familair with
the procedures, policies, tools, system)

Good Luck
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Interview Questions

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-03T06:03:23+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b7bkrt8i1siut1fp4nunfub3vb3kb986vt@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3bb701cd_2@corp-news.newsgroups.com> <nG2u7.5272$gQ.3502946@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net>`

```
On Mon, 1 Oct 2001 13:51:08 -0400 "James" <mangogrower@telocity.com> wrote:

:>Well, generally for COBOL some interview / test questions I've seen are:

:>describe 'COMP'

Well.....

OK, I guess.

:>how many bytes does a PIC 9(05) COMP-3 occupy?

What platform?

Why should the employer care?

:>how many bytes does a PIC 9(10) COMP-1 item occupy?

Why should the employer care?

How often have you seen a comp-1 (floating point number in IBM terminology) in
a COBOL program? Or are you referring to a different platform?

:>what is the difference between 'SEARCH' and 'SEARCH ALL'?

OK.

:>how would you 'UPDATE' a record?

Good general programming question.

:>what are the four DIVISIONS in a COBOL program?

You can even have personnel ask this question.

:>what are the types of 'variable' length records?

I couldn't answer this.

:>How much time do you think is needed to work?
:>(just starting out, as much as necessary to get the job done right, until
:>I'm familair with
:>the procedures, policies, tools, system)

What kind of answer would you expect? I have no idea what you are asking.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Interview Questions

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-10-03T14:19:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9pf6mf$o9m$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3bb701cd_2@corp-news.newsgroups.com> <nG2u7.5272$gQ.3502946@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net> <b7bkrt8i1siut1fp4nunfub3vb3kb986vt@4ax.com>`

```
On  2-Oct-2001, Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote:

> >Well, generally for COBOL some interview / test questions I've seen are:
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> What platform?

The one where the programmer is trying to get a job.

> Why should the employer care?

The person taking the interview doesn't care why the employer asked this -
it is a common question nevertheless.  I suppose it shows that the
interviewer knows the local platform.

> :>what are the four DIVISIONS in a COBOL program?
>
> You can even have personnel ask this question.

The original question was what the interviewer should expect to be asked. 
"You" is the person being asked, not the person doing the asking.

Smart questions, stupid questions, whatever questions - he wants to know
what to expect:

e.g.

What are your long term career goals?
Why did you pick CoBOL?
What do you know about our business?
Do you have CoBOL II experience? ( I had this asked once when I had the same
type of programming experience on a VAX - but they didn't call it CoBOL II.)
What is a VSAM file? (Mention KSDS in your answer)
Have you worked with accounting/sales/whatever?  (be ready to say what you
have done)
What is structured programming?
How do you write out data so that a user can read it in his spreadsheet?

Any question that interfaces CoBOL with the company's database.

What do you look for in a walk-thru?
How do you write a test plan?
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Interview Questions

- **From:** Charles <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-05T15:10:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<A3kv7.17290$ev2.27385@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<3bb701cd_2@corp-news.newsgroups.com>`

```
I think you have to worry more about the stupid questions like: 

"If you have an employee that you can not get along with, how do you handle it?"

"What is the one thing you have a problem with on the job?"

"On this job you may have to work some overtime, is that OK with you?"

"Tell us about the worst problem you ever had in the workplace..."

To the division question, I was taught to say "In Every Damn Program".  (The
first letters stand for the divisions.)  They might get a laugh out of that!

Actually it only takes one division to compile a program.

A good one would be to show someone a picture clause for a COMP-3 field and ask
them how much space it will take up, And then ask them how to take a ssn from an
alpha-numeric field and make it a 5 digit packed field.

Tables are always good question- 

"If you had a table with 5 totals for each of the 12 months out of the year, how
would you code the 2 level table for it?

Or better yet  "I just copy a similar program and change it a little bit, why
reinvent the wheel!"

If they really want to ask you this many questions they should just have a
formal test or something.

Questions are not worth much, it would be better to hand someone a program with
10 errors and be asked how to fix the problem.  

I think it is perfectly acceptable to consult a Language reference or
programming guide from time to time.

>
>I recently graduated from college with an A.A.S. degree in Computer
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

## Thread: OO COBOL Class vs. Factory? — 3 this month / 11 total

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo)_

_Thread started [2001-09](2001-09.md#t-c8123935f7)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO COBOL Class vs. Factory?

- **From:** Thane Hubbell <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-01T15:07:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pE%t7.11156$ev2.17441@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<9p0dul0iv1@enews3.newsguy.com> <Li6t7.8806$ev2.16173@www.newsranger.com> <9p2vi1018ul@enews3.newsguy.com>`

```
I'm pretty sure MicroFocus (at least with the ISO2000 directive) does have
Factory.  Class-Object describes the instance object, not the factory.  (Jimmy -
correct me if I am wrong.)

In article <9p2vi1018ul@enews3.newsguy.com>, Ahangar says...
>
>Thanks for the explanation. My exposure to OO COBOL has been with Micro
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO COBOL Class vs. Factory?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-01T17:38:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BB8AB67.5E45BAF@home.com>`
- **References:** `<9p0dul0iv1@enews3.newsguy.com> <Li6t7.8806$ev2.16173@www.newsranger.com> <9p2vi1018ul@enews3.newsguy.com> <pE%t7.11156$ev2.17441@www.newsranger.com>`

```


Thane Hubbell wrote:

> I'm pretty sure MicroFocus (at least with the ISO2000 directive) does have
> Factory.  Class-Object describes the instance object, not the factory.  (Jimmy -
> correct me if I am wrong.)

Will Price tried it, and I've tried it - I don't think the ISO2000 Directive does
much for you, other than trying to get the 'future' syntax - but I gave up as I had
compiler errors.

Where I'm using FACTORY, I'm not setting any directives - just using the word
'FACTORY" instead of "CLASS-OBJECT". To recap :-

    Class-id.
    FACTORY or CLASS-OBJECT (It is Class-object that you see in all M/F
        examples)
    Factory Methods
    END FACTORY
    *>---------
    OBJECT
    Object Methods
    END OBJECT
    *>--------
    End Class

Something which you are indirectly familiar with - my file handling. Given I want 10
different files all using a 'common' Class - Line Sequential,. I invoke Line
Sequential "new" 10 times with the method "new" in Factory. The 'common' file
specification (occurs a to b depending on c) appears at the top. The file access
VERB methods appear only in the Object 'Section', after Factory.

Brief example :-

*>---------------------------------------------
Class-id.              LineSequentialFile inherits from FileErrors.
Class-control.      LineSequentialFile      is class "lseqfile"
                           FileErrors                   is class "filerror"
INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
     Select               Data-File
     assign               etc.......
FILE SECTION.
FD  Data-File
    record varying from 0 to 1500 characters
       depending on DataRecordSize.
01  Data-record pic x(1500).
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
01 DataFilename                       pic x(100).
01 DataRecordSize                   pic x(4) comp-5 value 0.
01 FileStatus                             pic x(02).
*>----------------------------------------------------
FACTORY.
*>----------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "new".
*>----------------------------------------------------
Linkage section.
01 lnk-self                object reference.
Procedure Division returning lnk-self.
   invoke super "new" returning lnk-self
End Method "new".
*>----------------------------------------------------
End FACTORY.
*>----------------------------------------------------
OBJECT.
*>----------------------------------------------------
OBJECT-STORAGE SECTION.
*> above becomes W-S Section in COBOL 2002
01 os-Parent                        object reference.
*> This is my parent Window so I can pass this to FileErrors for a message box

*> Following is one of the methods applicable to a Line Sequential
*>-------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "ReadNext".
*>-------------------------------------------------------
*> "dbresult" - I'm using a common file for Level 88's for both COBOL files
*> and DB Tables (MS Access)

Linkage section.
01 lnk-values.
copy "dbresult.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==05 lnk==.
05 lnk-record.
   10  occurs 1 to 1500
       depending on DataRecordSize     pic x.
Procedure Division returning lnk-values.
  set ResultOK to true
  Read Data-File
    At End
      Set FileFinis to True
    Not at End
      Move Data-record to lnk-record
  End-Read

  Evaluate true
    when FileFinis and FileStatus = "10"
         continue
    when FileStatus <> "00"
         set FileError to true
         invoke self "fileError"
  End-evaluate

End Method "ReadNext".
*>-------------------------------------------------------
End OBJECT.
End CLASS LineSequentialFile.
*>-------------------------------------------------------

Jimmy
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OO COBOL Class vs. Factory?

- **From:** Thane Hubbell <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-01T15:10:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<UH%t7.11160$ev2.17359@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<9p0dul0iv1@enews3.newsguy.com> <9p7f2g01mis@enews3.newsguy.com>`

```
You are welcome.  And just a note - I was wrong - Jimmy's reply was correct on
the Class-Object = Factory issue.
```

```
quick.

COBOL
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Good Cobol Tutorial

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 2001-10-08T12:36:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3bc1994b.362271@news.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<9p7d1k$ek2$03$1@news.t-online.com> <9p82vl020mg@enews1.newsguy.com> <92861a7b.0110040853.2ce8b099@posting.google.com> <38315c2d.0110050657.70c6035b@posting.google.com>`

```
jon_bills@hotmail.com (Jon Bills) wrote:

>ammarz77@yahoo.com (Ammar) wrote in message news:<92861a7b.0110040853.2ce8b099@posting.google.com>...
>> I'm interested in learning COBOL.
>
>Perhaps you should see a doctor. Here's a handy link for you:
>http://www.tuxedo.org/~esr/jargon/html/entry/COBOL.html

Jon:

Are you still in high school?  Have you only recently graduated?  The
reason I ask these questions, is because your comments seem to
indicate that you may not be very familiar with the business
programming world.

When you attend college and eventually get a job in the real world,
you'll learn that the comments made by your high school computer
science instructors shouldn't be taken at face value.

Professional programmers and systems analysts are largely in agreement
that learning as many programming languages as practical can only
enhance one's career opportunities.  A programmer who has experience
writing COBOL, C++, Java, Visual Basic and Assembler is in far greater
demand and can earn much higher salaries than one who is only
experienced in a single programming language.

After you graduate, you'll also learn that each programming language,
including C++, has both advantages as well as disadvantages.  There is
no such thing as a  programming language which is suitable for every
possible task.

But, you'll learn these things as you continue your education and
embark upon your career in programming.  Good luck and remember to
keep an open mind!

>> I'm looking for source code in c++, especially data structure fields
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Jon.


Bob Wolfe
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Good Cobol Tutorial

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-10-11T16:39:48+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3bc5151e_5@news.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<9p7d1k$ek2$03$1@news.t-online.com> <9p82vl020mg@enews1.newsguy.com> <92861a7b.0110040853.2ce8b099@posting.google.com> <38315c2d.0110050657.70c6035b@posting.google.com> <3bc1994b.362271@news.epix.net> <38315c2d.0110081120.47b062fc@posting.google.com> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gkxwj82.pminews@News.CIS.DFN.DE> <9q2jvv07sv@enews3.newsguy.com>`

```
OK, I'll stick my neck out...(not for the first time...<G>).

I am seriously saying that within the lifetime of anybody in this group who
is currently under 35, not only C++ but also COBOL, JAVA, and Visual Basic
will fall into disuse!

In fact, these people will see the total demise of "computer programming" as
we understand it.

Intelligent operating systems will interact with users to establish what is
required, and they will build it from vast libraries of shared components
downloaded instantly from the net or preprogrammed in firmware.

The days of people telling computers what to do in stilted, formalised
"languages" are numbered. There are technologies in the pipeline which will
change the whole way that computers work and the way we interact with
them...

You'll remember these words when you see a computer achieve a result, and
you don't know how it did it.

Anyone who has ever written a heuristic program has had this experience
already, and it changes the way you think about things...

Pete.

"Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com> wrote in message
news:9q2jvv07sv@enews3.newsguy.com...
> > Don't worry Jon, we were codoing COBOL long before C++
> existed, and we'll
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-10-11T22:00:33+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BC60871.40789480@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<9p7d1k$ek2$03$1@news.t-online.com> <9p82vl020mg@enews1.newsguy.com> <92861a7b.0110040853.2ce8b099@posting.google.com> <38315c2d.0110050657.70c6035b@posting.google.com> <3bc1994b.362271@news.epix.net> <38315c2d.0110081120.47b062fc@posting.google.com> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gkxwj82.pminews@News.CIS.DFN.DE> <9q2jvv07sv@enews3.newsguy.com> <3bc5151e_5@news.newsgroups.com>`

```
Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote:
> 
> OK, I'll stick my neck out...(not for the first time...<G>).
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> we understand it.

I seem to recall exactly that being said some 30 years ago, though it
was Cobol, Fortran and Basic.

> Intelligent operating systems will interact with users to establish what is
> required, and they will build it from vast libraries of shared components
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> them...

I seem to recall exactly that being said some 30 years ago, though it
was AI, Linc and 'The Last One'.

The limitation of your projection (as it was about the 30 year old one)
is where you had it interacting with the _user_.  In theory the users
should know what they want.  In practice they haven't much of a clue
beyond the superficial.

The other problem is that all businesses are different from all the
other businesses and are different from what they used to be.  They
change and evolve to try to get a competitive advantage or to move into
a new market, possibly leaving the old.  They will think up ways of
operating for which the 'shared components' haven't been invented yet,
and they wouldn't want to share them because then the competitors could
benefit from them.

I seem to recall exactly that being said some 30 years ago, too.  It
wasn't you who said it was it ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT future tech. was Re: Good Cobol Tutorial

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3bc64561_7@news.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<9p7d1k$ek2$03$1@news.t-online.com> <9p82vl020mg@enews1.newsguy.com> <92861a7b.0110040853.2ce8b099@posting.google.com> <38315c2d.0110050657.70c6035b@posting.google.com> <3bc1994b.362271@news.epix.net> <38315c2d.0110081120.47b062fc@posting.google.com> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gkxwj82.pminews@News.CIS.DFN.DE> <9q2jvv07sv@enews3.newsguy.com> <3bc5151e_5@news.newsgroups.com> <3BC60871.40789480@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
"Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3BC60871.40789480@Azonic.co.nz...
> Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote:
> >
> > OK, I'll stick my neck out...(not for the first time...<G>).
> >
> > I am seriously saying that within the lifetime of anybody in this group
who
> > is currently under 35, not only C++ but also COBOL, JAVA, and Visual
Basic
> > will fall into disuse!
> >
> > In fact, these people will see the total demise of "computer
programming" as
> > we understand it.
>
> I seem to recall exactly that being said some 30 years ago, though it
> was Cobol, Fortran and Basic.
>
The difference is that now the technology (both software and hardware) has
advanced 30 years... Networking has completely changed things and new
emerging technologies are poised to do so even more.

> > Intelligent operating systems will interact with users to establish what
is
> > required, and they will build it from vast libraries of shared
components
> > downloaded instantly from the net or preprogrammed in firmware.
> >
> > The days of people telling computers what to do in stilted, formalised
> > "languages" are numbered. There are technologies in the pipeline which
will
> > change the whole way that computers work and the way we interact with
> > them...
>
> I seem to recall exactly that being said some 30 years ago, though it
> was AI, Linc and 'The Last One'.

Well, I remember 'The Last One' (closely followed by 'The Next One' <G>),
Linc was/is a particularly Kiwi oriented idea (and we all know how insane
THEY are...<G>), and AI should not be written off just yet, although I agree
it did not  deliver what was promised in the '80s (despite the Japanese
spending billions on it...). There is much more to AI than just neural
networks, heuristics, and List processing (although all of these have a
place); some AI theories cannot be implemented YET because the hardware
isn't there (but it is definitely on the way...).

It's a bit like Herwig's beloved Quantum Computer... theoretically
interesting but almost impossible to build with today's technology. We all
see the rate at which technology is accellerating, and it would therefore be
very unwise to suggest that the next 30 years will see nothing other than
the status quo maintained.

The point here, Richard, is, that because some old predictions failed to
eventuate, that doesn't mean that some current ones won't. (Of course, it
doesn't mean they WILL either; it just means that a prediction is exactly
that...a prediction. It may or may not eventuate. I take the position that
the predictions <I> make are based on extrapolation from existing or
"glimmering" technology. If I didn't believe there was at least a reasonable
chance of them eventuating, I wouldn't make them (it would be kind of
pointless...<G>).

You seem to believe that the only reality is the present one and you've
heard it all before so it is unlikely to transpire. A valid viewpoint and I
respect your right to it. (I just don't agree with it...<G>)

>
> The limitation of your projection (as it was about the 30 year old one)
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Yes, I agree. But you are talking about TODAY's users. And you have missed
the power of ITERATION. (Keep doing it until you get it right...<G>)

There is a generation of kids growing up with levels of technolgy we could
only dream about. I watched a two year old the other day using a mouse and
interacting with a software program AIMED AT 2 YEAR OLDS!

I believe the new User interface will be based on INTERACTION (probably in
natural language, with the assistance of icons where required for
quickness), and ITERATION. Iteration will work on the following basis:

1. Explain something to the computer.

2. Computer has a shot at it.

     Gets it wrong, but some of it is OK.

3. User evaluates, and indicates what is OK and what is not.

Process iterates from 2 until the desired result is achieved. Computer
remembers "how to do it" and that is that... in future user just requests
production.

This fundamental process will be "assisted" by components, smarter Operating
Systems (which will have "concept engines" that give them "knowledge" of
certain everyday concepts - like "customer", "product", "sale transaction"
etc.), transparent content addressable data stores, and a plethora of
emerging technology (plus some that I can't even begin to visualise...<G>)

Note that NO "Programming Language" is required in the above process. (The
only "code" necessary for this is machine code and this will be controlled
by the part of the OS which is responsible for "building" the solution. Even
this will only happen until a complete new generation of hardware emerges
from the labs, NOT based on the Von Neuman sequential processing model.
(Speculate here, around 25 years...could be 50))

> The other problem is that all businesses are different from all the
> other businesses and are different from what they used to be.  They
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> benefit from them.

We've argued this one before. I believe there is a core which is common to
ALL businesses in a certain sector. I agree with you that EVERY business
BELIEVES they are different and unique (I've made a good living out of this
belief...putting the same basic software (with a few bells and whistles)
into many different companies, each of whom believed they were the ONLY ones
with their specifc requirements and problems.)

Two things determine an individual's viewpoint on this:

1. How many companies they've worked in. (Contractors who move a lot will
see more of what I'm talking about than "Company Men" who stay many years in
one or a few companies.This is not an expressed or implied criticism of
either career choice.)

2. What level of abstraction they are looking at.

As for competitive advantage, this will be achieved in the WAY they do
business and not by the computer systems they use...given that everyone has
equally powerful computers available.

>
> I seem to recall exactly that being said some 30 years ago, too.  It
> wasn't you who said it was it ?

Hahaha! (Nice one...<G>!).

Sadly (for me, at least <G>), there is a slim chance I'll be around in 30
years to see which of us is right.

If it turns out that YOU are, Richard, and we are still doing exactly what
we did 40 years ago, giving sets of instructions in formal languages to Von
Neuman processors, then I'll count myself well out of it... It seems to me I
must've made the wrong career choice back in 1965 and my sojourn on this
planet would be pointless...<G>

If working with computers is a dead end that never progresses, then I'd
rather drive a truck.

Pete.
```

- **From:** Joseph J Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2001-10-12T22:53:11+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<josephk-C04133.22531112102001@echo-01.iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<9p7d1k$ek2$03$1@news.t-online.com> <9p82vl020mg@enews1.newsguy.com> <92861a7b.0110040853.2ce8b099@posting.google.com> <38315c2d.0110050657.70c6035b@posting.google.com> <3bc1994b.362271@news.epix.net> <38315c2d.0110081120.47b062fc@posting.google.com> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gkxwj82.pminews@News.CIS.DFN.DE> <9q2jvv07sv@enews3.newsguy.com> <3bc5151e_5@news.newsgroups.com> <3BC60871.40789480@Azonic.co.nz> <3bc64561_7@news.newsgroups.com>`

```
In article <3bc64561_7@news.newsgroups.com>,
 "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> 
> As for competitive advantage, this will be achieved in the WAY they do
> business and not by the computer systems they use...given that everyone has
> equally powerful computers available.
> 
But....
The WAY they do business impacts the business model in use and the 
business model in use defines what the software will have to do.
So different ways of doing business will ipso facto require different 
software. Different software will require someone to build it - Q.E.D.
Therefore business will always require someone to make these changes and 
software will never be able to be created the way you suggest. This is 
because (even for business process engineering) the domain of knowledge 
to achieve the outcome is too large for it to be encompassed by one 
person. There will always be a need for separate business process 
engineers and software engineers and managers and entepreneurs and all 
the various other skills required to make a successful business.

That is not to say that the software engineer of the future wouldn't 
have better tools.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT future tech. was Re: Good Cobol Tutorial

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-10-13T11:40:38+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3bc7752d_7@news.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<9p7d1k$ek2$03$1@news.t-online.com> <9p82vl020mg@enews1.newsguy.com> <92861a7b.0110040853.2ce8b099@posting.google.com> <38315c2d.0110050657.70c6035b@posting.google.com> <3bc1994b.362271@news.epix.net> <38315c2d.0110081120.47b062fc@posting.google.com> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gkxwj82.pminews@News.CIS.DFN.DE> <9q2jvv07sv@enews3.newsguy.com> <3bc5151e_5@news.newsgroups.com> <3BC60871.40789480@Azonic.co.nz> <3bc64561_7@news.newsgroups.com> <josephk-C04133.22531112102001@echo-01.iinet.net.au>`

```

"Joseph J Katnic" <josephk@iinet.net.au> wrote in message
news:josephk-C04133.22531112102001@echo-01.iinet.net.au...
> In article <3bc64561_7@news.newsgroups.com>,
>  "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> >
> > As for competitive advantage, this will be achieved in the WAY they do
> > business and not by the computer systems they use...given that everyone
has
> > equally powerful computers available.
> >
> But....
> The WAY they do business impacts the business model in use and the
> business model in use defines what the software will have to do.

Joe,

you are thinking like a computer person, not like someone in the real world.

If X has a business supplying grubblenuts and Y has the same kind of
business, given that both X and Y have the same computer systems in place to
support their efforts, then competitive advantage can only be obtained by
the WAY they do business.

If X packages his grubblenuts in attractive re-usable packaging, makes a
personal call on each of his customers at least once a month, understands
their problems and can address each customer by name, while Y simply
despatches grubblenuts in response to an order, who do you think has the
competitive advantage?

The business that will do the most transactions is the one that customers
feel most comfortable trading with.

This is what I am talking about when I said "the WAY" they do their
business.

EVERYTHING is NOT about software and computers. (Although most people here
see it in those terms...<G>)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT future tech. was Re: Good Cobol Tutorial

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-10-14T09:37:04+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BC94EB0.9E789BAE@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<9p7d1k$ek2$03$1@news.t-online.com> <9p82vl020mg@enews1.newsguy.com> <92861a7b.0110040853.2ce8b099@posting.google.com> <38315c2d.0110050657.70c6035b@posting.google.com> <3bc1994b.362271@news.epix.net> <38315c2d.0110081120.47b062fc@posting.google.com> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gkxwj82.pminews@News.CIS.DFN.DE> <9q2jvv07sv@enews3.newsguy.com> <3bc5151e_5@news.newsgroups.com> <3BC60871.40789480@Azonic.co.nz> <3bc64561_7@news.newsgroups.com> <josephk-C04133.22531112102001@echo-01.iinet.net.au> <3bc7752d_7@news.newsgroups.com>`

```
Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote:
> > >
> > But....
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> you are thinking like a computer person, not like someone in the real world.

This discussion started with focussing on computer systems, why not
continue this ?  There are many aspects to businesses: personnel
management, organizational structure, marketing, factory management; all
of which will contribute to competitiveness and survival.  This
discussion was focussed on the computer aspects of the business model,
you had originally asserted that:

> > Intelligent operating systems will interact with users to establish what is
> > required, and they will build it from vast libraries of shared components
> > downloaded instantly from the net or preprogrammed in firmware.

You now try to dismiss arguments against this as 'not being in the real
world', ironic really.

> If X has a business supplying grubblenuts and Y has the same kind of
> business, given that both X and Y have the same computer systems in place to
> support their efforts, then competitive advantage can only be obtained by
> the WAY they do business.

> If X packages his grubblenuts in attractive re-usable packaging,

'Grubblenuts in re-usable packaging' is a _product_, not a 'way of doing
business'.  Some don't seem to be able to tell the difference   ;-)

> makes a
> personal call on each of his customers at least once a month, understands
> their problems and can address each customer by name, while Y simply
> despatches grubblenuts in response to an order, who do you think has the
> competitive advantage?

This also has a large effect on cost and thus either pricing or
profitability.  In fact one could say they are in different businesses
(service and commodity sales).  The problem comes when the customer uses
the first for advice but actually buys from the 2nd because of cheaper
pricing, then the first goes out of business.
 
> The business that will do the most transactions is the one that customers
> feel most comfortable trading with.

Effect leads to cause ???

> This is what I am talking about when I said "the WAY" they do their
> business.

Many aspects of the 'WAY' businesses operate rely on computer system
implementing these as part of the business model.  Just because you can
think of ways that don't rely on computers does not mean they can't be
computerised in a way that reduces costs and improves competitiveness,
even if there isn't a pre-built component.
 
> EVERYTHING is NOT about software and computers. (Although most people here
> see it in those terms...<G>)

No, but this group is about software and computer.  If you want to
discuss such business practices as 're-usable packaging' there may be a
group for that.
```

```
"Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3BC94EB0.9E789BAE@Azonic.co.nz...
> Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote:
> > > >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

world.
>
> This discussion started with focussing on computer systems, why not
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> you had originally asserted that:

On the contrary, at the time I joined this discussion it was examining
whether or not C++ would cease to exist within the lifetime of the people
here. This is hardly "focussed on the computer aspects of the business
model"...anyway, I changed the topic to something more appropriate and you
should also know by now that NOTHING stays on topic in CLC for very long if
an interesting idea presents itself...;-)


>
> > > Intelligent operating systems will interact with users to establish
what is
> > > required, and they will build it from vast libraries of shared
components
> > > downloaded instantly from the net or preprogrammed in firmware.
>
> You now try to dismiss arguments against this as 'not being in the real
> world', ironic really.

My observation was intended to point out that competetitive advantage can be
obtained (in the "real world") by things other than computer software.
That's all.

>
> > If X has a business supplying grubblenuts and Y has the same kind of
> > business, given that both X and Y have the same computer systems in
place to
> > support their efforts, then competitive advantage can only be obtained
by
> > the WAY they do business.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
And it doesn't make a jot of difference to the argument. The packaging is
just an EXAMPLE of something OTHER than software, which may help someone to
obtain a competetive advantage. (It's not a GOOD example, but for this
forum, it will serve...)

> > makes a
> > personal call on each of his customers at least once a month,
understands
> > their problems and can address each customer by name, while Y simply
> > despatches grubblenuts in response to an order, who do you think has the
> > competitive advantage?
>

> This also has a large effect on cost and thus either pricing or
> profitability.  In fact one could say they are in different businesses
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
It was taken as read for the sake of the argument that they are equal on all
other aspects, other than the ones specified. It is therefore not possible
for one to undercut the other (at least as far as this particular discussion
is concerned...) This very unrealistic situation is intended to reinforce
the fact that competetive advantage can be obtained by methods OTHER than
computer systems and software.

> > The business that will do the most transactions is the one that
customers
> > feel most comfortable trading with.
>
> Effect leads to cause ???

No. The one they feel most comfortable with caused them to feel that way by
implementing the feeble examples I mentioned...why do I have to explain
this? Where is the confusion between cause and effect?
> > This is what I am talking about when I said "the WAY" they do their
> > business.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Quite so, where we differ is in the way the new functionality will be
developed in the future. I believe existing functionality will be extended
by the computers themselves, through interaction with expert users ("expert"
in the BUSINESS sense, NOT the COMPUTER sense...)

This is a completely different process from "computer programming", giving a
set of sequential instructions in a formal language which cover every
eventuality, as we do today.

> > EVERYTHING is NOT about software and computers. (Although most people
here
> > see it in those terms...<G>)
>
> No, but this group is about software and computer.

Yes, and this thread is about "future tech"... where's the problem?

>If you want to
> discuss such business practices as 're-usable packaging' there may be a
> group for that.

Ouch! I think that was meant to wound...it didn't <G>.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT future tech. was Re: Good Cobol Tutorial

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-10-18T16:03:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9qmucf$qkl$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<9p7d1k$ek2$03$1@news.t-online.com> <9p82vl020mg@enews1.newsguy.com> <92861a7b.0110040853.2ce8b099@posting.google.com> <38315c2d.0110050657.70c6035b@posting.google.com> <3bc1994b.362271@news.epix.net> <38315c2d.0110081120.47b062fc@posting.google.com> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gkxwj82.pminews@News.CIS.DFN.DE> <9q2jvv07sv@enews3.newsguy.com> <3bc5151e_5@news.newsgroups.com> <3BC60871.40789480@Azonic.co.nz> <3bc64561_7@news.newsgroups.com> <josephk-C04133.22531112102001@echo-01.iinet.net.au> <3bc7752d_7@news.newsgroups.com> <3BC94EB0.9E789BAE@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
If we predict the future based upon technology alone, we will fail.  
Sometimes the best tool gets used, other times it doesn't get used.

Sometimes someone will pay a fortune for a golf club or a rifle when
moderately priced one will be virtually as good.  Other times people use a
Swiss Army knife because it is handy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT future tech. was Re: Good Cobol Tutorial

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-10-13T09:41:28+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BC7FE38.F02FA3A@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<9p7d1k$ek2$03$1@news.t-online.com> <9p82vl020mg@enews1.newsguy.com> <92861a7b.0110040853.2ce8b099@posting.google.com> <38315c2d.0110050657.70c6035b@posting.google.com> <3bc1994b.362271@news.epix.net> <38315c2d.0110081120.47b062fc@posting.google.com> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gkxwj82.pminews@News.CIS.DFN.DE> <9q2jvv07sv@enews3.newsguy.com> <3bc5151e_5@news.newsgroups.com> <3BC60871.40789480@Azonic.co.nz> <3bc64561_7@news.newsgroups.com> <josephk-C04133.22531112102001@echo-01.iinet.net.au>`

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
> As for competitive advantage, this will be achieved in the WAY they do
> business and not by the computer systems they use...given that everyone has
> equally powerful computers available.

And for most businesses the _way_ they do business is embodied in their
computer systems.  For example pricing, discounting, promotions, the
most fundemental aspects of the business _could_ be done manually, but
only at huge cost in terms of man-hours and errors.

Loyalty card systems, as currently implemented, could never be done
without computers and computer communication.  These 'way of business'
were certainly not available as 'pre-written modules' when the loyalty
cards were adopted, each company implemented their own way of dealing
with these, different ways because they wanted to organise differently. 
(I was asked to investigate the use of one Loyalty card system from one
company for another.  In the original company that system worked fine as
it was a distributed system in a distributed company.  The company
adopting the software was centralised and I pointed out that it would be
useless for their needs.  They bought it anyway and found out I was
right, not that being right gained me anything.)

The next new thing that a company wants to do will also not be available
as modules and will also require computers.  Apart from that I don't
know what it will be.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT future tech. was Re: Good Cobol Tutorial

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-10-13T12:36:14+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3bc77fcc_4@news.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<9p7d1k$ek2$03$1@news.t-online.com> <9p82vl020mg@enews1.newsguy.com> <92861a7b.0110040853.2ce8b099@posting.google.com> <38315c2d.0110050657.70c6035b@posting.google.com> <3bc1994b.362271@news.epix.net> <38315c2d.0110081120.47b062fc@posting.google.com> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gkxwj82.pminews@News.CIS.DFN.DE> <9q2jvv07sv@enews3.newsguy.com> <3bc5151e_5@news.newsgroups.com> <3BC60871.40789480@Azonic.co.nz> <3bc64561_7@news.newsgroups.com> <josephk-C04133.22531112102001@echo-01.iinet.net.au> <3BC7FE38.F02FA3A@Azonic.co.nz>`

```

"Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3BC7FE38.F02FA3A@Azonic.co.nz...
> "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> >
> > As for competitive advantage, this will be achieved in the WAY they do
> > business and not by the computer systems they use...given that everyone
has
> > equally powerful computers available.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I have never suggested that everyone should go back to manual systems,
Richard. Neither have I suggested that all systems of the future will be
built entirely from pre-programmed modules, although the core processing
certainly will be, where this is feasible.


> Loyalty card systems, as currently implemented, could never be done
> without computers and computer communication.  These 'way of business'
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
OK. So you don't think it would be possible to "explain" the new loyalty
card system to a "smart" system, by interacting with it?

Here's a transcript:
(Given that the system already "understands" the following concepts:
"Customer", "Product", "Sale", "Account", "card" (tied to "Credit Card" and
"Method of Payment", which are part of the "Sale" concept)

user: "We want to build a Loyalty Card system."
computer: "What's a Loyalty Card?"
user: "A card which rewards our customers every time they buy something from
us."
computer: "So, when a Sale transaction occurs, I should do something with
this Loyalty Card?"
user:"Yes."
computer:"What should I do?"
user:"Check each item bought against the Product Database and get the number
of Loyalty points. Multiply this by the number of units bought and post the
result to the Customer's Loyalty Account."
computer: "I don't have a Loyalty Account. Shall I create one?"
user: "Yes."
computer: "What should I link it to?"
user: "Customer."
computer: "Will every customer have a Loyalty Account?"
user:"Yes. But it need not be active. It will be activated by the Loyalty
Sign-up Process."
computer:"I have no such process."
user: "We'll talk about that later."
computer:"OK."
... and so on...

From this, the system extends the Customer Object to include the new Loyalty
Account, and defines a flag property to indicate whether or not the account
is active.

It creates the necessary Loyalty Card Class with a Method to update the
Loyalty Account for that customer and ensures that it is triggered by the
Sales Transaction

The process of interaction ITERATES until the correct results are obtained.
The system creates test data with help from the user, and simulates sales
transactions and actual circumstances (sales, partial credits, refunds,
etc.)

The question of whether the system needs to be distributed or centralised is
irrelevant. The system KNOWS what it has and builds accordingly. It creates
an Object Model which can be implemented in ANY technical environment, using
whatever platforms (present and future) are available.

We don't care how Database engines retrieve data, (although we may ask them
to EXPLAIN themselves on occasion), why should we care HOW a transaction is
processed as long as it does what we want?

Obviously, the root of all this is Object Oriented code.

Objects can be EXTENDED to accommodate new properties and methods which were
not conceived of at the time we created the object originally. (Java
actually uses the word "EXTENDS" to indicate new sub-classes (and their
concomitant methods and properties) being added to a Class). The concepts
above are very close to this.

Although you have interpreted my statement that "the system would use
Component technology" to mean 'pre-written modules', I have never intended
it to mean that. Components are OO based. That means they can be extended.
Only when we actually package the objects for re-use is a component created.
This technology has a fair way to go yet and it is very likely that we shall
see dynamic components which can be extended and re-packaged "on the fly" in
the not-too-distant future.

Science Fiction? Maybe today. But I contend there is NOTHING in the above
that could not be done given the right hardware and software.

Hope I live to see it...

> The next new thing that a company wants to do will also not be available
> as modules and will also require computers.  Apart from that I don't
> know what it will be.

Agreed. I hope the above clarifies where I am coming from.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Good Cobol Tutorial

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-10-14T16:41:27+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BC9B227.87118FC1@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<9p7d1k$ek2$03$1@news.t-online.com> <9p82vl020mg@enews1.newsguy.com> <92861a7b.0110040853.2ce8b099@posting.google.com> <38315c2d.0110050657.70c6035b@posting.google.com> <3bc1994b.362271@news.epix.net> <38315c2d.0110081120.47b062fc@posting.google.com> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gkxwj82.pminews@News.CIS.DFN.DE> <9q2jvv07sv@enews3.newsguy.com> <3bc5151e_5@news.newsgroups.com>`

```
Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote:
> I have never suggested that everyone should go back to manual systems,

If the computer can't do it then it would have to be done manually.  For
example if a pricing scheme is required and the computer cannot follow
the rules, or take into account a particular parameter then the price
has to be calculated manually, even if this is being keyed into the
computer system.

> Richard. Neither have I suggested that all systems of the future will be
> built entirely from pre-programmed modules, 

Oh yes you did:

> > I am seriously saying that within the lifetime of anybody in this group who
> > is currently under 35, not only C++ but also COBOL, JAVA, and Visual Basic
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> > them...

> although the core processing certainly will be, where this is feasible.

This sounds like a considerable backdown from your previous position. 
From 'the total demise of computer programming' to 'maybe in some areas,
where feasible'.

> OK. So you don't think it would be possible to "explain" the new loyalty
> card system to a "smart" system, by interacting with it?

It would only be possible _IF_ the 'smart' system already 'understood'
the basics of what a loyalty system was _AND_ understood the particular
aspects of the required implementation.  This requires that the 'smart'
system builder can predict which completely unknown features will be
novel inventions in the future.

> user: "We want to build a Loyalty Card system."
> computer: "What's a Loyalty Card?"
> user: "A card which rewards our customers every time they buy something from
> us."

WRONG.  The 'Fly Buys' card, for example doesn't reward "our customers",
it has a third party (loyalty card company) reward 'our customer's 
customers' via a transaction that goes via the EFTPOS system
communication.

> From this, the system extends the Customer Object to include the new Loyalty
> Account, and defines a flag property to indicate whether or not the account
> is active.

Wrong. The retailer, who has the customers, plays almost no part in the
system except recording the card transaction, they certainly do not even
know who their customers are, let alone hold accounts for them (have you
never been into a shop?).  The wholesaler has no interaction with their
customer (the retailer) over this but they pay the Loyalty card company.

Now a 'user' may very well misunderstand this enough to think that what
the 'smart' system created might do the job, just as you have done, but
they would be very wrong.

In order to support the view that 'smart' systems could create these
systems from pre-built modules then every innovation would have to
pre-invented by the system writers before the businesses 'invented'
them.

> We don't care how Database engines retrieve data,

Oh, yes I do.  And everyone who has to wait half an hour for what they
think is a 'simple query' cares too.

> (although we may ask them
> to EXPLAIN themselves on occasion), why should we care HOW a transaction is
> processed as long as it does what we want?

Because there are only so many hours in a day.  Caring about _how_ it is
processed may make the difference between seeing your family or not.

> Obviously, the root of all this is Object Oriented code.
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> above are very close to this.

Of course the basis of extending objects is the writing of source code,
in say, Java or Cobol.  This clashes with you assertion that these will
fall into disuse.

This also seems to be an entire backdown from your original position.

But the problem with dynamically extending and overloading classes is
that there is a performance issue with each new layer, as well as new
levels of obscurity.  The problem is that eventually no-one knows what
is _actually_ happening.  It is not auditable, it can only be determined
by exhaustive testing of every possible combination.

Cobol will remain in business use because it is deterministic by
reference only to the source code and the language reference manual. 
With 'smart' systems dynamically extending classes there is no guarantee
that somewhere in that lot there isn't some module EMailing your main
competitor because they paid the component writer for special services
(cf Microsoft, eg the argument between them and Kodak). 

> Although you have interpreted my statement that "the system would use
> Component technology" to mean 'pre-written modules',

No, actually I interpreted your use of "preprogrammed" to mean
'pre-written modules'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Good Cobol Tutorial

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-10-15T06:41:44+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3bc9cfcf_5@news.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<9p7d1k$ek2$03$1@news.t-online.com> <9p82vl020mg@enews1.newsguy.com> <92861a7b.0110040853.2ce8b099@posting.google.com> <38315c2d.0110050657.70c6035b@posting.google.com> <3bc1994b.362271@news.epix.net> <38315c2d.0110081120.47b062fc@posting.google.com> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gkxwj82.pminews@News.CIS.DFN.DE> <9q2jvv07sv@enews3.newsguy.com> <3bc5151e_5@news.newsgroups.com> <3BC9B227.87118FC1@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
"Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3BC9B227.87118FC1@Azonic.co.nz...
> Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote:
> > I have never suggested that everyone should go back to manual systems,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> computer system.

There is no question of the computer "not being able to do it". We are at
issue as to HOW it will do it.

You seem to have the missed the whole point of interaction and iteration.
There is NO way that the computer "cannot follow the rules" because these
rules will have been explained to it at length until it CAN follow them. In
fact, even if the "rules" have gradually evolved, or been subject to extreme
change and volatility, it will STILL follow them.
>
> > Richard. Neither have I suggested that all systems of the future will be
> > built entirely from pre-programmed modules,
>
> Oh yes you did:

Oh NO I didn't! (look out behind you...<G>).

I said SOME concepts will be pre-programmed in firmware (or whatever the
equivalent is by then) and I said components will be used as the basis for
the systems (by then there will be MILLIONS of available components and
that's why the computers themselves will track what is available and
download it). These components will then be extended by the "smart"
Operating systems to meet new requirements. I believe this will be done
interactively, and when the final solution is built, the objects will be
re-packaged as components and available (with enhanced functionality) to
other systems.

>
> > > I am seriously saying that within the lifetime of anybody in this
group who
> > > is currently under 35, not only C++ but also COBOL, JAVA, and Visual
Basic
> > > will fall into disuse!
> > >
> > > In fact, these people will see the total demise of "computer
programming" as
> > > we understand it.
> > >
> > > Intelligent operating systems will interact with users to establish
what is
> > > required, and they will build it from vast libraries of shared
components
> > > downloaded instantly from the net or preprogrammed in firmware.
> > >
> > > The days of people telling computers what to do in stilted, formalised
> > > "languages" are numbered. There are technologies in the pipeline which
will
> > > change the whole way that computers work and the way we interact with
> > > them...
>
> > although the core processing certainly will be, where this is feasible.
>
I still stand by all of the above.

> This sounds like a considerable backdown from your previous position.
> From 'the total demise of computer programming' to 'maybe in some areas,
> where feasible'.
>
Nope. No backdown (although I would do so if I believed I was wrong or no
longer believed what I have previously suggested...It would be very stupid
to maintain a position just for the sake of pride and I am not stupid...).

The computers (hardware and software) will write/maintain the code (as long
as it is still necessary to HAVE code as we understand it) based on
interaction with an expert human user. ("expert" in the BUSINESS sense NOT
the COMPUTER sense.)

This is obviously nothing like the process we use today, hence I stand by
"total demise of computer programming" as I always have.

If a completely new, unforeseeable, type of business was invented for which
there were NO exising base process components, then, obviously, base
components could not be used. Hence "where feasible". (Sad I needed to spell
that out, really...)

In this case the fundamental concept engines inherent in the system (the
bits that understand "customer", "product" etc.) could be used to build the
new concepts, objects and, eventually, processes, for the new business.
There would still be no "computer programming" as we understand it (you can
argue that the process of "educating" the system is a form of programming,
but it would be as far removed from writing a COBOL program as a Zippo is
from rubbing two sticks together...)

> > OK. So you don't think it would be possible to "explain" the new loyalty
> > card system to a "smart" system, by interacting with it?
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
The system will have it explained to it. It doesn't have to understand
Loyalty schemes; it has to understand the objects which make such schemes
possible.

> > user: "We want to build a Loyalty Card system."
> > computer: "What's a Loyalty Card?"
> > user: "A card which rewards our customers every time they buy something
from
> > us."
>

> WRONG.  The 'Fly Buys' card, for example doesn't reward "our customers",
> it has a third party (loyalty card company) reward 'our customer's
> customers' via a transaction that goes via the EFTPOS system
> communication.
>
It is not wrong, it is an EXAMPLE. You don't seriously think in the few
words available here I am going to cover EVERY possible eventuality
including Fly-buys and EFTPOS? Get Real! I am trying simply to demonstrate
how a system can extend concepts it already has, thru interaction and
iteration. Gimme a break...!

This issue is NOT going to be sidetracked into a discussion about the
details of Loyalty Cards. The example I have chosen is a very simple
(imaginary) one which will be understandable to the majority of this user
base (who have probably had little or no exposure to Fly-Buys and EFTPOS, as
these systems are indigenous to Australasia.)

> > From this, the system extends the Customer Object to include the new
Loyalty
> > Account, and defines a flag property to indicate whether or not the
account
> > is active.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
It isn't wrong, because that's how I'm choosing to define it. I really don't
care HOW YOUR Loyalty Card system works and it is irrelevant to this
discussion. What is important is the way in which new functionality, based
on existing objects, is introduced to and recognised by, the system.

> Now a 'user' may very well misunderstand this enough to think that what
> the 'smart' system created might do the job, just as you have done, but
> they would be very wrong.

Again you are missing the underlying concept of the example. I accept that
what you are saying is correct as far as Loyalty systems go, but this is a
VERY simple example based on just the fundamental processes for ONE type of
Loyalty Card (maybe an in-store one). Is that so wrong? Can you not see how
it COULD work rather than decide it is "wrong" because it doesn't meet the
details of YOUR particular Loyalty Card system?

As for the User believing incorrectly the final result... The final result
either meets the requirements or it doesn't. If it doesn't then the process
ITERATES until it does. It cannot be right and wrong at the same time
because the test results would be checked by other people and departments.
(Very similar to the process of UAT which systems undergo now...)
>
> In order to support the view that 'smart' systems could create these
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I never advocated pre-built modules as a TOTAL solution. As I work with
components all the time, I am very familiar with the use of such objects. I
know it would not be feasible to build a complete dynamically responsive
system ENTIRELY from them (except under very exceptional circumstances).
However, the base for a business process could certainly be done in this
way. The idea is analogous to (but light years advanced from) the
"tailoring" of a package.


> > We don't care how Database engines retrieve data,
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > to EXPLAIN themselves on occasion), why should we care HOW a transaction
is
> > processed as long as it does what we want?
>
> Because there are only so many hours in a day.  Caring about _how_ it is
> processed may make the difference between seeing your family or not.
>

Not on the processors of tomorrow.


> > Obviously, the root of all this is Object Oriented code.
> >
> > Objects can be EXTENDED to accommodate new properties and methods which
were
> > not conceived of at the time we created the object originally. (Java
> > actually uses the word "EXTENDS" to indicate new sub-classes (and their
> > concomitant methods and properties) being added to a Class). The
concepts
> > above are very close to this.
>

> Of course the basis of extending objects is the writing of source code,
> in say, Java or Cobol.  This clashes with you assertion that these will
> fall into disuse.
>

That is indeed today's technology. I believe that the OBJECT code will
replace the source language (it is already irrelevant what source language
you use to write components in...hence, it is quite feasible for us to use
whatever language is best suited to the purpose).

Best suited for whom? For a Human. Because the Language has been developed
to address a specific area of computing, and it is intended for a Human to
learn it and write it, it will have specific constructs and grammar intended
for Human consumption. The system has no such requirements.

In the future, the system will take on the chore of modifying the base code
and it can do so in the language it understands: Object code.

It will never be maintained by a Human, anyway. Even if the system was to do
it in COBOL (for example), no source would be maintained. When the component
is re-packaged at Build time, it is ONLY the OBJECT code which is saved.
(However, the system is likely to attach its own "documentation" to each
component it modifies or generates. In fact some component formats (like
COM) require this already. The system can therefore always produce the
Methods and Properties available with a given component, even if only the
object code is available.)

This is the single hardest concept for most people here to grasp. We have
had 40 years of maintaining Source code. We have insisted on ONE language to
make it easier to mantain. Now the requirements are different and we NEED
multiple Languages to achieve things. Source is no longer King. If you write
a function in both C and COBOL, the resulting Object code will be similar...
(if it runs under .NET it will be almost identical). Only a very skilled
programmer could tell which came from the C source and which from the COBOL
(given that vendor flags were removed), yet the system runs either without
prejudice. Object code in a common wrapper is one definition of a component.

> This also seems to be an entire backdown from your original position.
>
Even if it was, (which it isn't), why is that so important to you? I suggest
that you NEVER understood my position. I also take some responsibility for
not presenting it in the detail which was apparently required. Because these
concepts are everyday to me, I may have assumed that everyone else would
similarly understand what I was saying.

> But the problem with dynamically extending and overloading classes is
> that there is a performance issue with each new layer, as well as new
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
No. It can be audited by another computer designed specially for this
purpose. We do this already with Heuristic programs. If a program runs for
say, 5 seconds and modifies itself 10 million times in that period, it is
not feasible for a Human to analyse the log of exactly which heuristics were
modified and how....it could take generations of lifetimes (the sort of
thing the Japanese would be capable of; they are very good when it comes to
the long view).

Instead we utilise another system to analyse these logs, searching for
specific patterns of interest to us. At the end of the day, we still don't
know exactly how it achieved its result necessarily, but we have some
insight. Whether or not we should care about this is a completely different
issue...

As for performance issues, there are two things which will address that:

1. More powerful platforms. This is a "given" with the passage of time.

2. Specialised agent software which will exist ONLY to improve performance.
This may extend to "performance analysis" computers similar to the
diagnostic computers used to check out performance of modern engines when
your car needs tuning...  The point is, that in the same way that "coding"
won't be done by Humans, neither will debugging or performance improvement.

"Centre of Competence" type hardware and software will download itself to
networks affected, analyse what is going on, detect and clear bottlenecks,
and reconfigure both software and hardware to ensure optimised performance,
all completely transparent to Humans, who would simply observe response
times getting faster... Only as an absolute last resort would these
facilities communicate with Humans...Like, "Buy some more disk drives" or
"Replace backbone cabling with fibre optic...." <G>

> Cobol will remain in business use because it is deterministic by
> reference only to the source code and the language reference manual.

As noted above, the relevance of Source Code is becoming more and more
seriously in question. As long as it remains relevant, your statement is
true.

This is one reason why I am predicting a 15-20 year life for COBOL. Within 6
or 7 years Source code will be irrelevant; but we still have the Legacy
COBOL albatross round our necks and that will take some time to deal with.
There are emerging tools which make the task of extracting Business Rules
from this code feasible. These tools will get better.

> With 'smart' systems dynamically extending classes there is no guarantee
> that somewhere in that lot there isn't some module EMailing your main
> competitor because they paid the component writer for special services
> (cf Microsoft, eg the argument between them and Kodak).
>
Haha! Good point. I hope you're not serious. Intelligent agents will be
monitoring security and capable of reproducing everything that is sent
across any given port.

> > Although you have interpreted my statement that "the system would use
> > Component technology" to mean 'pre-written modules',
>
> No, actually I interpreted your use of "preprogrammed" to mean
> 'pre-written modules'.
Well that was in reference to firmware, not software.

OK, I imagine that the majority of people here are pretty bored with this by
now so I won't be posting any more on the subject.

I will happily answer questions or explain further background by private
mail.

I have honestly attempted to explain my position to the best of my ability.

To see it, requires vision and imagination. Without these qualities, my
effort is as pointless as trying to explain the colour "red" to a blind
man...

Pete.
```

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in
message news:3bc5151e_5@news.newsgroups.com...
> OK, I'll stick my neck out...(not for the first time...<G>).

It's appreciated...

> I am seriously saying that within the lifetime of anybody in
this group who
> is currently under 35, not only C++ but also COBOL, JAVA, and
Visual Basic
> will fall into disuse!
>
> In fact, these people will see the total demise of "computer
programming" as
> we understand it.
>
> Intelligent operating systems will interact with users to
establish what is
> required, and they will build it from vast libraries of shared
components
> downloaded instantly from the net or preprogrammed in
firmware.
>
> The days of people telling computers what to do in stilted,
formalised
> "languages" are numbered. There are technologies in the
pipeline which will
> change the whole way that computers work and the way we
interact with
> them...
>
> You'll remember these words when you see a computer achieve a
result, and
> you don't know how it did it.
>
> Anyone who has ever written a heuristic program has had this
experience
> already, and it changes the way you think about things...

I'm not very convinced by this argument.  Script, code, whatever
it's called, is extraordinarily flexible and articulate.
Something of that nature will have to be available to glue the
components together.  We're alreadying seeing the transformation
you suggest--many entrant VB programmers are confounded by
having to use some else's code (components) instead of getting
to write real foundational code.  However capable the components
are, it still takes some custom tailoring to get them to play
together.

You can look at this in a limited arena--web page creation.
Skads of WYSIWYG editors exist, but a web designer that does not
know how to cut custom pieces out of straight HTML will quickly
reach their limit.

I'll happily concede that I'll probably do less trench-level
programming in the future, but suspect that there will always be
a need for it.

______

On additional analogy--a similar argument has been made about
the keyboard in favor of pens and voice actuation.  Although
these technologies have promise, the density of information that
can be provided from slapping the keys has retained it.
```

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-10-16T07:09:35+01:00
