- **Message-ID:** `<3BCBCF1F.8BD1ED5C@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<9p7d1k$ek2$03$1@news.t-online.com> <9p82vl020mg@enews1.newsguy.com> <92861a7b.0110040853.2ce8b099@posting.google.com> <38315c2d.0110050657.70c6035b@posting.google.com> <3bc1994b.362271@news.epix.net> <38315c2d.0110081120.47b062fc@posting.google.com> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gkxwj82.pminews@News.CIS.DFN.DE> <9q2jvv07sv@enews3.newsguy.com> <3bc5151e_5@news.newsgroups.com> <9qdc8g01b0j@enews3.newsguy.com> <3bca5f74_8@news.newsgroups.com>`

```
Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote:

> I see no point in arguing further as it is like trying to explain "red" to a
> blind man.

It is not that I don't see what you are saying, it is simply that I
completely disagree with with it.  You are trying to explain "red" as
the colour of the sky (future of programming).

Over the last 30 years predictions just like your have been made many
times, and in fact many systems have been implemented such as LINC or
'The Last One' which purport to do exactly as you say (except the
voice). These failed eventually because the users always wanted
something just beyond what the developers had imagined.  LINC, the most
successful, required just as many Cobol programmers creating skeletons
for the generator as it would take to write the whole system, and it
finally closed when new systems required structures that would take a
complete redesign of LINC to cope with.

Certainly if you want a nice simple mainframe batch/CICS accounting
system LINC can knock one off for you, but no-one wants those anymore.

Sometimes the sky is "red", sometimes AI systems and/or voice and/or
user generated systems do look like the future, but then it all fades
away in the night and users dream of new things they want.
```

Pete.

## Thread: Compile Proc? — 5 msgs

### Compile Proc?

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praul@isot.NOSPAM.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-01T20:07:23-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tri4u47nk1ki7a@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Oh Phooey... 
I have been doing a lot of work in Assembler the past few months, without access to all my little skeleton files on the mainframe. Not being at all what one might call JCL tolerant, I find myself agog at why 
the doggone thing is fighting with me. (*sigh*) Would someone with a less tired pair of eyeballs who
needs a good laught tell me what the heck I am or am not doing right here please? 

 This is on a pretty generic 2.7 system on a P/390. It is slower than the same software running on a 
PC under Hercules right now, so I'm really starting to get frustrated. I do know it is something with overriding the SYSLMOD DD in the LKED step, and I have a vauge memory about needing some kind of JOBLIB card? 
Anyway, it is step 23 that has a resolution problem.  This is jus using the standard IGYWCL proc, though I 
did have to add a JCLLIB card to find it. 


Thanks
-Paul

#### ↳ Re: Compile Proc?

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2001-10-02T03:28:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20011001232811.01505.00001103@mb-bh.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<tri4u47nk1ki7a@corp.supernews.com>`

```
First, please stop sending MIME and HTML to a usenet. It is very hard to read
with all the extra punctuation. 



 This is on a pretty generic 2.7 system on a P/390. It is slower than =
the same software running on a=20
PC under Hercules right now, so I'm really starting to get frustrated. I =
do know it is something with overriding the SYSLMOD DD in the LKED step, =
and I have a vauge memory about needing some kind of JOBLIB card?=20
Anyway, it is step 23 that has a resolution problem.  This is jus using =
the standard IGYWCL proc, though I=20
did have to add a JCLLIB card to find it.=20

Not your SYSLMOD, your SYSLIB on the Link step is data set not found; you need
to find out the dsnames of all the relevant files; for example, you use
IGY.V1R2M0.SIGYCOMP for the library holding the compiler and
CEE.V1R5M0.SCEELKED for the LE link time library; if your shop has installed a
later release than V1R5 (and that is pretty old), then the dataset name has
probably changed - a real reason not to have the version and release number in
your data set names!

Hope this helps


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a6rirt87lc3o7jilhrald933i108mcnft5@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<tri4u47nk1ki7a@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Mon, 1 Oct 2001 20:07:23 -0500, "Paul Raulerson"
<praul@isot.NOSPAM.com> wrote:

>        17 XXLKED   EXEC PGM=HEWL,COND=(8,LT,COBOL),REGION=1024K                   00430000
>        18 //LKED.SYSLIB   DD DSN=IGY.V1R2M0.SIGYPROC,DISP=SHR                     00250006
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> IEF272I PRAULCC2 LKED COBS1 - STEP WAS NOT EXECUTED.

SYSLIB+001 Dataset not found

SYSLIB+1 DD DISP=SHR,DSN=SYS1.SCREERUN

Shouldn't that be SCEERUN instead of SCREERUN?

## Thread: About Legacy Coding — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL's future, legacy, and obsolescence`](topics.md#future)_

### About Legacy Coding

- **From:** "Editor" <Editor@aboutlegacycoding.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-02T15:10:33-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fj.comp.lang.cobol,fj.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9pd3fd$1q3o$1@allnight.news.cais.net>`

```
We are a couple of days late publishing this issue of About Legacy Coding.
It's been a heck of a month.  Development Methodologies are the focus of
this issue  along with an informative article on the new COBOL Standard.
This issue of About Legacy Coding is online now at
www.aboutlegacycoding.com.

In This Issue

- Pete Dashwood (who is fast becoming a regular contributor!) provides an
excellent analysis of two development methodologies in Shoot the Rapids,
Avoid the Waterfall .
- Compass brings us a great white paper on Timeboxing Applicaton Development
.
- Norm Praed of The Progeni Corporation shares his views on A Strategic
Evolution Architecture for Legacy Systems in Chapter 1 of a multi-part
series.
- Stephen Spiro gives us his thoughts on What is Wrong with the New COBOL
Standard in this detailed discussion of his concerns regarding this new
standard.

To read this issue, click on the URL below or type the URL into your browser
address line.
http://www.aboutlegacycoding.com/

I hope you enjoy this issue.
```

## Thread: We Need Mathematical proofs for the Language Constructs — 3 msgs

### We Need Mathematical proofs for the Language Constructs

- **From:** ammarkazim@hotmail.com (Ammar Kazim)
- **Date:** 2001-10-03T07:27:06-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<234f426d.0110030627.48709135@posting.google.com>`

```
Dear Users or Developers or Mathematicians we need the mathematical
proofs for the Language constructs becouse we are going to make the
tool on Reverse Engineering for the COBOL LEGACY SYSTEMS so please
anyone from you can help us in this.
Waiting for your response.
Ammar Kazim
Pakistan
ammarkazim@hotmail.com
```

#### ↳ Re: We Need Mathematical proofs for the Language Constructs

- **From:** Herwig Huener <Herwig.Huener@fujitsu-siemens.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-04T15:55:06+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BBC6A3A.B0DA6191@fujitsu-siemens.com>`
- **References:** `<234f426d.0110030627.48709135@posting.google.com>`

```
2001-10-04 15:55:55 MESZ

Ammar Kazim schrieb:
> 
> Dear Users or Developers or Mathematicians we need the mathematical
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> ammarkazim@hotmail.com

No use doing that. You can establish formal correspondence between
a language construct in a programming language and a mathematical
theorem or proof. But then you are where you have been before: Who
says that the thusly discovered mathematical structure has
something to do with the intentions of the programmer?

There will always be a substantial gap between formal systems
and real-world problems. What you need is good programmers and good
salaries for them.

Herwig
```

#### ↳ Re: We Need Mathematical proofs for the Language Constructs

- **From:** "Ira D. Baxter" <idbaxter@semdesigns.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-04T09:35:27-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3bbc7635_1@feed1.realtime.net>`
- **References:** `<234f426d.0110030627.48709135@posting.google.com>`

```
You can build very effective reverse engineering tools without any formal
proofs.
The question at hand is not "what can I prove?",
but rather "How is the software organized?".  The latter isn't a proof.
```

frequent comp.lang.cobol submitters).

Windows/98, and Windows/2000.

<a id="t-f7d7e0ae9b"></a>
## Thread: test IF A AND B AND C in COBOL (82) — 21 msgs

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### test IF A AND B AND C in COBOL (82)

- **From:** kyle.katarn@nospam.free.fr (Kyle Katarn)
- **Date:** 2001-10-04T15:26:16+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Xns91309FFF334ACkatarnkyle@2.1.1.45>`

```
    	Hello,

I've a question about the evaluation of the expression:

IF A AND B AND C  ....

if A is false, are B and C evaluate ?
(by example if the validity of B depends of value of A....)

it depends of options ?

(I think that A B C are evaluate first)


    	Thanks !
```

#### ↳ Re: test IF A AND B AND C in COBOL (82)

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-10-04T14:48:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9phspd$ja$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<Xns91309FFF334ACkatarnkyle@2.1.1.45>`

```

On  4-Oct-2001, kyle.katarn@nospam.free.fr (Kyle Katarn) wrote:

> I've a question about the evaluation of the expression:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> it depends of options ?

I don't understand "(by example if the validity of B depends of value of
A....)".    A and B and C are separate conditions.  Testing for A won't
change whether B is true.

Why does it matter whether B and C are evaluated?   Logically the order
doesn't matter - your only concern is which code runs faster (and this
concern will be very rare in this instance).

If your conditions are complex enough and you have knowledge that makes you
smarter than your optimizer - then change the AND to another IF.  
Otherwise, trust your optimizer, which might out-guess you otherwise.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: test IF A AND B AND C in COBOL (82)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-04T11:53:28-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9pi46r$l87$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<Xns91309FFF334ACkatarnkyle@2.1.1.45> <9phspd$ja$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
There are actually two times (that I can think of) that actually impact
program logic - depending on whether all three "things" are evaluated - or
just the first one (that turns out to be false).

1)  If A is false, but B or C have "incompatible data" in them, I know that
HISTORICALLY some compilers have produced code that "abended" - or ended
abnormally.  I believe that this is "standard conforming" as B and C are
"referenced" - even if their values are never needed.  My guess is that MOST
compilers do not do this - but some do (or did).

2) If B or C reference the RANDOM intrinsic function, there is some question
about whether or not one more in the "series" of returned values is
incremented.  This is actually a place where the '85 (actually '89) Standard
and the draft of the next standard differ (for EVALUATE - not IF).  I won't
go into the details (as this really is pretty obscure) - but it COULD impact
program logic.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: test IF A AND B AND C in COBOL (82)

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-10-04T17:33:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a41v7.375164$Lw3.23611968@news2.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<Xns91309FFF334ACkatarnkyle@2.1.1.45> <9phspd$ja$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <9pi46r$l87$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

On  4-Oct-2001, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:

> There are actually two times (that I can think of) that actually impact
> program logic - depending on whether all three "things" are evaluated - or
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> MOST  compilers do not do this - but some do (or did).

Where this is a concern, I nest an IF instead of using AND

> 2) If B or C reference the RANDOM intrinsic function, there is some
> question
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> impact  program logic.

Interesting.

I am trying to think of a case where we care which RANDOM is used first (or
not used at all) in such an IF statement - but I am failing even to come up
with a scenario where I would use RANDOM that way.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: test IF A AND B AND C in COBOL (82)

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-10-05T07:19:17+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BBD50E5.56614F45@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<Xns91309FFF334ACkatarnkyle@2.1.1.45> <9phspd$ja$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <9pi46r$l87$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> There are actually two times (that I can think of) that actually impact
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> compilers do not do this - but some do (or did).

According to Jim Inglis in his 'Cobol 85 for Programmers' in Cobol 85
the "evaluation ceases as soon as the truth value of the expression can
be determined".  The 85 Standard certainly states that data items are
referenced only when required to be evaluated.  My experience is that
all compilers I have used work this way.

It is perfectly safe in '85 (using compilers that correctly implement
this) to have:

         
         PERFORM
             VARYING Table-Index FROM 1 BY 1
               UNTIL Table-Index > Table-Occurs
                  OR Table-Item(Table-Index) = Search-Item

Without having a bound error.  It could fail if the two conditions were
transposed because '85 specifies Left to Right evaluation.  That is: if
a bound error occurs then the compiler is broken.

I have seen examples of code, some posted right here by those who should
know better, where the conditions were ordered incorrectly (ie the
reverse of the above example).  The only reason why they didn't get an
abend seems to be because bound-checking was off when it checked the
array item beyond the bound (or maybe it never failed to match in the
array - but might do at 2am some day).  
 
> 2) If B or C reference the RANDOM intrinsic function, there is some question
> about whether or not one more in the "series" of returned values is
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> program logic.

There is a significant problem with side-effects (or lack of them) when
short-circuit of evaluation occurs and the condition contains a
function.  As you say, it is only RANDOM that has any impact with Cobol,
and this is why user defined functions are such an issue.

If user-defined functions could be used in conditional expressions and
the function has side-effects, then they need to care about whether it
is evaluated or not.

Many inexperienced C programmers are mystified when the code in their
conditionals fails to execute due to short-circuit evaluation.  As
assignment is just another operator in C, it is quite common to have
assignments in conditions.  Cobol programmers are not ready for that, I
doubt that many even consider the implications of combined conditionals
and short circuiting and probably would be tempted to use a GO TO rather
than an AND or an OR.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: test IF A AND B AND C in COBOL (82)

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-10-05T07:36:34+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BBD54F2.9631C1D6@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<Xns91309FFF334ACkatarnkyle@2.1.1.45> <9phspd$ja$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> Why does it matter whether B and C are evaluated?   Logically the order
> doesn't matter - 

Oh yes it does:

     01  Table.
         03  Table-Item             PIC X OCCURS 10.
     ...

         IF X > 0 AND X NOT > 10 AND Table-Item(X) = SPACES
             ...

Logically the order and whether 'C' is evaluated is _very_ important. 
'85 gets this right by doing left to right evaluation, short circuiting
as soon as it can determine the value and by only referencing values
when required.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: test IF A AND B AND C in COBOL (82)

- **From:** Thane Hubbell <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-05T18:14:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ILmv7.17603$ev2.27668@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<Xns91309FFF334ACkatarnkyle@2.1.1.45> <9phspd$ja$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3BBD54F2.9631C1D6@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Just as a matter of "trivia" the ICL runtime of Microsoft.net stops evaluation
when truth can be determined.  This has broken many visual basic programs -
especially those using functions as items in the comparison - as the functions
are never performed if truth can be determined before the fact.



In article <3BBD54F2.9631C1D6@Azonic.co.nz>, Richard Plinston says...
>
>Howard Brazee wrote:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>when required.
```

> Just as a matter of "trivia" the ICL runtime of Microsoft.net
stops evaluation

basic programs -

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: test IF A AND B AND C in COBOL (82)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-05T21:11:38-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9plpdu02qjq@enews3.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<Xns91309FFF334ACkatarnkyle@2.1.1.45> <9phspd$ja$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3BBD54F2.9631C1D6@Azonic.co.nz> <ILmv7.17603$ev2.27668@www.newsranger.com>`

```
I like the density of the code you can write utilizing
short-circuits, but largely avoid them in favor of being
explicit.  Also, I tend to believe that an approach that
ambitiously uses short-circuits will present subtle bugs that
could have been avoid by nesting the checks.

Delphi allows short-circuits based upon a compiler switch--so
you can flip-flop back and forth.  What a reverse-engineering
nightmare!
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: test IF A AND B AND C in COBOL (82)

On Thu, 4 Oct 2001 14:48:57 GMT, "Howard Brazee"
<howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote:

>
>On  4-Oct-2001, kyle.katarn@nospam.free.fr (Kyle Katarn) wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>

It does make sense, though:  

If A NOT EQUAL ZERO AND 1/A GREATER THAN 1000

will give a runtime error if the second condition (1/A > 10000) is
evaluated even after the first (A=0) was evaluated as false

In cases like this, BTW, I use nested IFs

IF A NOT EQUAL ZERO
   IF 1/A GREATER THAN 1000
      PERFORM Some-action
   END-IF
END-IF

>doesn't matter - your only concern is which code runs faster (and this

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

I don't do drugs anymore 'cause I find I get the 
same effect just by standing up really fast.

~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

#### ↳ Re: test IF A AND B AND C in COBOL (82)

- **From:** "Ron" <Ron@NoSpamForMe.Com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-04T11:13:52-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9pi1v7$41n$1@suaar1ac.prod.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<Xns91309FFF334ACkatarnkyle@2.1.1.45>`

```
Looking at the generated assembler code on OS/390 the evaluation
of the expression is terminated when a false condition is found.
In other words, if a 'A' is false then 'B' and 'C' are not evaluated.
In an 'IF' statement with only 'AND' conditions, every test must
be true for the entire 'IF' to be considered true. When one condition
is determined false then the 'IF' is considered false and evaluation
stops. It sometimes can be important how you code this as well.
For example:

01  INPUTREC.
 05  A  PIC 99.
 05  B  PIC XXX.

   IF A IS NUMERIC   AND
      A >= 10        AND
      A <= 20        AND
      B = '123'
         PERFORM DO-SOMETHING.

If the value 'A' is spaces the entire 'IF' is false and evaluation
is abandoned at the numeric test.

If however you code:

  IF A >= 10         AND
     A <= 20         AND
     A IS NUMERIC    AND
     B = '123'
        PERFORM DO-SOMETHING.

You will most likely abend S0C7 if the value of 'A' is spaces because
'A' is not valid.

I know this is kind of a cheesy example... it's just to illustrate the
point how it works.

================================================================


"Kyle Katarn" <kyle.katarn@nospam.free.fr> wrote in message
news:Xns91309FFF334ACkatarnkyle@2.1.1.45...
>     Hello,
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: test IF A AND B AND C in COBOL (82)

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-10-04T16:47:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<oo0v7.101678$hh.9070966@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<Xns91309FFF334ACkatarnkyle@2.1.1.45> <9pi1v7$41n$1@suaar1ac.prod.compuserve.com>`

```

On  4-Oct-2001, "Ron" <Ron@NoSpamForMe.Com> wrote:

>    IF A IS NUMERIC   AND
>       A >= 10        AND
>       A <= 20        AND
>       B = '123'
>          PERFORM DO-SOMETHING.


Why not be safe and change that first AND to an IF?

That way, you don't have to guess that the optimizer will do the same thing
next time.  No maintenance programmers will wonder which will happen first
either.
```

In an 'IF' statement with only 'AND' conditions, every test must

01  INPUTREC.

#### ↳ Re: test IF A AND B AND C in COBOL (82)

- **From:** lesterzick@worldnet.att.net
- **Date:** 2001-10-04T16:30:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3bbc6325.510699@netnews.att.net>`
- **References:** `<Xns91309FFF334ACkatarnkyle@2.1.1.45>`

```
I don't know if it's still true, but years ago the COBOL language
specs and programmer's guide contained detailed charts showing exactly
the order of  evaluation for conditionals. As I recall for the example
shown, without parentheses, the expression would simply be evaluated
left to right and terminated when a false condition is found. If an OR
were included, the AND's would be evaluated first and then the OR.

Regards - Lester 

On Thu, 4 Oct 2001 15:26:16 +0200 , kyle.katarn@nospam.free.fr (Kyle
Katarn) wrote:

>    	Hello,
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Regards - Lester

ref http://home.earthlink.net/~lesterzick
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: test IF A AND B AND C in COBOL (82)

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-10-04T16:50:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3r0v7.374920$Lw3.23592284@news2.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<Xns91309FFF334ACkatarnkyle@2.1.1.45> <3bbc6325.510699@netnews.att.net>`

```

On  4-Oct-2001, lesterzick@worldnet.att.net wrote:

> I don't know if it's still true, but years ago the COBOL language
> specs and programmer's guide contained detailed charts showing exactly
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> were included, the AND's would be evaluated first and then the OR.

These are nice to have and to know - but a wise programmer puts in redundant
parenthesis.

Code that does what you want is necessary.  Code that does what you want -
and is clear - is better.  (your intentions should be clear)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: test IF A AND B AND C in COBOL (82)

- **From:** Calvin Crumrine <Calvin_Crumrine@dced.state.ak.us>
- **Date:** 2001-10-04T10:02:05-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BBCA41C.352BE458@dced.state.ak.us>`
- **References:** `<Xns91309FFF334ACkatarnkyle@2.1.1.45> <3bbc6325.510699@netnews.att.net> <3r0v7.374920$Lw3.23592284@news2.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:

> On  4-Oct-2001, lesterzick@worldnet.att.net wrote:
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> and is clear - is better.  (your intentions should be clear)

Similarly wise programmers don't rely on defaults. Explicitly initialize your
variables, etc. Not only is it clearer but defaults change with different
installations while explicit statements don't.
```

#### ↳ Re: test IF A AND B AND C in COBOL (82)

- **From:** "James" <mangogrower@telocity.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-04T14:16:08-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qG1v7.8411$W11.1909400@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net>`
- **References:** `<Xns91309FFF334ACkatarnkyle@2.1.1.45>`

```
The question (restated) is:
IF CONDITION-A TRUE and CONDITION-B TRUE and CONDITION-C TRUE

Well, If CONDITION-A AINT TRUE, it don't matter what the others be.
Without Parenthesis, the compilers will evaluate similar relations /
operands in LEFT to RIGHT order.

The compilers should be ignoring B&C if A is FALSE.  (Without Optimization)

#### ↳ Re: test IF A AND B AND C in COBOL (82)

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2001-10-05T03:22:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lI9v7.6030$bp6.1122533658@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<Xns91309FFF334ACkatarnkyle@2.1.1.45>`

```
    On a related note, older compilers used to croak on the following if A
was not numeric:
IF A > ZERO AND A NUMERIC
As a result, it was important to reverse the checks as follows:
IF A NUMERIC AND A > ZERO
I've noticed recently that the first example will no longer abend.  It
apparently checks for numeric before checking the value of A.
```

Herwig Huener  <Herwig.Huener@fujitsu-siemens.com> wrote:
>2001-10-04 16:00:00 MEST

…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>productivity. I heard that with my own ears.

Nothing new there... I have been asked, as a consultant who is paid on an
hourly basis, to work hours and not bill for them.  My response has been
that I would no more think of turning my client into a thief, stealing my
time, than I would think of turning myself into a thief and stealing money
by billing for hours I have not worked; both are equally hateful to me.

DD
```

news:j2_u7.8300$ym4.356946@iad-read.news.verio.net...

I have worked hours as a consultant, and not billed them - but only to
monitor production
as part of support.

But when my lead TOLD me to reduce the number of hours billed, because there
weren't
enough hours in the project - he'd done the sizing - I told him he would
have to talk to the
account manager at my agency. The numb nuts actually did that. My account
manager called
the client's staffing office, who called the manager's boss, who "explained"
a few things to my
lead. I wasn't asked not to bill hours worked, after that, but my manager
got ME to do more
of the sizings, after that.

 .
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT (Humor) RESUME 2001

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-10-05T12:34:06+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3bbd01bf_6@news.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<B7DC8DD7.3EE8%edgould@ameritech.net> <9p7jia$2a4$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3BBC6B1B.C6C5FF07@fujitsu-siemens.com> <j2_u7.8300$ym4.356946@iad-read.news.verio.net> <j92v7.100791$aZ6.22848834@news1.rdc1.az.home.com>`

```
LOL! Good one, Rufio!

Instances like this make me think that the current European trend of billing
for a "professional day" is not such a bad idea.

(Personally, I like to work this way because it is more "goal oriented" than
the hourly rate. We all know that there are a certain percentage of hours
which are worked which don't necessarily achieve much (or even anything) yet
they are billed for nevertheless...)

The "professional day" was started by companies who figured they could get 9
or 10 hours for the price of 8...

However, it cuts both ways... My "professional" day ends when I have
achieved the detailed goals I set each morning...  This means my day is
anything between 6 and 8 hours...On very rare occasions it might go to 10,
or even 12, the point is that it is goal driven. I do whatever it takes and
encourage teams to do likewise. This is a different mind set from hourly
work. If you are motivated, it is actually fun to achieve the goals you set
and the odd "hard day" is no problem.

I don't know if this approach is being adopted in the USA, but I would
advise embracing it if it is <G> (Be sure that you deliver, though...)

When assessing workloads, I work on the basis of 5.5 PRODUCTIVE hours per
person per day.

The reasons behind this are too involved to go into here, but it works.

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT (Humor) RESUME 2001

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Rufio" <davecawdell@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-05T04:11:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<upav7.102163$aZ6.23267822@news1.rdc1.az.home.com>`
- **References:** `<B7DC8DD7.3EE8%edgould@ameritech.net> <j2_u7.8300$ym4.356946@iad-read.news.verio.net> <j92v7.100791$aZ6.22848834@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <3bbd01bf_6@news.newsgroups.com> <y_8v7.8378$ym4.359422@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
My problems came about, because of a single manager, my lead P/A. He was
more interested in covering his own butt after screwing up, than in running
the team responsibly. He has done no technical work or day-to-day project
management for over 10 years, and doesn't know how to now. As a result
anyone outside the team, who wanted information, came to me or my buddy
(also a senior P/A0 or the business analyst, and we took care of it. The
only time we had problems, was when the lead P/A found out, and inadvisably
kicked up a fuss.

But once you find an honest client manager, stick with them. It is possible
to simply do what you feel is an honest day, then go home, regardless of
whether you've done 6 hours or 10. Once the manager sees you make your
goals, they generally don't care how you achieve them - at least not the
good managers.

But as I haven't worked since April, I'd even be HAPPY to work for a bad
manager. Anyone need a Senior/Lead P/A? MVS, IMS DB/DC DB2 CICS ? With 14
years service. WILL WORK FOR MONEY !

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT (Humor) RESUME 2001

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-10-05T12:18:55+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3bbcfe22_8@news.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<B7DC8DD7.3EE8%edgould@ameritech.net> <9p7jia$2a4$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3BBC6B1B.C6C5FF07@fujitsu-siemens.com> <j2_u7.8300$ym4.356946@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
This raises a very good point, Doc.

I completely agree with your reasoning, however, there HAVE been occasions
when I have worked considerable hours without billing for it...

Your post made me think about this, and I have come up with the following
"rules" as to when extra unbillable hours would be acceptable FOR ME (I
accept that this is a VERY personal thing and some people will NEVER work
unbilled hours; I don't think they are wrong...)

1. If the Client ASKS me to work unbilled hours (as in your example) I would
NOT do it.  (This is demeaning and insulting. In effect they are saying: "We
know you can't get a job anywhere else so if you wish to continue enjoying
working for us, then scratch our backs...". I would politely but firmly
decline and if they decided to terminate the contract I would consider it a
lucky escape...<G>)

Such a client is unlikely to have my services in the future, UNLESS they
offered some other form of incentive (like, say a fully paid holiday in some
exotic location - I have had two instances (in twenty five years) where this
was done - the reasoning was that they could raid the "travel" budget more
easily than they could get more "contract resource" cash....).

2. If I have responsibility for goals and targets and I undercall it, I see
that as MY failure, not theirs, so I will gladly put in extra unbillable
hours to make it right.

3. If I accept THEIR time estimates and deadlines then point 2 above also
applies. Once it is agreed, then it is just the same as if I had done it. If
I have reservations about the timeframe I will explain why, where I see
pitfalls, and negotiate...

As contractors we have all been given unreasonable estimates from Managers
who are seeking to further their careers and don't necessarily understand
the effort involved. Here is an actual scenario, almost verbatim, that
happened in my career many years ago...

Manager: "This has to be done by Thursday."

(I take the spec. and read it)

Me: "Good luck. I hope you find someone who can do it. I can't."

(Look of shock from Manager...)
Manager: "What do you mean?! It has to be done by Thursday."

Me: "I can do it by Monday if I work some time over the weekend, drop what
I'm doing, and give this top priority. It will need exclusive access to the
system and that can only be obtained at the weekend anyway, it is predicated
on certain modules being available; I happen to know that at least one of
these is still being written, and there is a large amount of coding
required, which takes time."

In the end, he went away, consulted his Boss, and came back the next day.

Manager: "OK, Monday."

Me:"Nope, sorry, now it'll be Tuesday."

Manager: "But you said Monday!".

Me: "I did indeed. But that was yesterday and 24 hours have now been
wasted."

It was delivered on Tuesday morning...

Now the question arises, should I bill for the extra time I put in? Most
reasonable people would say "Yes", and especially as weekend work was
involved.

(As it happened, this was in a strange country (well, strange to me that
is...<G>) and I had nothing to do over the weekend anyway. In fact, I was
quite happy to get some peace and quiet to do the job. Being of a naturally
generous disposition, I decided NOT to bill them for the weekend work (it
was about 10 hours), but write it off to "Goodwill" instead...

Bad idea.

Later, when I needed some time for a personal reason, my request was
declined. I gently reminded them that there had been numerous occasions (the
above was not the only one...) when I had worked above and beyond what was
required and could they please cut me some slack. No dice. If they did that
for me they would have to do it for everybody. (Like, that is SUCH a bad
idea <G>?).

When the contract came up for renewal they asked me to extend. I declined.
They offered more money. I told them it wasn't about money, I just needed to
move on...

I have never worked there since and wouldn't, under the same conditions of
employment. (I would manage projects for them, because this is a different
deal. I have a large degree of autonomy and am paid on results.)

3. If a company I have had a good relationship with has an "emergency" I
will work whatever it takes and worry about how much of it gets billed,
later.

This is simple Marketing. I sell my service and sometimes, as in any
Marketing operation, it is necessary to look after the "regulars". However,
if they "expect" this or (as in Doc's example), actually ASK for it, then my
natural generosity disappears like frost in Summer...

SUMMARISING:

It is good to be reasonable. Be generous where you can. But don't let people
take advantage of your good nature...<G>

Pete.

PS. I endorse wholeheartedly Doc's point that he wouldn't bill for hours not
worked. I would have no truck with any contractor who did this.

From my time at the Deutsche Bank:

Project Leader: 'So... can you do it?'

Me: 'Of course I can do it... this is what I'm paid for.'

Home-Office Supervisor: 'Occhhhh... herrre at ze Deutsche Bank ve like to
do chust a liddle pit *more* zen vhut ve are *paidt* to.'

Me: 'Oh, really?  I used to do that... but then I learned something.'

H-O S: 'Oohhh?  Undt vhut didt you learn?'

Me: 'I learned that people were taking advantage of me.'


H-O S: 'Mmmpppphhhhhh'... and turning that funny shade of red that Germans
turn when they are trying not to laugh.

Some people confuse generosity with weakness.

DD

## Thread: Zeros in my code :( — 4 msgs

### Zeros in my code :(

- **From:** "DJ_JB" <jwh014@eminem.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-04T12:36:30-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d51v7.4777$WX5.78149@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com>`

```
Ok my problem is when I run my program I get zeros at the very top of my
output file.  I've tried moving spaces, etc... Initializing...  fillers...
Anyway, just want to know if someone might be able to help me out...

Thanks


IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
 PROGRAM-ID. VIDEO.
**************************************************************
* This program makes a master file that is created from the *
* rental file and the sales file.                            *
**************************************************************
 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
 INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
 FILE-CONTROL.
     SELECT RENT-IN ASSIGN TO "d:\rentalrec"
       ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.
     SELECT SALES-IN ASSIGN TO "d:\salesrec"
       ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.
     SELECT MERGED1 ASSIGN TO "d:\mergedrec"
       ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.
 DATA DIVISION.
 FILE SECTION.
 FD RENT-IN LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD.
 01 RENTAL-REC.
         05 ITEM-IN-SALES         PICTURE 9(3).
         05 VIDEO-NAME-IN-SALES   PICTURE x(17).
         05 TAPES-IN-STOCK-SALES  PICTURE 9(3).
 FD SALES-IN LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD.
 01 SALE-REC.
         05 ITEM-IN-RENTAL         PICTURE 9(3).
         05 VIDEO-NAME-RENTAL      PICTURE x(17).
         05 TAPES-FOR-SALE-RENTAL  PICTURE 9(3).
 FD MERGED1 LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD.
 01 MERGEDREC.
         05                            PICTURE XXX.
         05 OUT-ITEM-IN-SALES          PICTURE 9(3).
         05 OUT-VIDEO-NAME-SALES       PICTURE x(17).
         05 OUT-TAPES-IN-STOCK         PICTURE 9(3).
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
 01 ARE-THERE-MORE-RECORDS      PIC X(3) VALUE 'YES'.
 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
 100-MAIN-MODULE.
     OPEN INPUT RENT-IN
          INPUT SALES-IN
          OUTPUT MERGED1
     PERFORM UNTIL ARE-THERE-MORE-RECORDS = 'NO'
       READ RENT-IN
        AT END
         MOVE 'NO' TO ARE-THERE-MORE-RECORDS
          NOT AT END
           PERFORM 200-RENTAL-RTN
         END-READ
       READ SALES-IN
          AT END
           MOVE 'NO' TO ARE-THERE-MORE-RECORDS
          NOT AT END
         PERFORM 300-SALES-RTN
       END-READ
        END-PERFORM
     CLOSE RENT-IN
           SALES-IN
           MERGED1
     STOP RUN.
 200-RENTAL-RTN.
     INITIALIZE MERGEDREC
     MOVE ITEM-IN-RENTAL TO OUT-ITEM-IN-SALES
     MOVE VIDEO-NAME-RENTAL TO OUT-VIDEO-NAME-SALES
     MOVE TAPES-FOR-SALE-RENTAL TO OUT-TAPES-IN-STOCK
     WRITE MERGEDREC.
 300-SALES-RTN.
     INITIALIZE MERGEDREC
     MOVE ITEM-IN-SALES TO OUT-ITEM-IN-SALES
     MOVE VIDEO-NAME-IN-SALES TO OUT-VIDEO-NAME-SALES
     MOVE TAPES-IN-STOCK-SALES TO OUT-TAPES-IN-STOCK
     WRITE MERGEDREC.
```

#### ↳ Re: Zeros in my code :(

- **From:** Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-10-04T12:49:00-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BBCA10C.BD2E3E1E@mb.sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<d51v7.4777$WX5.78149@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com>`

```
DJ_JB wrote:

> Ok my problem is when I run my program I get zeros at the very top of my
> output file.  I've tried moving spaces, etc... Initializing...  fillers...
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Is there a record in your input file that's full of zeros?

Also: it looks to me as though you won't get all the records from both files
written out.  Since you're setting your end-of-file flag whenever EITHER file
terminates, the read loop will miss any records in the file that has more
records.

PL
```

#### ↳ Re: Zeros in my code :(

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-10-05T06:39:34+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BBD4796.BDE77A62@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<d51v7.4777$WX5.78149@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com>`

```
DJ_JB wrote:
> 
> Ok my problem is when I run my program I get zeros at the very top of my
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>      WRITE MERGEDREC.

An INITIALIZE only affects named items.  MergedRec contains an unnamed
item (implicitly a FILLER) which will be ignored by the INITIALIZE.  Use
MOVE SPACES TO MergedRec instead of, or before, INITIALIZE, or named the
first field.
```

#### ↳ Re: Zeros in my code :(

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2001-10-07T12:22:42-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0110071122.1698c9ca@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<d51v7.4777$WX5.78149@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com>`

```
"DJ_JB" <jwh014@eminem.com> wrote in message news:<d51v7.4777$WX5.78149@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com>...
> Ok my problem is when I run my program I get zeros at the very top of my
> output file.  I've tried moving spaces, etc... Initializing...  fillers...
…[74 more quoted lines elided]…
>      WRITE MERGEDREC.
This looks suspiciously like a homework assignment.

If it isn't, then the easiest way to merge two files is to simply
append one to the other using the MS-DOS COPY command thus :

         COPY d:\rentalrec+d:\salesrec d:\mergedrec

         The snag with this is your filenames, which in two cases are
nine bytes long, you could "cheat" by renaming them with explorer for
the purposes of using COPY, then rename them back to
their original names afterwards.

If it is a homework assignment, then what I suspect is required is to
merge the two files in key sequence, where ITEM-IN-SALES and
ITEM-IN-RENTAL are the key fields for the input files and
OUT-ITEM-IN-SALES is the key field for the output file.  If so, then
the COBOL MERGE statement would be the best way of doing it, using an
OUTPUT PROCEDURE to create the output records from the merged input
and putting spaces in the leading three byte filler field of the
output file.  Alternately, it  might be that the task is somewhat more
complex, requiring you to produce an output file that represents the
stock held after accounting for the sales made.  In this case you
would have to provide matching logic in your program code to ensure
that sales and rental records tie up even when there are missing
records for particular items in one or other of  the two input files. 
This would include the necessity to handle the situations where the
first two records don't match and/or  where the last two records don't
match.  For matching records you would have to add the fields
representing numbers of items together to produce the output result.

However, these potential solutions are speculative, we would have to
see the problem specification, for example it may be that the
video-names are the key fields.  I can't see from your program whether
the item fields are identity codes or numeric quantities.
```

## Thread: Math Survey: — 2 msgs

### Math Survey:

- **From:** "JerryP" <news@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-05T11:35:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9Wgv7.808369$ai2.61913050@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
Question: This is "Math Awareness" month. Are you aware of math?"

Answer 1: "Yes, I am aware of math, having been at one time a math major. I
am also aware that I am aware of math. However, I had not been aware that I
am aware that I was aware of math. Hence, until this survey I had been
unaware of math at the second derivative of awareness. Now that this survey
has come out, however, and I have had a chance to reflect while composing
this message, I am aware that I am aware that I am aware of math."

Answer 2: "Yes. However, the one place in which I do not see math at all is
at my work. I have spent more years than I care to admit working as a
computer programmer, and not once have I had the opportunity to work with
math. This should be comforting to those concerned with America losing its
place as the world's leader in high technology. Our students' obliviousness
to math will in no way affect their computer skills."

More survey answers at:

http://www.improb.com/news/2001/apr/math-awareness.html
```

<a id="t-b86e0ade58"></a>
## Thread: SELECT ... ASSIGN TO UT-S-XXXX. — 14 msgs

### SELECT ... ASSIGN TO UT-S-XXXX.

- **From:** dimbriale@bear.com (Don Imbriale)
- **Date:** 2001-10-05T12:35:01-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<340ff33e.0110051135.2f19a38c@posting.google.com>`

```
IBM mainframe COBOL: In the ASSIGN clause on a SELECT statement under
FILE-CONTROL. in the INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION., is there any significance
to the "UT-"?  What about the "S-"?  It seems that any values are
accepted by the compiler.  Do they affect processing?  Are they
needed?
```

#### ↳ Re: SELECT ... ASSIGN TO UT-S-XXXX.

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-05T15:15:17-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9pl4gu$bno$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<340ff33e.0110051135.2f19a38c@posting.google.com>`

```
Assuming an OS/390, z/OS, or VM environment, see
  http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igylr205/4.2.3.1
for what is and is not "meaningful" in currently supported IBM mainframe
compilers.

I believe that VSE still "parses" some of the other prefixes. (Let me know
if  you need me to find the online references for that)

OLDER COBOL's used the "stuff before the DDName" for a LOT more information
than the current compilers/run-times do.  If you are supporting older
(DOS/VS COBOL, OS/VS COBOL, or even ANS COBOL) applications, then you will
need to find the HARDCOPY versions of those manuals for all the "gruesome"
details.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: SELECT ... ASSIGN TO UT-S-XXXX.

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-10-06T11:25:34+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3bbe425b_3@news.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<340ff33e.0110051135.2f19a38c@posting.google.com> <9pl4gu$bno$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
The "UT" stands for "Utility" which translates as "disk" (the general
workhorse medium of IBM Mainframes).

The "S" means "Sequential", so the ASSIGN is telling the compiler that this
will be a sequential disk file which can be allocated anywhere on available
disk storage.

Like Bill said, it applies more to the "old days" and is probably not so
relevant or necessary now...

As COBOL moves further away from its original simplicity, it is pretty
difficult to keep up with what is relevant. Fortunately, we have guys like
Bill in the NG who DO keep up with it...<G>

For myself (and a number of others who have written to me), I just write it
and if it doesn't work, THEN I investigate it...

This is an easier strategy than concerning myself with the details of what
is in every COBOL Standard (real or imagined, implemented or
"forthcoming"...), which MIGHT  (or might not) be implemented by the
particular vendor whose platform I am looking at.

To be fair, it is many years now since I wrote IBM COBOL Mainframe code.

Pete.
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:9pl4gu$bno$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net...
> Assuming an OS/390, z/OS, or VM environment, see
>   http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igylr205/4.2.3.1
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> OLDER COBOL's used the "stuff before the DDName" for a LOT more
information
> than the current compilers/run-times do.  If you are supporting older
> (DOS/VS COBOL, OS/VS COBOL, or even ANS COBOL) applications, then you will
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>

From my very old "Fundamentals of COBOL Programming" by Carl Feingold,
the Class Indicator can be and have the following meanings:

UR = Unit Record - card reader, card punch, printer

UT = Utility - Tape or Disk or Drum or Cell (note:  if UT and Disk or
Tape, organization must be S)

DA = Direct Access - Disk, Drum or Cell (note:  organization indicator
must be I or D).

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

I don't do drugs anymore 'cause I find I get the 
same effect just by standing up really fast.

~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SELECT ... ASSIGN TO UT-S-XXXX.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-10-08T11:14:06+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3bc0d429_7@news.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<340ff33e.0110051135.2f19a38c@posting.google.com> <9pl4gu$bno$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <3bbe425b_3@news.newsgroups.com> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gkslo80.pminews@News.CIS.DFN.DE>`

```
Before there were disk drives, tapes were "utility devices".

I remember programming under TOS (Tape Operating System) and it used to be
real fun to watch the SYSRES tape loading logical transients...

When the first 360s became available we used to run them at night with all
the lights out...

Watching the console lights and the tape selector lights on the tape drives
was a VERY spooky experience. Protected tapes had a red light, tapes being
written had a green light and when a drive was selected it had a yellow
(white) light. As the program progressed these lights would dance along the
tape drives.

The SYSRES tape was nearly ALWAYS "busy" and it used to be housed on a drive
which could read forward and backwards so it could be passed faster (not ALL
Tape drives had this capability; it cost extra).

The concept of a UTILITY device changed when the first 2311s arrived, but
COBOL supported either tape or disk as Utility.

The "DA" was for "Direct Access" and that meant "non-sequential" and
therefore could NOT be a UTILITY device.

The "UR" meant "Unit Record" which really meant "unblocked" (early printers
and punch card readers/punches accepted a single "record"). It was only
later when we started spooling print to disk that print files became
"blocked", but COBOL still allowed them to be treated as "unit record" (as
it does to this day...).

Ah, happy times...<G>

Pete.

"Willem Clements" <willem@horizontes-informatica.com> wrote in message

```

On  5-Oct-2001, dimbriale@bear.com (Don Imbriale) wrote:

> IBM mainframe COBOL: In the ASSIGN clause on a SELECT statement under
> FILE-CONTROL. in the INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION., is there any significance
> to the "UT-"?  What about the "S-"?  It seems that any values are
> accepted by the compiler.  Do they affect processing?  Are they
> needed?


They used to be required, and still are accepted.  I don't use them anymore.
```

#### ↳ Re: SELECT ... ASSIGN TO UT-S-XXXX.

- **From:** dimbriale@bear.com (Don Imbriale)
- **Date:** 2001-10-08T14:51:04-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<340ff33e.0110081351.733aa307@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<340ff33e.0110051135.2f19a38c@posting.google.com>`

```
dimbriale@bear.com (Don Imbriale) wrote in message news:<340ff33e.0110051135.2f19a38c@posting.google.com>...
> IBM mainframe COBOL: In the ASSIGN clause on a SELECT statement under
> FILE-CONTROL. in the INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION., is there any significance
> to the "UT-"?  What about the "S-"?  It seems that any values are
> accepted by the compiler.  Do they affect processing?  Are they
> needed?

Thanks for all the responses.  From the COBOL Language Reference
manual:

"label-
    Documents the device and device class to which a file is assigned.
 If
    specified, it must end with a hyphen.

S-
    For QSAM files, the S- (organization) field can be omitted.

AS-
    For VSAM sequential files, the AS- (organization) field must be
    specified.

    For VSAM indexed and relative files, the organization field must
be
    omitted.

The syntax diagram shows that both are optional.  Empirical testing
reveals that any characters are acceptable for the 'label'.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: SELECT ... ASSIGN TO UT-S-XXXX.

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-08T17:12:37-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9pt8ef$ij7$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<340ff33e.0110051135.2f19a38c@posting.google.com> <340ff33e.0110081351.733aa307@posting.google.com>`

```
FYI -
  I have gone thru my "historical" manuals and have found in
    GC28-6396-5
        which is the LRM for "IBM OS Full American National Standard COBOL"
aka ANS V4

that the "last" IBM mainframe compiler to actually "use" this field was ANS
COBOL V3.  From ANS V4 on (including OS/VS COBOL, VS COBOL II, AD/Cycle SAA
COBOL/370, IBM COBOL for MVS & VM, and IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM) this has
been treated as totally a "documentary" field.  (I am still not positive if
all the VSE compiler also "ignore" it.)

In the older compiler (ANS COBOL V3 and earlier) there were a BUNCH of
fields that had meaning.  Some of these are discussed in the "no longer
supported" section of the current Migration Guide - see
  http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igymg202/4.1.5?

and see the sections on
  "ASSIGN TO integer system-name"
    and
  "ASSIGN ... FOR MULTIPLE REEL/UNIT"

The ANS COBOL V4 LRM does "confirm" that when IBM last supported
"meaningful" entries in this field - that it only supported the three values
(already discussed in this thread) of
   DA (mass storage)
   UT (utility)
   UR (unit-record)
        - (The parenthetical are what page 74 of that manual calls them)

If anyone REALLY needs/wants the gruesome details on this, I probably could
scan the 3 pages talking about the ASSIGN clause in that manual.  However, I
assume that as this field has been treated as "documentary ONLY" for a
couple of decades now, that this probably isn't useful.  Please send me
PRIMATE email, if you need/want those pages.
```

- **From:** "JerryP" <news@bisusa.com>

- **Message-ID:** `<zTCv7.632727$NK1.57364914@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
A project created under V5 when compiled with V6 yields the following fatal
message:

"Invalid setting or getting of the property...."

for the code:

MOVE POW-TRUE TO 'CHECK' OF OURMENU

and similar lines (there are maybe 30 lines like this in the project - each
is flagged as an error).

Changing the offending line to "MOVE 1 TO ...  " or "MOVE 0 TO ..."

makes no difference; the error message remains unchanged.

A project created from scratch in V6 with the same line of code compiles
cleanly.

FJ tech support wants a copy of the project, but that's a medium-sized
package to put together, so I thought someone here might have encountered
this situation.

Thanks in advance,

Jerry
```

If X.PPJ exhibits the problem symptons, do the PowerCOBOL shuffle:

Open: X.PPJ
Save as: A.PPJ
Open: A.PPJ
Save as: X.PPJ

X.PPJ is automagically healed.

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> out).

Here too.  Creating a new project and dragging each form over to the new
one somehow fixes whatever it is that mysteriously crashed the original
project file.  Most annoying complication is that it is necessary to
tediously copy over all the properties at the project and module levels.

> 
> By the way, I can't figure out how the heck PowerGem could work with
> PowerCOBOL inasmuch as a PPJ file isn't REALLY source code. Any ideas? (FJ
> is researching the question (giggle, giggle).)
>
There is indeed a source file with a .COB extension located in the
subfolder which PowerCOBOL creates for each project.  The identifier
names therein are nothing like the code you wrote but you can easily
identify the procedures.
```

- **Date:** 2001-10-11T12:00:26+00:00

## Thread: Look for tips on EURO conversion (decimals) — 12 msgs

### Look for tips on EURO conversion (decimals)

- **From:** "Federico Ghigo" <f.ghigo@dicaf.it>
- **Date:** 2001-10-06T14:51:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fj.comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<BTEv7.8151$DW5.180215@news2.tin.it>`

```
Hi,
I want to convert my SW to EURO and I was wandering if there was a tip for
having a PIC ZZZ.ZZZ,99 field moved to a PIC 9(8) without loosing the
decimals (this would allow me not to change anything inside the program);
can anyone help me pls ?
```

#### ↳ Re: Look for tips on EURO conversion (decimals)

- **From:** "Willem Clements" <willem@horizontes-informatica.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-06T19:17:37+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fj.comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gkt4pd0.pminews@news.wanadoo.es>`
- **References:** `<BTEv7.8151$DW5.180215@news2.tin.it>`

```
On Sat, 06 Oct 2001 14:51:13 GMT, Federico Ghigo wrote:

>Hi,
>I want to convert my SW to EURO and I was wandering if there was a tip for
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Hi,

I don't understand what you are trying to do. Do you have edited fields as
input? Also, if Euro is your final result, it should have two decimals as
well. What is your current currency?

Regards,
Willem Clements
BrainBench MVP for COBOL II

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Look for tips on EURO conversion (decimals)

- **From:** "Federico Ghigo" <f.ghigo@dicaf.it>
- **Date:** 2001-10-08T14:10:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fj.comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Zsiw7.16564$DW5.334684@news2.tin.it>`
- **References:** `<BTEv7.8151$DW5.180215@news2.tin.it> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gkt4pd0.pminews@news.wanadoo.es>`

```
Hi, first of all thanks for your interest.
Actually my SW works with Lire (no decimals). All inputs are in numeric
edited formats (only integers of course). Now I use a pic zzz.zzz.zzz.zz9-
for inputing numbers, that I subsequentely move into a 9(12) working field.
I have to change the edited to a pic z.zzz.zzz.zz9,99- (for Euros)  (I
decided to use the same number of characters, always 12). If I could obtain
that it behaves as an integer when I move it into the 9(12) ( that means
that the comma should be just an "esthetic" view) I wouldn't have to worry
about anything that "happens" into the procedure, the other fields, and the
files too.

For instance if now I accept 1.568.256,25 and then I move it into the 9(12)
I get, of course, 1568256, while I would like to get 156825625 ( with the
decimals too): this would allow me not to worry about all the other elements
of the program.

Sincerely,
Federico Ghigo
DI.CAF. S.p.A.
f.ghigo@dicaf.it

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Look for tips on EURO conversion (decimals)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-10-09T11:33:51+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fj.comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3bc22cad_4@news.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<BTEv7.8151$DW5.180215@news2.tin.it> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gkt4pd0.pminews@news.wanadoo.es> <Zsiw7.16564$DW5.334684@news2.tin.it> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gkwmf00.pminews@news.wanadoo.es>`

```
PMFJI

Willem,

your suggestion will just cause two zeros to be appended to the "rightmost"
10 digits of his input.

But you are on the right track...

He needs to redefine the 12 digit field as follows:

01 twelve-digits       pic 9(12).
01 twelve-digits-dec redefines twelve-digits pic 9(10)v99.

Now he can move his "input" to twelve-digits (as he currently does), but
manipulate twelve-digits-dec (which has the correct implied decimal) from
then on.

A note in passing...

It is very bad practice, in my opinion, to attempt to "de-edit" an edited
field by manipulating it. I don't do it and most of the versions of COBOL I
grew up with don't allow it. (Of course, nowadays the Language has become
bastardized in response to users who never really understood how to use it,
and an over-eagerness on the part of the Standards Authority to appease
these users... we are still seeing this, but that's another story...).

Once you move something to an edited picture, that's it... Leave it alone!
These edits are intended for output, not for manipulation.

HTH,

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Look for tips on EURO conversion (decimals)

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-10-15T19:12:09+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fj.comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3bca7ea2_7@news.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<BTEv7.8151$DW5.180215@news2.tin.it> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gkt4pd0.pminews@news.wanadoo.es> <Zsiw7.16564$DW5.334684@news2.tin.it> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gkwmf00.pminews@news.wanadoo.es> <3bc22cad_4@news.newsgroups.com> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gkxwd50.pminews@News.CIS.DFN.DE> <3bc394f8_7@news.newsgroups.com> <w7oy7.28927$ev2.35629@www.newsranger.com>`

```
Comments below, Jack.

Pete.

"John H Sleight Jr" <nospam@newsranger.com> wrote in message
news:w7oy7.28927$ev2.35629@www.newsranger.com...
> Hi Pete, et al,
>
> I read your comments about de-editing. I assume your comments apply to the
new
> allowable MOVE of a num/edit fld to a num fld. I thought it was a Godsend
for
> dealing w/flds when forced to use a report as input (I've been told to no
more
> than one occasion), or in more recent times, inputs from Access and Excel.
>
> If my assumption is correct, could you please amplify your disapproval
with
> emphasis on the Move de-edit capability. I'd value your input.
>
OK, let me start by saying that this is purely a personal opinion and I
don't think that people who disagree are "wrong". I can only tell you why
<I> don't move edited fields to numeric fields ("de-edit" a field).

I see a strong difference between numeric fields and fields that contain
anything OTHER than a number. This goes back to my background in Assembler
on various platforms (particularly with IBM mainframes which, right from the
time Gene Amdahl designed the famous System 360, included checking
"circuitry" (firmware, actually)  to detect invalid numbers.)

These systems were what you might call (certainly by today's standards)
"unforgiving". If the "data check" failed, the program was wiped, with a
dump. Programmers came to dread the famous "S0C7" which usually meant you
had "packed a blank"...(moved blanks or some other non-numeric character(s)
to a field which was supposed to contain a packed decimal operand.)

To get numeric data displayed with decimal points and signs in it we used
MASK instructions and always ensured that this was the FINAL thing that was
done on that field. So the association with "editing" and "OUTPUT" began to
get established in our (well, certainly in mine, anyway <G>) brains. Very
often, when this simple principle wasn't followed, the effect was disastrous
and sometimes it could require tedious poring over hexadecimal output to
locate and correct what had happened.

With the advent of COBOL (after we got over our initial resistance to this
"system resource devourer"...<G>) we came to realise that typing and
automatic "casting" was a feature of the language and all the editing was
done by masks in the PICTURE. However, COBOL made no distinction between
"numeric edited" and "alphanumeric edited" fields. (So you couldn't just
assume that an edited field must be numeric...). We very soon learned the
rules and found that if you moved numeric fields to numeric edited fields
everything worked fine. If you got cheeky and redefined an edited field with
the idea of using it again, all Hell often broke loose... The "rule" that
editing should be the final operation on a number was thus reinforced.

This brings us to the present, where as you correctly point out, systems are
required to deal with edited input from spreadsheets, databases, and
reports.

I handle this by NOT thinking of these fields as "numeric edited" or even as
numbers, but as STRINGS. For me, if it contains ANYTHING other than a
number, then it is a STRING. The language has been extended to permit the
moving of edited numeric data to numeric fields and decimal alignment is
handled for the COBOL programmer (the old "dot goes on the V", trick). We
are currently examining intrinsic functions like NUMVAL and TEST-NUMVAL.

Not for me.

I do a fair bit of development for Windows in PowerCOBOL. Text fields on
screens can contain numbers or strings. But PowerCOBOL ALWAYS treats them as
a string. (This is a different mindset to the 3270 days where you could set
numeric attributes on the screen and be assured that only a number could be
input... I'm not sure whether PowerCOBOL supports numeric only input or not,
to be honest, but I have become accustomed to dealing with ALL input as
STRING.)

So, I spent a few days and decided to put this "problem" to bed forever. The
result is the component I described elsewhere, "String2Num".

If I have to use numeric input which may be edited, I just give it to this
component and it gives me everything I could possibly want, in every
possible format, or it tells me the input is not valid numeric.

It is exactly the same principle as having a callable subroutine which looks
at a string and does the biz...

As a result, I won't need NUMVAL or TEST-NUMVAL because both these functions
are combined in the component.

Now, if you are required to process data from reports (how sad is THAT?!
Sounds like there is room for some design improvements on your site...<G>),
I can see that using a de-editing move can be of some benefit. I still
believe it is error-prone, but needs must when the Devil drives...<G>.

BOTTOM LINE:

1. If you use the capability to "de-edit" fields which is now available in
COBOL, EXPECT to have problems. If you don't, then it will be a pleasant
bonus, won't it? <G>

2. Use NUMVAL to check input strings (if your compiler supports intrinsic
functions) or else do your own as I did.
(The fact that I wrote "String2Num" is not an implied criticism of NUMVAL. I
wanted a component that could run in different environments (not just COBOL)
and I wanted to extend the functionality.
In theory, (I haven't done it yet) I can embed this component on a web page,
for example, and check input to a HTML form, without using CGI script. (The
Applet code can use the component's Methods and Properties).

In the final analysis we all use what works for us. I don't see a "DO" or
"DON'T" or a "right" or "wrong" here, but I would ADVISE against de-editing
if at all possible.

Pete.
>
> In article <3bc394f8_7@news.newsgroups.com>, Peter E. C. Dashwood says...
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> >Now he can move his "input" to twelve-digits (as he currently does),
but
> >> >manipulate twelve-digits-dec (which has the correct implied decimal)
from
> >> >then on.
> >> >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> Don't think so, he states that he looses the last two positions, so
he'll
> >> have DECIMAL POINT IS COMMA defined.
> >> With your solution, he'll still lose the last two digits, with mine he
> >won't.
> >>
> >> I do however agree with you that de-editing should be avoided, that's
why
> >I
> >> asked the guy if he really had an edited field as input.
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>

We seem to be able to get these to run as per the spec. BUT... When
using PC_PRINTER_OPEN the pop-up window that allows the user to select
the printer etc. has PaperSize and Source selection boxes (Source
offering "continuous Page Break" and "Continuous No Page Break" for
example). What I'm looking for is where is the value of the user's
entered "Sources" stored and how can I subsequently set it directly
from the application?

Using PC_PRINTER_INFO does not appear to return "Source", or at least
not in an obvious location. We want to be able to take the user's
settings and save them so that we can set them up as defaults for the
next printer selection and, in some cases, to only allow certain
values (i.e. for some application all output to Continuous printer
must be "no page break").

Any comments gratefully received...

graham
Olympic Computers (UK)

## Thread: VISOC — 2 msgs

### VISOC

- **From:** "Michel Merlin" <michel.merlin@skynet.be>
- **Date:** 2001-10-07T20:02:12+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3bc098a7$0$36466$ba620e4c@news.skynet.be>`

```
Where can I find the COBOL program called VISOC?
Any help will be appreciated. Thks in advance.
Michael
```

#### ↳ Re: VISOC

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-07T17:57:54-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9pqmr3$q2m$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3bc098a7$0$36466$ba620e4c@news.skynet.be>`

```
VisOC - was the first "object oriented" COBOL *compiler* from Micro Focus -
MANY years ago.  That product was replaced by NetExpress - which is now at
Version 3.1.  If you want to purchase either the "full" NetExpress product -
or look into its academic program, check out

   www.microfocus.com

If it isn't clear - VisOC is not itself a "COBOL program" - it is a
compiler.  (Well MUCH of the product is written in COBOL, but that is
another thread).

If you REALLY need that OLD product itself, you might try ebay or something
like that - but I don't know how many people still have copies - and have
copies that they would want to sell.
```

#### ↳ Re: What do these cobol types look like?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-09T00:39:11-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9pu2p9$e87$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3BC1E9B1.4EB9F8E7@yahoo.com>`

```
You don't tell us your platform, compiler, or more importantly WHY you are
asking this question.  If this is a "homework" assignment, then I suggest
that you check the references that you are using for your class.  If this is
to "inter-act" with a program in another programming language, then your
question probably won't get you the information that you need/want.
Definitely some of the forms (DBCS and internal/external floating point) are
VERY compiler dependent today.

HOWEVER, just to give you the "absolutely" correct answer (for the NEXT
COBOL Standard),  I thought I would give you the full answer - that probably
won't answer your question at all:

To define an item as alphanumeric-edited, character-string-1 shall include
- at least one symbol 'A' or one symbol 'X', and
- at least one of the symbols from the set 'B', '0', '/'.

To define an item as numeric-edited, one of the following options shall be
specified:
a) To produce a fixed-point edited result, character-string-1 shall include:
- at least one symbol 'Z'; or
- at least one symbol '*'; or
- at least two identical symbols from the set '+', '-', currency symbol; or
- at least one symbol '9' and at least one of the symbols from the set 'B',
'CR', 'DB', '0', '/', ',',
'.', '+', '-', the currency symbol.
b) To produce a floating-point edited result <external floating point>,
characters-string-1 shall consist of two parts, separated without any
spaces, by the symbol 'E'. The first part represents the significand; the
second part represents the exponent.
The significand shall be a valid character-string for either a numeric item
or a numeric-edited item for a
fixed-point result.
The exponent shall be '+9', '+99', '+999', or '+9(n)' where n = 1, 2, or 3.

To define an item as national <DBCS>, character-string-1 shall contain only
one or more occurrences of the symbol 'N'.

The usages float-short, float-long and float-extended define signed numeric
data items that are held in a floating-point format suitable for the machine
on which the runtime module is to run <internal floating point>. The size
and permitted range of values for these fields is defined by the
implementor. Any value that may be held in a data item of usage float-short
shall also be expressible in a data item of usage float-long. Any value that
may be held in a data item of usage float-long shall also be expressible in
a data item of usage float-extended.

  ***

From the terminology used ("internal/external floating point" and "DBCS") it
wouldn't surprise me if this question has to do with an IBM or
IBM-compatible environment.  If  so, you can find their definitions of such
terms at:

http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igylr205/5.3.11.3
    and
http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igylr205/5.3.16.1
```

#### ↳ Re: What do these cobol types look like?

- **From:** Charles <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-15T16:52:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VuEy7.29836$ev2.36524@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<3BC1E9B1.4EB9F8E7@yahoo.com>`

```
On Mon, 08 Oct 2001 14:00:18 -0400, in article <3BC1E9B1.4EB9F8E7@yahoo.com>,
Steve stated 
>
>Can anyone give some example for each of those cobol types?

Hope this helps a little Steve.....

>COBOLAlphaNumericEditedType pic xxxbxxbxxxx 
(adds blanks for ssn convert to '-' later with inspect verb)

>COBOLNumericEditedType      pic zz9          (shows one digit for 009)

>COBOLDBCSType               Never used and am clueless what it is used for.
(Double Byte Character Data)

99v99 is a fixed decimal point these below are not fixed and will not line up
necessarily.  I have never used them and do not know what they look when
displayed.

>COBOLExternalFloatType (I have not used)  
>
This may be referring to the floating dollar sign $$$$$9.99
>Thanks!
>
Technical manuals and school books are introducing new terms.
In my manual, External Floating-Point refers to not being in a fixed format.
05  Result   pic -9v9(9)E-99.  (no value clause allowed)
I am guessing this means the decimal point can be anywhere, but there is a
decimal point and it has 9 digits.  


>COBOLInternalFloatType     ( I have never used this)

Internal floating point is for comp-1 or comp-2

A picture clause is not allowed.

05 Result            Usage Comp-1 Value 06.23E-24

This is like 06.23 X 10^-24
or something like:
".00000000000000000000000623";
that is a mouth full.

As a Note, COMP is defined by the compiler, and many compilers treat how it is
applied differently.  I use the IBM Mainframe COBOL LE for VSE/ESA compiler.
```

Gerald Goebel <gerald@wagors.de> wrote in message news:<3BC24742.147EA519@wagors.de>...

Hi, Gerald:

> Hi list, hi Dean,
> 
> 
> looks fine, but ican't find the source, want to try it on linux.
> 


The source is bundled in with the setup program.  When you run setup,
it should unpack the source along with it.  You should find it in the
same directory into which you have installed the editor; it's a single
file that has the extension '.pac'

If anyone who is working in a non-Microsoft environment would like to
take a look at the source, I'd be more than happy to e-mail it, or I
can make it available from Clockwork's website.

I'd also be interested in hearing any feedback from anyone who has
tried to use Sourcerer for COBOL.  Problems, comments, praises, or
(useful <g>) suggestions for future enhancements are always welcome.

I have not yet decided if it's worth it to port this to Linux...I'd be
willing to do this if there's anough interest from the Linux
community.  Again, input is always welcome.

Regards,
Dean Powell
```
