<a id="t-6f09184e5a"></a>
## Thread: Having problems with NUMVAL function — 20 msgs

### Having problems with NUMVAL function

- **From:** ellie.odonnell.iei8@statefarm.com (Ellie O'Donnell)
- **Date:** 2001-10-09T06:44:27-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6eb5dfdb.0110090544.233ba662@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,

Since all attributes, even money, are PIC X(50) fields, I've had to
convert from alphanumeric to numeric in order to work with monetary
amounts.

I decided to use the NUMVAL function to do this and have had varying
success. First I was testing it using a decimal point and it worked
fine. However, the way our screens are set up, there won't be decimal
point when value is sent to me. Even though the NUMVAL reference
material states that a decimal point isn't needed, it won't convert
the value without it. So fine, we worked around that. Now I've been
sent a record with AFAFAFAF in the field. My code is still inserting
the decimal point in the correct spot, but NUMVAL won't convert it
because they're alpha characters. Even though the reference states it
should convert any alphanumeric character to its numeric equivalent.

Does anyone know a way around this?

I'm using OS/390. I noticed that the refernce documentation listed
OS/370. I don't know if this means NUMVAL won't work for OS/390 or
not.

Thanks,
Ellie
```

#### ↳ Re: Having problems with NUMVAL function

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-09T10:18:03-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9pv4gk$le7$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<6eb5dfdb.0110090544.233ba662@posting.google.com>`

```
Good news and bad news -

Good news - The current IBM COBOL compiler *definitely* allows for input
with and without a decimal point.  What results were you getting when using
input without it?  This may be a "product error" in your service level and
you should contact IBM support if you are still having problems with this.

Bad news - Any ANSI conforming COBOL compiler (which includes the IBM
compiler) is allowed to "choke" on invalid input.  This includes fields that
have alphabet characters in it. Therefore, you need to "pre-check" your
input data before using the NUMVAL intrinsic function.

The exact rules (what you need to check for) for input to the NUMVAL
intrinsic function are found at:
 http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igylr205/7.1.30?

You need to look at the syntax diagram and figure out exactly what
combination of sign, space, digit, and decimal points are allowed.

P.S. If you are a member of SHARE or work with your shop's "system
programmer" who knows how to submit a "REQUEST" to IBM, you might want to
ask for "early" support of the TEST-NUMVAL intrinsic function which is
currently scheduled to be included in the next COBOL Standard.  This will do
the "checking" for you.
```

#### ↳ Re: Having problems with NUMVAL function

- **From:** Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-10-09T11:10:50-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BC32189.94D9F7AF@mb.sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<6eb5dfdb.0110090544.233ba662@posting.google.com>`

```
Ellie O'Donnell wrote:

> Hi,
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I don't know if this would still work, but - we used to test fields for
numeric by moving them to a 999's field, all but the "sign" position,
then compare the results;  i.e.,

01 is-it-num.
    05 first-9 pic x(9).
    05 sign-pos pic x.

01 9-digits pic 9(9) comp-3.   (i.e., "packed")
01 9-digits-unpacked pic 9(9).

...

move first-9 to 9-digits
move 9-digits to 9-digits-unpacked
if first-9 = 9-digits-unpacked                -> first-9 is numeric.

The idea being that since the first move stripped all the zones and the
second plugged in valid numeric zones, then if the comparison worked the
original field must have had valid zones and so was numeric.  This worked
with EBCDIC; maybe not in ASCII.

Maybe this is just an historical anecdote!

PL
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Having problems with NUMVAL function

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-10-10T13:03:46+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3bc3929e_8@news.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<6eb5dfdb.0110090544.233ba662@posting.google.com> <3BC32189.94D9F7AF@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
Fascinating!

I found this very interesting and quite an ingenious solution to the problem
of testing if a field is numeric.

However, since COBOL has provided a facility to test the class of a field
(NUMERIC, ALPHABETIC, and ALPHANUMERIC) since COBOL '59, I have to ask why a
COBOL programmer would not simply say: "IF NUMERIC..."

Then memory jogged and I remembered that on IBM mainframes, external decimal
fields could have the sign as an alphabetic character (a zoned overpunch
from unit record days).  The COBOL Class test (in IBM environments)
recognises this and allows certain non-numeric characters to be interpreted
as signs. So if an alphabetic character just happened to appear in the input
by accident in the right place,  the field would still be considered
numeric...

We used to get around this by stating:

IF NUMERIC AND POSITIVE...

I believe the example Peter posted is designed to avoid the same problem. It
just involves more work and is less "COBOL like".

Bill recently requested code for testing NUMVAL input and this should take
care of just about anything including +, -, decimals etc.

As I wrote a component in Fujitsu PowerCOBOL to do this nearly a year ago, I
didn't respond to Bill's request. It is unlikely that I will use NUMVAL as
it is easier to simply invoke "String2num", which does the combined
functions of the proposed TEST-NUMVAL and NUMVAL itself.

You pass the component a string when you invoke its "checknum" Method, and
it sets a Boolean indicating whether the string is numeric or not. If it IS
numeric, it also sets around 8 different object properties which give you
the number in every conceivable representation, including PACKED, ZONED,
FLOATING POINT, BINARY, and with the integer and the decimal parts as
separate fields. Other properties tell you how many characters the field is,
and how many of these are after the decimal point.

The component has other methods which can notify the User via a GUI message
box that the input is invalid, and give some clues as to what is required.

I have it packaged as an ActiveX control, and simply drag and drop its icon
onto any forms I am developing which require the input of numeric data. I
haven't tried using it on a Web Page yet, but I expect it to work just like
any other component or Java Bean.

This covers the whole story on Numeric Validation as far as I am concerned.

It also demonstrates what I have been saying for some time now about the
advent of components. You focus on a problem, solve it ONCE, and that's
it... You never need to worry about it again.

Object Oriented COBOL facilitates this, and that's the main reason I use it.

I have a couple of similar components that do EVERYTHING with dates
(including validation and computation). One of them is used for inputting
dates. It offers a choice between keyboard or mouse whenever the user moves
to a DATE input field, then remembers what was chosen. Any subsequent fields
will pop up a calendar, so dates can be entered by point-and-click if that
was the user's choice, or it allows DDMM to be keyed, (with a default to the
current year that can be overridden) if quick keying is desired. All the
problems regarding different date formats are taken care of. Dates are
displayed on screen as DD-MMM-YYYY (in the user's system  selected language)
but are available internally in any of 7 different formats, including the 64
bit floating point format used by Microsoft.

This trend of isolating and encapsulating functionality so you don't need to
worry about it any more is like providing extensions to the Language. I
don't think in terms of NUMVAL, or numeric validation, or what format I need
to have a number in; I simply think "String2num" and know that all my
requirements are met, every time.

Furthermore I know that ONE copy of this code will be used across my entire
system (it is a registered component) and if I ever need to extend it, I can
do so in ONE place, with ONE set of source code.

It goes way beyond User Functions.

If anyone would like more information and/or a copy of the components
described, mail me privately. (For non-Fujitsu Users, it is a fairly large
download, as the Fujitsu RTS has to be included...it still works.)

Pete.

news:3BC32189.94D9F7AF@mb.sympatico.ca...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Having problems with NUMVAL function

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-09T19:20:23-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9q04b8$84h$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<6eb5dfdb.0110090544.233ba662@posting.google.com> <3BC32189.94D9F7AF@mb.sympatico.ca> <3bc3929e_8@news.newsgroups.com>`

```
Of course NUMVAL has been around since '89 - but there are a couple of
"nice" things that it does that Pete's routine may (or may not) do - but
NumVal does it in a "portable" way.

A) it allows any number of spaces at the beginning or end of the field.
This is "handy" when dealing with user input that hasn't already been
"normalized".

B) It handles + - CR and DB - with + and - allowed at either end of the
input (again handy if you have "unreliable" user input that you haven't
already normalized)

C) it "justifies" the decimal point for you.  You don't need to know how
many (if any) digits left/right of the decimal point their are in the input.

  ***

Having said all of this - the ORIGINAL "draft" Intrinsic Function module had
an (awful) solution to invalid input.  ANSI/ISO "removed" this and promised
a "quick" solution to the problem.  It should NOW be available in the
Standard - by the end of 2002 <G> or sigh
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Having problems with NUMVAL function

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-10-10T13:37:18+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3bc398ca_2@news.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<6eb5dfdb.0110090544.233ba662@posting.google.com> <3BC32189.94D9F7AF@mb.sympatico.ca> <3bc3929e_8@news.newsgroups.com> <9q04b8$84h$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Just to reassure you, Bill, "String2num" does ALL of the things stated and
more besides...(like checking for "numeric" in different number bases, user
specified, but default is DECIMAL. The latest version supports HEX, OCTAL,
and BINARY).

As for being "portable"... The component can run on ANY platform that
supports COM or CORBA.  (That's Windows or Unix). Because it is intended for
a GUI environment it is not runnable on mainframes. (is anybody STILL using
that stuff  <G>?)...

Pete.
```

```
Pete (and others),
  From the "little knowledge is a dangerous thing" category - as far as IBM
mainframes go, you might want to start at:
  http://www-4.ibm.com/software/webservers/appserv/zos_os390/

I understand just about enough on this page to think it PROBABLY includes a
reference to the answer you asked.

I find on that page,
  "WebSphere Application Server V4.0.1 for z/OS and OS/390 now includes
support for the following:

 - Java Messaging Service (JMS), JavaMail, and Client Container
 - Web services equivalent to those in the Version 4 distributed family of
servers
 - Web services with SOAP and UDDI open standards support Servlets,
JavaServer Pages (JSPs)T, and Enterprise JavaBeans (EJBs)T in compliance
with J2EE specifications"

P.S.  If you go "up" a few levels from that URL to
 http://www-4.ibm.com/software/webservers/
there is even a link to:
  "Announcement:
WebSphere Business Components: Making EJB development easier with V1.2.1"
 as I know that "components" are always of interest to you.
```

```
and Numval-C set the EC-INCOMPATIBLE-DATA exception rather than relying on a two
step test then process method.  One of the first things I will do when I have a
conforming compiler is make my own function - NUMVAL-TAH that does the test then
if successful does the actual numval.
```

#### ↳ Re: Having problems with NUMVAL function

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2001-10-09T17:30:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20011009133053.14725.00000612@nso-fl.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<6eb5dfdb.0110090544.233ba662@posting.google.com>`

```
In article <6eb5dfdb.0110090544.233ba662@posting.google.com>,
ellie.odonnell.iei8@statefarm.com (Ellie O'Donnell) writes:

>ent a record with AFAFAFAF in the field. My code is still inserting
>the decimal point in the correct spot, but NUMVAL won't convert it
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I've used NUMVAL successfully in the OS390 environment, but it definitely
DOES NOT deal with converting alpha characters to numeric.
The intent (as far as I was able to validate) is to aide in processing a
numeric-edited field (alpha-numeric) back to a true numeric field for use in
arithmetic operations.  I never encountered a situation that 'forced' me to
supply a  period/decimal in the input string.
Most of my use came from translating variable position input fields
from a card deck and translating the fields into offsets/lengths for extracting
data from a client record or generating report detail content.
```

```
FYI - from private email, the "decimal point problem" really was an
"application" problem - not a NUMVAL problem.  What the programmer WANTED
was for an input field with

  "005000"
to "auto-magically" be treated as
  "0050.00" rather than
  "005000."

This is definitely NOT the way that NUMVAL (or NUMVAL-C) will/should work.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Having problems with NUMVAL function

- **From:** Charles <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-12T16:20:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<YKEx7.26252$ev2.34846@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<6eb5dfdb.0110090544.233ba662@posting.google.com> <wjJw7.866415$ai2.67094489@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
On Tue, 09 Oct 2001 20:43:08 GMT, in article
<wjJw7.866415$ai2.67094489@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>, JerryP stated 
>
>There was a discussion on this ng a couple of weeks ago about a
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I was looking up in an old System/370 Ref Summary and "AF" is suppose to be a
big dot like you see on print files between data items.  You could always go
back to basics and process the field as a table with subscripting and put only
the numbers in a tempory field by themselves.  Then the only thing left is a
decimal point problem.  Another idea is just write an INSPECT statement, and
replace all non-numeric leading and trailing items with spaces or something like
that.  If it is a text field, you should eiter have trailing or following non
numeric characters.  Varying with reference modification might work also.

If you just counted where the first and last numeric location was in a field,
you might be able to move just that part or do numval on just that part with
reference modification.

As a note, I have always thought forcing a user to enter a numeric value and
storing it as a numeric value is a better option.  It is far easier to prevent
problems through intelligent design than to come up with a way to fix them.  
The user will always enter garbage at one time or another in any field you give
them access to.

Afterthought is  Hell!!!
```

```
Charles  <nospam@newsranger.com> wrote:

[snippage]

>The user will always enter garbage at one time or another in any field you give
>them access to.

As was told the class by The Respected and Honored Mr Mullen, my first
programming instructor:

'What is the definition of a computer user?'

(various answers)

'Wrong.  A computer user is one who, when asked to give a 'Y' or 'N'
response, will key in the numeral '7'.  Write all of your online
applications with this in mind and your users will love you.'

DD
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: I'm searching a validation procedure for the date/month & year

- **From:** Charles <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-09T16:44:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zPFw7.21746$ev2.30284@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<%lEw7.22827$Hw5.1804333@weber.videotron.net> <yrEw7.8734$ym4.374917@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
This is not an easy task.

I did this for a JAVA class and we used Rule Based Design Methods to identify
all of the rules and then designed the code to cover every single rule.
Just be aware that there are special rules for Leap years and century years.

I recommend first ruling out the obvious.

1. A day of the month must be between 1 and 31.
2. A Month must be between 1 and 12.
3. A year mut be between (Use your own rule!).

In some instances you may want all known dates, but it doesn't seem likely.
Using 3 rules like this eliminates all of the obvious errors only the not so
obvious errors like 31 days in February can get through.  The hard part is the
difference in days of the month.  February requires special processing for leap
year and century years.

Another way to do this is to load a system dictionary with all known possible
dates for a given year.  That way you can look the possible date up without
having to test it, and load all possible dates into a table to test in memory.

Of course we went further than this.  We designed a way to find the next day,
the previous day, and then X days in the past or present, and then the number of
days between 2 dates.

There is probably some obvious way to test this.  Like saving the system date
and attempting to change it and looking for the error code in assembly as a
called routine.  Or converting a date to julian and looking for failure.  I
don't know what would be the result if you looked for the julian date of a given
date and it was invalid to start with. It may just return all zeroes.

--------------------------
>Dark Shade <Deathunthot@hotmail.com> wrote:
>>I'm searching a validation procedure for the date/month & year.
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: I'm searching a validation procedure for the date/month & year

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@paralynx.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-09T10:34:30-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BC33526.22C7@paralynx.com>`

```
Charles wrote:

> There is probably some obvious way to test this.  Like saving the system date
> and attempting to change it and looking for the error code in assembly as a
> called routine.  Or converting a date to julian and looking for failure.  I
> don't know what would be the result if you looked for the julian date of a given
> date and it was invalid to start with. It may just return all zeroes.

If you have decent julian routines you could convert TO julian, then convert the result 
back FROM julian and compare against what you started with.  If they are the same, it's 
valid.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: I'm searching a validation procedure for the date/month & year

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2001-10-09T23:31:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20011009193156.28076.00000993@nso-da.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<zPFw7.21746$ev2.30284@www.newsranger.com>`

```
In article <zPFw7.21746$ev2.30284@www.newsranger.com>, Charles
<nospam@newsranger.com> writes:

>
>Another way to do this is to load a system dictionary with all known possible
>dates for a given year.  That way you can look the possible date up without
>having to test it, and load all possible dates into a table to test in
>memory.

  You should only need a table of 12 entries for the standards days per month.
  Validate the year range, determine leap year, validate month range, test days
> zero and <= days-per-month(month) or leap-year and month=02 and days=29.


>
>Of course we went further than this.  We designed a way to find the next day,
>the previous day, and then X days in the past or present, and then the number
>of
>days between 2 dates.

  tomorrow = function date-of-integer(function integer-of-date(today) + 1)
  yesterday = function date-of-integer(function integer-of-date(today) - 1)
  days-diff = function integer-of-date(most recent) - 
                          function integer-of-date(oldest )
```

#### ↳ Re: I'm searching a validation procedure for the date/month & year

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-09T12:57:12-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9pvdrb$3hl$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<%lEw7.22827$Hw5.1804333@weber.videotron.net>`

```
You don't tell us your operating system or compiler - but if you are running
on IBM OS/390 (with LE) or Micro Focus COBOL, then you can use the LE
callable service CEEDAYS - see:
 http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/ceea3110/3.5.24?

This lets you use a "mask" to tell what type of date format you have as
input (e.g. MM/DD/YY - CCYY-MM-DD - Mmm dd, CCYY - etc)

If you look at that URL and search for "The following Feedback Codes can
result from this service: "

it will show you the type of validation errors that it will detect.
```

#### ↳ Re: I'm searching a validation procedure for the date/month & year

- **From:** Charles <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-10T19:01:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%V0x7.23504$ev2.32120@www.newsranger.com>`

```
On Tue, 9 Oct 2001 11:08:01 -0300, in article
<%lEw7.22827$Hw5.1804333@weber.videotron.net>, Dark Shade stated 
>
>I'm searching a validation procedure for the date/month & year.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
If this was for a School Project, I would say that the object is to see if the
student can use a proper If,Else if,Else or evalute structure to accomplish the
task in a logical manner that covers all of the possiblities.  If that was the
case, it would be best to just figure it out for yourself.  Take this one step
at a time, and subdivide it up into separate things to test for, and then just
write the code for what you want to test for.  

I will give you a hint;  most months have either 30 or 31 days and you only need
one of 2 tests for the days of the month for 11 different months.
Months 1,3,5,7,8,10,12 have 1-31 days and months 4,6,9,11 have 1-30 days.  That
eliminates the test for 11 months with only 2 tests to code for.
```

#### ↳ Re: I'm searching a validation procedure for the date/month & year

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-11T17:19:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Fwkx7.24763$ev2.33682@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<%lEw7.22827$Hw5.1804333@weber.videotron.net>`

```
Hi DS,

The only part of the verification process that presents a challenge is
determining the # of days for a given month.

I've enclosed 2 pieces of code that should help. The 1st is a table that
contains, among other things, the # of days in each month. The 2nd is a routine
to recognize a leap year. This is used to modify the Feb days (add 1) in a leap
year. 

Regards, Jack.

CODE SET #1

*===============================================================
*==================    END OF MONTH TABLE    ===================
*===============================================================
*    NOTE: THIS IS A GENERALIZED TABLE TO BE USED FOR DATE     *
*          MANIPULATION. EACH ENTRY CONTAINS THE NAME OF THE   *
*          MONTH AND THE NUMBER OF DAYS IN EACH MONTH AND ITS  *
*          IMMEDIATE NEIGHBORS. THE JAN-PREV-EOM FIELD CON-    *
*          TAINS THE NUMBER OF DAYS IN THE PREVIOUS DECEMBER.  *
*          THE DEC-NEXT-EOM FIELD CONTAINS THE NUMBER OF DAYS  *
*          IN THE NEXT JANUARY.                                *
*
*          IN LEAP YEARS THE USER MUST INCREMENT THE FOLLOWING *
*          FIELDS BY ONE:                                      *
*                         JAN-NEXT-EOM-VAL                     *
*                         FEB-EOM-VAL                          *
*                         MAR-PREV-EOM-VAL                     *
*===============================================================

01  END-OF-MONTH-VALUES.
*========================
05  JAN-VALUES.
10  JAN-NAME                PIC X(009) VALUE
'JANUARY'.
10  JAN-EOM-VAL             PIC 9(002) VALUE 31.
10  JAN-PREV-EOM-VAL        PIC 9(002) VALUE 31.
10  JAN-NEXT-EOM-VAL        PIC 9(002) VALUE 28.
05  FEB-VALUES.
10  FEB-NAME                PIC X(009) VALUE
'FEBRUARY'.
10  FEB-EOM-VAL             PIC 9(002) VALUE 28.
10  FEB-PREV-EOM-VAL        PIC 9(002) VALUE 31.
10  FEB-NEXT-EOM-VAL        PIC 9(002) VALUE 31.
05  MAR-VALUES.
10  MAR-NAME                PIC X(009) VALUE
'MARCH'.
10  MAR-EOM-VAL             PIC 9(002) VALUE 31.
10  MAR-PREV-EOM-VAL        PIC 9(002) VALUE 28.
10  MAR-NEXT-EOM-VAL        PIC 9(002) VALUE 30.
05  APR-VALUES.
10  APR-NAME                PIC X(009) VALUE
'APRIL'.
10  APR-EOM-VAL             PIC 9(002) VALUE 30.
10  APR-PREV-EOM-VAL        PIC 9(002) VALUE 31.
10  APR-NEXT-EOM-VAL        PIC 9(002) VALUE 31.
05  MAY-VALUES.
10  MAY-NAME                PIC X(009) VALUE
'MAY'.
10  MAY-EOM-VAL             PIC 9(002) VALUE 31.
10  MAY-PREV-EOM-VAL        PIC 9(002) VALUE 30.
10  MAY-NEXT-EOM-VAL        PIC 9(002) VALUE 30.
05  JUN-VALUES.
10  JUN-NAME                PIC X(009) VALUE
'JUNE'.
10  JUN-EOM-VAL             PIC 9(002) VALUE 30.
10  JUN-PREV-EOM-VAL        PIC 9(002) VALUE 31.
10  JUN-NEXT-EOM-VAL        PIC 9(002) VALUE 31.
05  JUL-VALUES.
10  JUL-NAME                PIC X(009) VALUE
'JULY'.
10  JUL-EOM-VAL             PIC 9(002) VALUE 31.
10  JUL-PREV-EOM-VAL        PIC 9(002) VALUE 30.
10  JUL-NEXT-EOM-VAL        PIC 9(002) VALUE 31.
05  AUG-VALUES.
10  AUG-NAME                PIC X(009) VALUE
'AUGUST'.
10  AUG-EOM-VAL             PIC 9(002) VALUE 31.
10  AUG-PREV-EOM-VAL        PIC 9(002) VALUE 31.
10  AUG-NEXT-EOM-VAL        PIC 9(002) VALUE 30.
05  SEP-VALUES.
10  SEP-NAME                PIC X(009) VALUE
'SEPTEMBER'.
10  SEP-EOM-VAL             PIC 9(002) VALUE 30.
10  SEP-PREV-EOM-VAL        PIC 9(002) VALUE 31.
10  SEP-NEXT-EOM-VAL        PIC 9(002) VALUE 31.
05  OCT-VALUES.
10  OCT-NAME                PIC X(009) VALUE
'OCTOBER'.
10  OCT-EOM-VAL             PIC 9(002) VALUE 31.
10  OCT-PREV-EOM-VAL        PIC 9(002) VALUE 30.
10  OCT-NEXT-EOM-VAL        PIC 9(002) VALUE 30.
05  NOV-VALUES.
10  NOV-NAME                PIC X(009) VALUE
'NOVEMBER'.
10  NOV-EOM-VAL             PIC 9(002) VALUE 30.
10  NOV-PREV-EOM-VAL        PIC 9(002) VALUE 31.
10  NOV-NEXT-EOM-VAL        PIC 9(002) VALUE 31.
05  DEC-VALUES.
10  DEC-NAME                PIC X(009) VALUE
'DECEMBER'.
10  DEC-EOM-VAL             PIC 9(002) VALUE 31.
10  DEC-PREV-EOM-VAL        PIC 9(002) VALUE 30.
10  DEC-NEXT-EOM-VAL        PIC 9(002) VALUE 31.

01  EOM-TABLE REDEFINES
END-OF-MONTH-VALUES.
05  ET-MONTHLY-ENTRY     OCCURS 012  TIMES.
10  ET-MONTH-NAME           PIC X(009).
10  ET-EOM                  PIC 9(002).
10  ET-PREV-EOM             PIC 9(002).
10  ET-NEXT-EOM             PIC 9(002).


CODE SET #2

05  WS-LEAP-YR-IND             PIC  X(01).
88  ITS-A-LEAP-YR                     VALUE 'L'.
88  ITS-NOT-A-LEAP-YR            VALUE 'N'.


05  WS-DST-START-LONG.
10  WDS-CCYY.
20  WDS-CC                      PIC 9(02) VALUE 19.
20  WDS-YY                     PIC 9(02).
10  WDS-CCYY-NUM REDEFINES
WDS-CCYY                             PIC S9(04).
10  WDS-MM                    PIC 9(02). 
10  WDS-DD                     PIC 9(02)..


05  WS-REMAINDERS.
10  WR-REMAINDER-4           PIC S9(01) COMP.
88  DIVISIBLE-BY-4                      VALUE +0.
10  WR-REMAINDER-100       PIC S9(02) COMP.
88  DIVISIBLE-BY-100                  VALUE +0.
10  WR-REMAINDER-400       PIC S9(03) COMP.
88  DIVISIBLE-BY-400                  VALUE +0.


******************************************************************
*  DETERMINE IF ITS A LEAP YEAR
******************************************************************
SET     ITS-NOT-A-LEAP-YR    TO  TRUE
DIVIDE  WDS-CCYY-NUM         BY  +4
GIVING  WS-BIT-BUCKET
REMAINDER WS-REMAINDER-4

DIVIDE  WDS-CCYY-NUM         BY  +100
GIVING  WS-BIT-BUCKET
REMAINDER WS-REMAINDER-100

DIVIDE  WDS-CCYY-NUM         BY  +400
GIVING  WS-BIT-BUCKET
REMAINDER WS-REMAINDER-400

IF DIVISIBLE-BY-400
OR
(DIVISIBLE-BY-4 AND NOT DIVISIBLE-BY-100)
SET ITS-A-LEAP-YR    TO TRUE
END-IF
******************************************************************
```

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>

```
Bravo Jack!

This is only the second time in 30 years that I have seen someone code the
determination of a leap year correctly. (The other was a programmer named
Bernie Altenbach who did so in Spain about 18 years ago...)

Sadly, for most people it is simply:

DIVIDE 4 INTO WS-YEAR REMAINDER MAYBE-LEAP-YEAR

(MAYBE-LEAP-YEAR has an 88 equating a value of zero to "ITS-LEAP-YEAR")

Newbies, PLEASE take note!

Pete.

"John H Sleight Jr" <nospam@newsranger.com> wrote in message
```

```
Hi Binyamin,

While I do agree with the "how far do we go with this" attitude, I'm sure the
same attitude was prevalent in the Sixties, and there we (the industry) were 40
yrs later spending billions to correct the flaw. Surely, the lesson learned from
that, should prompt a different answer today.  

Forty or 98 yrs - not that much difference in the great scheme of things. Forty
years! Who wudda thunk! And that was COBOL, a language frowned upon and sneered
at by some. Can you imagine when all that code is rewritten in a REAL language.
Why, the code may stay around for 98 years! And we'll be glad we took the few
extra minutes to do it right.  

Regards, Jack.
```

- **References:** `<%lEw7.22827$Hw5.1804333@weber.videotron.net> <Fwkx7.24763$ev2.33682@www.newsranger.com> <3bc6454d_7@news.newsgroups.com> <3m7jst8gn4k2fcvug5vr0ungtg52etluej@4ax.com> <5kry7.29160$ev2.35810@www.newsranger.com>`

```
John H Sleight Jr <nospam@newsranger.com> wrote in message news:<5kry7.29160$ev2.35810@www.newsranger.com>...
> Hi Binyamin,
> 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Only to complete your posting....maybe well known but always true.

Sincerely - Stefan Meyer

There was once a COBOL programmer in the mid to late 1990s. For the
sake of this story, we'll call him Charlie.
 
After years of being treated as a technological dinosaur by all the UNIX
programmers and website developers, Charlie was finally getting some
respect. He'd become a private consultant specializing in Year 2000
conversions. He was working 70 and 80 and even 90 hour weeks, but it
was worth it.

Several years of this had taken its toll on Charlie. He had problems
sleeping and began having anxiety dreams about the Year 2000. All he
could think about was how he could avoid the year 2000 and all that
came with it.

Charlie made a deal to have himself frozen until March 15th, 2000. The
next thing he would know is he'd wake up in the year 2000; after the
New Year celebrations and computer debacles; after the leap day.

He was put into his cryogenic receptacle, the technicians set the revive
date, and that was that.

The next thing that Charlie saw was an enormous room filled with
people. They were all shouting "I can't believe it!" and "It's a
miracle" and "He's alive!".

Charlie couldn't contain his enthusiasm. "It is over?" he asked. "Is
2000 already here? Are all the millennial parties and promotions and
crises all over and done with?"

A spokesman explained that there had been a problem with the programming
of the timer on Charlie's cryogenic receptacle, it hadn't been year 2000
compliant. It was actually eight thousand years later, not the year 2000.
But the spokesman told Charlie that he shouldn't get excited; someone
important wanted to speak to him.

Suddenly a wall-sized 3D hologram displayed the image of the Prime
Minister of Earth.  He told Charlie not to be upset. That this was a
wonderful time to be alive. That there was world peace and no more
starvation, there were colonies on all the planets. That technology had
advanced to such a degree that everyone had virtual reality interfaces
which allowed them to contact anyone else in the solar system, or to
watch any entertainment, or to hear any music recorded anywhere.

"That sounds terrific," said Charlie. "But I'm curious. Why is everybody
so interested in me?"

"Well," said the Prime Minister. "The year 10,000 is just around the
corner, and it says in your files that you know COBOL".
```

```
Comments below, Ben...

"Binyamin Dissen" <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote in message
news:3m7jst8gn4k2fcvug5vr0ungtg52etluej@4ax.com...
> On Fri, 12 Oct 2001 13:17:16 +1300 "Peter E. C. Dashwood"
> <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
> :>This is only the second time in 30 years that I have seen someone code
the
> :>determination of a leap year correctly. (The other was a programmer
named
> :>Bernie Altenbach who did so in Spain about 18 years ago...)
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Which is valid thru 2100.

Hmmm...I see what you are saying.
>
> Those that put in the century check as well (without the four century
check)
> failed in 2000.
>

Which supports the argument for doing it properly; with the 400 check.

> :>Newbies, PLEASE take note!
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> now?

I understand your pragmatic approach, I just don't agree with it <G> (Funny,
I usually LIKE pragmatic approaches...)

I guess the REAL decision is: How much extra effort do I need to provide
something that will be right for longer, but in either case is adequate for
this lifetime?

It has to be subjective. As the code has been published, it takes a cut and
paste. I would do it, maybe you wouldn't. Neither of us is wrong.

However, when it comes to people learning the language, I believe, we should
seek to instill sound habits and practices which involve thinking beyond the
immmediate requirements (I agree this is arguable; but that's my position on
it <G>). I guess my objection to "divide by 4" is that it is so OBVIOUS! If
newbies learn to immediately do what is obvious, without any further
thought, then I believe they have learned the "wrong" lesson...
>
> What about support for five digit years?
>

My own date routines are good for a little over 28000 years (including
Millennium compliance and leap years).

Now, you may argue that this is "overkill" but it took no more effort (just
using 32 bit numbers) and it really looks good in the Sales literature...<G>

Pete.
```

```
:>> What about support for five digit years?

:>My own date routines are good for a little over 28000 years (including
:>Millennium compliance and leap years).

:>Now, you may argue that this is "overkill" but it took no more effort (just
:>using 32 bit numbers) and it really looks good in the Sales literature...<G>

I was negotiating a IBM mainframe contract several years ago and the client
wanted guarantees that the code would properly handle dates up thru 2100. This
clause was removed when I showed that the format of the system clock was
limited to 2043 or so (this has changed since then) and I had no idea how the
hardware was going to change. As a side point I did not agree to the contract
(about six month fixed bid) since the client was not willing to agree on a
spec while insisting that they need not pay unless they were happy with the
results, while I insisted that I needed agreement on the spec (T&M) so that I
could know what was required to make them happy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: I'm searching a validation procedure for the date/month & year

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-10-16T07:04:22+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BCBCDE6.5782CE70@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<%lEw7.22827$Hw5.1804333@weber.videotron.net> <Fwkx7.24763$ev2.33682@www.newsranger.com> <3bc6454d_7@news.newsgroups.com> <3m7jst8gn4k2fcvug5vr0ungtg52etluej@4ax.com> <3bca6a15_3@news.newsgroups.com>`

```
Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote:
> 
> My own date routines are good for a little over 28000 years (including
> Millennium compliance and leap years).

That is unlikely to actually be true.  It is known that a further
adjustment will be required every 4000 years or so to keep the total
error to half a day or less, but variations in orbits mean that they
have not been able to define when exactly it should be between 3600 and
4000, that has been left for later generations to determine.

If you have catered for this 4000 year adjustment you have probably done
it wrong (it has not yet been defined), if you haven't then the
adjustment will break your code.
```

## Thread: [OT] Instead of DB2... Batch? Forward... Into the Past! — 23 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### [OT] Instead of DB2... Batch? Forward... Into the Past!

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ( NA)
- **Date:** 2001-10-09T14:08:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<RxDw7.8729$ym4.375005@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```


All right, gang... now it is time for *me* to ask for someone to Do My Job
for me.  I hope I've included enough information to make it less tedious.

What I have is a DB2 table... actually two tables but of almost identical
design.  Changing a few minor details in order to protect the identity of
my client... the tables contain detail-information about orders and
charges placed by customers.  The first table is for charges less than 14
days old; after two weeks a batch process shuffles stuff onto the
report-charge table (so named because it is used more for reports than
online queries.  Combined the tables contain about 3,000,000 rows.

The table(s) are ordered by are:

CUST_NO
ORDER_NO
BRANCH_CD
DEPT_CD
TOTAL_PRICE
RECORD_STATUS
REVERSAL_IND

... and now, of course, the client wants an online report where a range of
dates can be selected (up to one year) for a given BRANCH_CD/DEPT_CD
combination and then the count of customers (DISTINCT CUST_NO) and grand
total of prices (SUM TOTAL_PRICE) can be shown for a couple of different
RECORD_STATUSes.  REVERSAL_IND is to show that the order was reversed and
the TOTAL_PRICE is negative.

Data volume is... unpredictable, to say the least; a given
BRANCH_CD/DEPT_CD combo can have, for a year, a couple of hundred thousand
transactions... or none... or any number in-between.  Initial analysis
against the tables showed that for a larger-volume combo a query of:

SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CUST_NO), SUM(TOTAL_PRICE)
 INTO :WS-BILLED-NOTREV-CNT,                    
      :WS-BILLED-NOTREV-SUM                     
 FROM   BIGGERTBL                               
     WHERE SERVICE_DT BETWEEN :WS-FROM-DT AND WS-TO-DT           
       AND RECORD_STATUS = 'B'                  
       AND REVERSAL_CHG_IND ,= 'Y'

... took about twenty minutes to run.  Not wanting to face a Corner-Office
Idiot after he'd waited through such a response-time I had an idea which,
of course, violates all the rules of System Design: build another table
for a single application.  The new table would have 366 rows for each
BRANCH_CD/DEPT_CD combo (there are about 400 such combos, making for a
full table - initially - of a hair more than 146K rows), one for each day,
and have each row contain the various COUNTs and SUMs for the sundry
RECORD_STATUSes and REVERSAL_INDs.  That way a given day's data would be
available immediately and a range BETWEEN dates would involve a scan of a
much smaller table.

Are you still with me?  Since this NEWTBL would have to be created daily
(on any given day orders can be submitted for any given customer for any
given service-date so I cannot initially load history and then just add
new transactions) and the larger-volume queries, as shown above, can take
inordinate amounts of time I put together a program which would create a
temporary table for each BRANCH_CD/DEPT_CD combo so that SUMs would not
have to go against the larger table... in other words I would:

INSERT INTO TEMPTBL                               
    SELECT CUST_NO,                               
           SERVICE_DT,                            
           BRANCH_CD,                         
           DEPT_CD,                             
           TOTAL_PRICE,                           
           RECORD_STATUS,                         
           REVERSAL_CHG_IND                       
FROM   BIGGERTBL                                
WHERE BRANCH_CD = :WS-BRANCH-CD     
  AND DEPT_CD     = :WS-DEPT-CD         
  AND SERVICE_DT BETWEEN :WS-FROM-DT AND :WS-TO-DT
  AND RECORD_STATUS IN ('B', 'S')

... and then I would SELECT my various COUNTs and SUMs for each date
against this subset.

The only problem is... it *still* take too long; depending on system load
the job runs 9 - 13 hrs wall-time and burns up 3+hrs of CPU.  For a daily
process this is unacceptable.

So... whence go I from here?  My only alternative seems to be... Back to
Batch.  As I see it now I would use ol' IKJEFT01 to

SELECT CUST_NO,                                        
       SERVICE_DT,                                     
       BRANCH_CD,                                  
       DEPT_CD,                                      
       TOTAL_PRICE,                                    
       RECORD_STATUS,                                  
       REVERSAL_CHG_IND                                
FROM   BIGGERTBL                              
ORDER BY BRANCH_CD, DEPT_CD, SERVICE_DT, CUST_NO;

... and dump the results to tape.  (this takes a mere 10 minutes)  Next
would be to MERGE this with the same results from the SMALLERTBL and then
run the whole mess through a COBOL batch program to generate the NEWTBL
rows, with COUNTs and SUMs done in olde-style control-break fashion.  Just
to keep it fast I would probably write all the output to a flat file and
then finish the jobstream with another IKJEFT01 to DROP the current
NEWTBL, re-CREATE it and then LOAD DATA with a DSNUTILB.

Back in the Oldene Dayse my Drill Sergeant used a phrase to describe a
situation which was unpleasant for the active participant, unpleasant for
the passive participant and unpleasant for the observer... something which
met these criteria was called 'monkey dicked the dog'.  Creating a new
table, used by a single application, in this fashion - because the data
are of such volume and arrangement that SQL functions take too long -
strikes me as just this.  In the spirit of 'four-eye debugging' I present
this to the group, then, and ask for responses as time, inclination and
generosity permit.

(note: consider this to be the issuing of 'Good luck, ya poor bastard';
it, and its variants, now no longer need consume bandwidth)

Thanks much!

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: [OT] Instead of DB2... Batch? Forward... Into the Past!

- **From:** Thane Hubbell <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-09T19:08:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LWHw7.21960$ev2.30599@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<RxDw7.8729$ym4.375005@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
Well DD,

I hit such a beast where I work now and guess what?  I ended up extracting and
sorting and creating again from a good old COBOL program with a sort.  My
process takes about 1/100 of the time the QUERY takes.  However, might I suggest
an alternative for you to TRY that stays within the database world.

Create a VIEW of the original table based on your requirements and then add some
indexes to it.  Might work - might not.  Might slow the WHOLE ENTERPRISE Down
while the view is updated as records are inserted -- might be unnoticable.


In article <RxDw7.8729$ym4.375005@iad-read.news.verio.net>,   NA says...
```

```
NA
    Susan is on the right track!  When I was a DB2 DBA (hint) the indexes
were more important than the table sizes.  HOWEVER; after you make sure you
have the correct indexes, you MUST check the bind to make sure that the
indexes are being used (and correctly).  Also, please note that only unique
indexes should be used.

    Tom
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Instead of DB2... Batch? Forward... Into the Past!

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ( NA)
- **Date:** 2001-10-12T13:59:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dHCx7.9100$ym4.389802@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<RxDw7.8729$ym4.375005@iad-read.news.verio.net> <_Q1x7.23577$ev2.32503@www.newsranger.com> <Lfex7.8976$ym4.382572@iad-read.news.verio.net> <5tpx7.25255$ev2.34367@www.newsranger.com>`

```
In article <5tpx7.25255$ev2.34367@www.newsranger.com>,
Thane Hubbell  <nospam@newsranger.com> wrote:
>I live in the Oracle world.  I'm not sure if what I suggested is possible - with
>Oracle or DB2 -- but I was fairly certain one could add an index to a view.

According to the SQL Reference (DB2 for OS/390, Version 5, SC26-8966-00) a
view is created as a subselect:

CREATE VIEW view-name (column-name) AS subselect WITH buncha stuff

... and an index is created on a table:

CREATE (TYPE 1/2) (UNIQUE) INDEX index-name ON table-name.

>Maybe NOT!

Be *very* wary of *any* solution which begins with 'All ya gotta do is...'

> I'll investigate with my Oracle guru's tomorrow - any DB2ers here
>know about DB2 having this feature?

As noted above, thus speaketh the manual... but perhaps there's more out
there.

Thanks much, though!

DD

>
>
…[49 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

- **Message-ID:** `<aBJx7.26807$ev2.35415@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<RxDw7.8729$ym4.375005@iad-read.news.verio.net> <_Q1x7.23577$ev2.32503@www.newsranger.com> <Lfex7.8976$ym4.382572@iad-read.news.verio.net> <5tpx7.25255$ev2.34367@www.newsranger.com> <dHCx7.9100$ym4.389802@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
Well... the Oracle people here say the same thing.  But...

They say you can create an index on the combination of colums.  HOWEVER to make
sure that index is USED you must specify the columns in the SAME order as the
index is defined in your where clause - else it will not be used.  So there may
yet be hope.  Can you see if the same applies to DB2?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Instead of DB2... Batch? Forward... Into the Past!

- **From:** "Rick Price" <rick@hpdsoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-12T09:31:54+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9q69m5$lvagn$1@ID-39799.news.dfncis.de>`
- **References:** `<RxDw7.8729$ym4.375005@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3BC35517.1F3C5726@boeing.com> <VT_w7.8842$ym4.379758@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```

" NA" <docdwarf@clark.net> wrote in message
news:VT_w7.8842$ym4.379758@iad-read.news.verio.net...
> In article <3BC35517.1F3C5726@boeing.com>,
> Susan A Allen  <susan.a.allen@boeing.com> wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> All right, gang... now it is time for *me* to ask for someone to Do My
Job
> >> for me.  I hope I've included enough information to make it less
tedious.
> >>
> >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> DEPT_CD but I need access by these two + SERVICE_DT.

If  there is a secondary index on branch and dept then,  AFAIK, the query
should make use of it even though you have additional criteria.  Your
original sample SELECT COUNT doesn't actually do a selection on branch and
dept.  Is this just an oversight in the sample?  If you are using branch and
dept criteria, maybe you need to check if the query is using this available
index, and if its not, try to find out why not.

How long does it take to retrieve all the data where the only criteria are
branch and dept?  I don't actually know anything about DB2 (nor much about
RDBMS) but is it possible that this index is set up so that it is only
updated when used?  Probably not possible, but I know that on the AS400, it
is possible to set up alternate key paths that are not updated dynamically
but only when the data is used.

Have you got a test DB so you can test with different criteria?  I know this
is a totally different  set up and size problem, but we recently had a query
that was taking 20 seconds (shock horror) on a SQL Server data base.  For
the amount of data, this was too long.  We discovered that it wasn't using
an available index.  We played around with the query,  including trying
different nonce criteria (e.g DATA = %) and finally got it to use the index.
The query then took 2 seconds.

>
> >I have built indexes to speed up processing (& it can make a huge
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Instead of DB2... Batch? Forward... Into the Past!

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ( NA)
- **Date:** 2001-10-15T13:15:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3kBy7.9298$ym4.404013@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<RxDw7.8729$ym4.375005@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3BC35517.1F3C5726@boeing.com> <VT_w7.8842$ym4.379758@iad-read.news.verio.net> <9q69m5$lvagn$1@ID-39799.news.dfncis.de>`

```
In article <9q69m5$lvagn$1@ID-39799.news.dfncis.de>,
Rick Price <rick@hpdsoftware.com> wrote:
>
>" NA" <docdwarf@clark.net> wrote in message
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>index, and if its not, try to find out why not.

Well, even if it is using the index it takes too long.  For a sample from
one of the larger Branch/Dept combos (100K+ rows covering 166 days) the
whole process - create TEMPTBL, INSERT into TEMPTBL the SELECTs of rows
from both main tbls, process TEMPTBL with four SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT) and
SUMs per day (only DISPLAYing results), DROPing the TEMPTBL and
re-creating it for the next combo - took 7 min, 43 sec of wall-time.

At the other end of the scale a Branch/Dept combo with only one row, for
one day's data, went through this loop in 45 sec of wall... but these are
both extremes, of course.  For approx. 400 Branch/Dept combos, each with
366 days of data it is just... too much.  I've managed, I think, to
duplicate the COUNT(DISTINCT) and SUM with the following batch COBOL
logic:

(note - the four conditions being totalled for are billed/reversed,
billed/not reversed, sent/reversed and sent/not reversed)

IF WS-IN-CUSTNO NOT = WS-PREV-CUSTNO                        
    MOVE WS-IN-CUSTNO  TO  WS-PREV-CUSTNO                   
    MOVE SPACES TO WS-COND-FLAGS.                           
                                                            
EVALUATE TRUE                                               
    WHEN WS-IN-RECSTAT = 'B' AND                            
         WS-IN-REVERSAL-IND NOT = 'Y'                       
        ADD WS-IN-TOTPRC  TO  WS-OUT-BILLED-NOTREV-SUM      
        IF NOT COND1-FOUND                                  
            SET COND1-FOUND TO TRUE                         
            ADD +1          TO  WS-OUT-BILLED-NOTREV-CNT    
        END-IF                                              
    WHEN WS-IN-RECSTAT = 'B' AND WS-IN-REVERSAL-IND = 'Y'   
        ADD WS-IN-TOTPRC  TO  WS-OUT-BILLED-REV-SUM         
        IF NOT COND2-FOUND                                  
            SET COND2-FOUND TO TRUE                         
            ADD +1          TO  WS-OUT-BILLED-REV-CNT       
        END-IF                                              
    WHEN WS-IN-RECSTAT = 'S' AND                         
         WS-IN-REVERSAL-IND NOT = 'Y'                    
        ADD WS-IN-TOTPRC  TO  WS-OUT-SENT-NOTREV-SUM     
        IF NOT COND3-FOUND                               
            SET COND3-FOUND TO TRUE                      
            ADD +1          TO  WS-OUT-SENT-NOTREV-CNT   
        END-IF                                           
    WHEN WS-IN-RECSTAT = 'S' AND WS-IN-REVERSAL-IND = 'Y'
        ADD WS-IN-TOTPRC  TO  WS-OUT-SENT-REV-SUM        
        IF NOT COND4-FOUND                               
            SET COND4-FOUND TO TRUE                      
            ADD +1          TO  WS-OUT-SENT-REV-CNT      
        END-IF                                           
END-EVALUATE.                                            

... and yes, I know that the same customer can, in theory, be counted four
times but this is what The Customer wants.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Instead of DB2... Batch? Forward... Into the Past!

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 2001-10-12T06:49:09+10:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BC605C4.D1A76241@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<RxDw7.8729$ym4.375005@iad-read.news.verio.net> <ts8dud3t0rc821@corp.supernews.com> <NVmx7.25044$ev2.33985@www.newsranger.com>`

```
What you are doing, maybe without realizing it, is creating a data warehouse.
There are lots of good books on the topic that discuss the problems you are
having in detail.

Mainly you are doing the right thing, separating out the DW from the operational
system, etc.

The problem with data warehouses is that, as you found, the normalized data
model leads to inefficient query performance in various domains. Thus the Data
Mart was invented. It is a specialized database with denormalized structure
built for good query performance in various domains. You build the DW and then
build the DMs from it. Some data marts even use 'summary tables' which are
predigested tables of summary statistics. This is what you are thinking of
doing. The usual advice is 'only do that if you need to do it' but if you need
to do it you are following the right approach - though on some platforms there
are tools to help you do all this.

I known this may be heresy but you could put the query database on a Wintel box
and give it 1gb of RAM. The whole table would fit in memory and the queries
should go really fast. Very few data warehouses are on manframes; they are
usually on Wintel or Unix depending on relability and scale requirements.

One main risk here is that you are going to create a DW/DM without having
properly architected it, and you will have to redo it all in a few years time.

Tim Josling

>news:RxDw7.8729$ym4.375005@iad-read.news.verio.net...
>>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>> ... and dump the results to tape.  (this takes a mere 10 minutes)
(...etc...)

>
```

```
Is this a "native" Assembler program that you want to convert to COBOL - or
is it actually "object code" assembler from an older COBOL program - that
you have lost the COBOL source code for?  If it is the latter, then there is
(at least one) company that does this.  If it is the former, then I think
that the "hire a programmer" is your best bet.
```

#### ↳ Re: Conversion of assembler to cobol

- **From:** Steve Burns <stephen.d.burns2@boeing.com>

```
Try http://www.raincode.com

They have a 4gl system that may be of use.  Unless you have a tone of
code to convert, it probably will be easier to have programmer do it
manually, though.  (It is possible that raincode has already done
something like thins, in which case, they may have 4gl code all ready to
use.  Raincode will also bid on doing the conversion for you.

One of my first jobs (decodes ago) was to convert IBM assembler programs
to cobol.  It ws not a difficult technical job, but understanding
exactly what the code did was tough.  Some of my code contained comments
to say that I had no idea what portions of the code actually
accomplished, but I was pretty sure that the cobol translation
duplicated the assembler stuff.....

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
```

#### ↳ Re: Conversion of assembler to cobol

- **From:** Greg Ferro <gferro@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-14T09:39:56-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BC9A3BC.648BCA16@sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<98bf16f9.0110090836.219ae0a3@posting.google.com>`

```
The following is from the FAQ for the RETIRE conversion product from
Source Recovery at  www.source-recovery.com

5.  Can SRC convert IBM Assembler or PL/1programs into COBOL?  It
depends.   SRC can convert certain types of business-oriented Assembler
or PL/1 programs to COBOL.   For example, SRC can normally convert to
Assembler programs that primarily use the following macros/SVCs:
OPEN/CLOSE, GET/PUT, READ/WRITE, SAVE/RETURN, and DCB.  Often, however,
Assembler programs cannot be automatically converted to anything
remotely approaching hand written COBOL.

If your assembler programs are doing any bit manipulation or dealing
with exotic data formats (e.g., unsigned packed decimal), you will
probably be forced to do a manual conversion.


David Smith wrote:

> Is there any software out there that will do some rudimentary
> conversion of assembler to COBOL under OS/390.  I have already done
> some research
> on the Web, and the pickings seem slim.  Have I simply not looked
> sufficiently, or does the animal not exist?
```

## Thread: Xpediter 7.0 Question — 2 msgs

### Xpediter 7.0 Question

- **From:** aul1231@yahoo.com (Vik Mehta)
- **Date:** 2001-10-09T10:56:46-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2df8b8ca.0110090956.55a1cc91@posting.google.com>`

```
We have Xpediter/TSO 7.0 installed at our shop.  When doing a Find
(PF5) on a particular string in interactive mode we've been
experiencing a problem.
Xpediter will find the first occurrence of the string but it will not
find the subsequent occurrence if we don't execute another command
(PF9, PF8, ...) before hitting F5 again!

Any ideas, suggestions?

Thanks,
Vik
```

#### ↳ Re: Xpediter 7.0 Question

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2001-10-14T13:40:32-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BC9F840.1A138469@attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<2df8b8ca.0110090956.55a1cc91@posting.google.com>`

```
Vik,
Compuware has a way for you to ask this question of their support people
via their Web page.  Go to "www.compuware.com", choose "Support", then
"Frontline Technical Support".  You can register, then you can ask your
question.

Our shop has Xpediter and other debuggers.  I prefer to use IBM's Debug
Tool, which comes with COBOL for OS/390 & VM if you order the "full
pack".  If your shop has C/C++ or the newest PL/1 Debug Tool supports
these languages as well, and may have been ordered with one of those
languages.  You may want to check it out.
Colin Campbell

> (PF5) on a particular string in interactive mode we've been
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
then I try and link the object code and get some lines of text in a
dos box, but it aborts with this error code:

LINK : fatal error LNK1561: entry point must be defined

Here is the code that works at school, I have changed the file
locations to point to the folders on my computer at home and not those
at school. Any help would be appreciated.

      **************

*
      *                INPUT FILE - CURRENCY FILE
*
      *
*

******************************************************************

       FD  CURRENCY-FILE.

       01  CURRENCT-REC.
           02 CC-COUNTRY-CODE                  PIC XX.

           02 CC-ORIGINAL-ID                   PIC X.

*

*

*

******************************************************************

      *************************


******************************************************************
      *
*

*
      *
*

******************************************************************

           02 RPT-HEADER-LINE1.
                03 RH-YEAR                      PIC XXXX.

                03 RH-MONTH                     PIC XX.
               	03 FILLER			PIC X VALUE "/".

                03 FILLER                       PIC X(21).

                           VALUE "ISO WORLDWIDE CURRENCY REPORT".
                03 FILLER                       PIC X(23).
               	03 FILLER			PIC X(5) VALUE "PAGE
".
                03 PAGE-COUNT                   PIC 99.

           02 RPT-HEADER-LINE2.
                      03 FILLER                 PIC X(7)
                           VALUE "COUNTRY".
                      03 FILLER                 PIC X(38).
                      03 FILLER                 PIC X(8)
                           VALUE "CURRENCY".
                      03 FILLER                 PIC X(37).

           02 RPT-HEADER-LINE3.
                03 FILLER                       PIC X(7)
                           VALUE " CODE  ".
                03 FILLER                       PIC X(11).
                03 FILLER                       PIC X(12)
                           VALUE "COUNTRY".
            	03 FILLER			PIC X(17).
                03 FILLER                       PIC X(4)
                           VALUE "CODE".
                03 FILLER                       PIC X(12).
                03 FILLER                       PIC X(13)
                           VALUE "CURRENCY NAME".
               	03 FILLER			PIC X(14).
            	
           02 RPT-HEADER-LINE4.
                  03 FILLER               PIC X(90) VALUE ALL "-".

           02 RPT-DETAIL.
                03 FILLER                       PIC XX.
               	03 RD-COUNTRY-CODE              PIC XX.
               	03 FILLER                       PIC X(6).
               	03 RD-COUNTRY-NAME              PIC X(40).
      *         03 FILLER                       PIC X(3).
               	03 RD-ALPHA-CURRENCY-CODE       PIC XXX.
                03 FILLER                       PIC XX.
               	03 RD-CURRENCY-NAME             PIC X(30).
                03 FILLER                       PIC X(5).


******************************************************************
      *
*
      *                          DATE
*
      *
*

******************************************************************

       01  WA-TODAYS-DATE.
           02  WA-YEAR                     PIC XXXX.
           02  WA-MONTH                    PIC XX.
           02  WA-DAY                      PIC XX.
           02  WA-TIME                     PIC X(13).



******************************************************************
      *
*
      *                         ACCUMULATORS
*
      *
*

******************************************************************

       01  COUNTERS.
      *    02 PAGE-COUNT                       PIC 99.
           02 LINE-COUNT                       PIC 99.
            

******************************************************************
      *
*
      *                         SWITCHES
*
      *
*

******************************************************************
      
       01  SWITCHES.
           02 SW-END-OF-FILE                      PIC X.
               88 END-OF-FILE                          VALUE "Y".
               

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
      ********************
       
       MAIN.
           PERFORM A100-OPEN-SHOP.
           PERFORM B100-PROCESS UNTIL END-OF-FILE.
           MOVE "LEGIT END" TO CURR-OUTPUT-REC.
           WRITE CURR-OUTPUT-REC
               AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINE.
           PERFORM C100-CLOSE-SHOP.
           STOP RUN.
       
       A100-OPEN-SHOP.
           MOVE 60 TO LINE-COUNT.
           MOVE ZERO TO PAGE-COUNT.
           MOVE FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE TO WA-TODAYS-DATE.
           MOVE WA-YEAR TO RH-YEAR.
           MOVE WA-MONTH TO RH-MONTH.
           MOVE WA-DAY TO RH-DAY.
           MOVE "N" TO SW-END-OF-FILE.
           OPEN INPUT CURRENCY-FILE
                OUTPUT OUTPUT-CURRENCY-REPORT.

                
       B100-PROCESS.
           READ CURRENCY-FILE
               AT END MOVE "Y" TO SW-END-OF-FILE.
           IF LINE-COUNT > 40
               PERFORM B200-PRINT-HEADERS.
           MOVE CC-COUNTRY-CODE TO RD-COUNTRY-CODE.
           MOVE CC-COUNTRY-NAME TO RD-COUNTRY-NAME.
           MOVE CC-ALPHA-CURRENCY-CODE TO RD-ALPHA-CURRENCY-CODE.
           MOVE CC-CURRENCY-NAME TO RD-CURRENCY-NAME.
           WRITE CURR-OUTPUT-REC FROM RPT-DETAIL
               AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINE.
           ADD 1 TO LINE-COUNT.

       B200-PRINT-HEADERS.

           ADD 1 TO PAGE-COUNT.
           WRITE CURR-OUTPUT-REC FROM RPT-HEADER-LINE1
               AFTER ADVANCING 2 LINES.
           WRITE CURR-OUTPUT-REC FROM RPT-HEADER-LINE2
               AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINE.
           WRITE CURR-OUTPUT-REC FROM RPT-HEADER-LINE3
               AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINE.
           WRITE CURR-OUTPUT-REC FROM RPT-HEADER-LINE4
               AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINE.
           MOVE 4 TO LINE-COUNT.
           
       C100-CLOSE-SHOP.
           CLOSE CURRENCY-FILE
                 OUTPUT-CURRENCY-REPORT.

       D100-END.
           DISPLAY "OWWWWWWWW".
           DISPLAY "THAT HURT".
           DISPLAY "YOU SHOULDN'T BE HERE".
           STOP RUN.
```

#### ↳ Re: Student question about Fujitsu PowerCobol3

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-09T22:07:45-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9q0e5p$pup$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<clb7st0vfs2famg3plj7bs2kgf1pgplfvi@4ax.com>`

```
This is NOT really a problem with your code.  It is a common "new to Fujitsu
problem".  You need to tell Fujitsu (unlike Micro Focus) which is the MAIN
program in your application.  There are several ways to do this.  If you are
using Power - I think you can just "click" on it and mark it as "MAIN".
Otherwise, you can put an @OPTIONS MAIN statement as the first line of your
program.
```

### Two compiler on same PC ???

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-09T22:11:33-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9q0ed1$2g5$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I am curious.  Has anyone in CLC ever SUCCESSFULLY installed *both* Fujitsu
PowerCOBOL and Micro Focus NetExpress on the same PC (at the same time)?

I don't know if this should work or not (and I am not about to be the first
person to try it).

I want to do some "research" that would require me to be able to easily test
code with both compilers - without having to re-boot - much less re-install
each time.  I certainly do NOT expect either vendor to document this as a
"supported" environment - but I could imagine others wanting to do it.

If you HAVE done this, please let me know any "tricks" required.  (FYI - I
am running Windows98 - if that makes any difference)
```

- **Message-ID:** `<20011010115436.17640.00000681@nso-cb.aol.com>`

```
<wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> writes:

>
>I am curious.  Has anyone in CLC ever SUCCESSFULLY installed *both* Fujitsu
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

This sounds like an interesting 'opportunity'.  It would seem that you would
need some sort of inventory of the programs/DLLs that might be called and
review for possible duplication of 'Vendor specific' program/dll names.

I would imagine that you could manage it with careful monitoring of the PATH.
Both products probably execute a COBOL97 and depending on which is found in the
search path first will determine which compiler is actually used.

Probably would need to create a BAT file around the IDE to set the correct PATH
for the particular vendor/compiler.
```

#### ↳ Re: Two compiler on same PC ???

- **From:** JAndersen@screenio.com (John Andersen)
- **Date:** 2001-10-10T19:24:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3bc49bfa.74833877@192.168.2.2>`
- **References:** `<9q0ed1$2g5$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
On Tue, 9 Oct 2001 22:11:33 -0500, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
wrote:

 >I am curious.  Has anyone in CLC ever SUCCESSFULLY installed *both* Fujitsu
 >PowerCOBOL and Micro Focus NetExpress on the same PC (at the same time)?
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
 >am running Windows98 - if that makes any difference)

In our development of ScreenIO, and maintenance of dozens of 
systems for clients, we have had to work this way for the last
20 years.

I have at least 5 different compilers installed here.

There is usually no problem in installation, I simply point the
installation to different directories, (which most of the 
installers do by default anyway).  We tend to store all
software packages on the network, but not always - such as
when the license won't allow it.

Tricks:  Some arrogant compilers presume to set environmentals in
your autoexec.bat as if they were the sole reason you purchased
a PC.  Occasionally then mess around with your path.  (Love to
get my hands on the programmer who decided that was a good idea,
Fujitsu, this means you...)

Occasionally they set an environmental variable that conflicts
with another compiler (rare, but I seem to remember it).

Extract any of these path setting or environment settings
and put them in a separate bat.  Name the bat for the compiler.

Launch a dos box and invoke said bat. Then from
the command line invoke any compiler work benches (if
you insist on using the work bench - which I always avoid
if possible).  Anything invoked from said dos box
will inherit the environment from which it is launched.

We tend to have bat files witch do all the
drive mapping, environment setting, etc for each 
software system we work on.  That bat knows everything
needed by that system.  We set up our compilers to put
everything in the proper place: separate directories
for OBJs, LSTs, Copybooks, source, etc.  So often
Workbenches will, out of the box, put everything in
one huge hodgepodge directory. What a mess.

We've done this on a single windows box, or a network
(Novell, MS-Workgroups, Linux, etc.). Works very
well.








John Andersen
NORCOM
http://www.SCREENIO.com/
Juneau, Alaska
```

```
Bill,

Use Partition Magic to create a new partition (logical disk drive) on your
hard drive.

Install to that partition and you will NEVER have any problems with EVs,
Autoexec.bat, or PATH conflicts on your system.

Furthermore, you can make it bootable into that specific environment if you
want to.

I am considering this currently to support Fujitsu V5 and V6 on the same
machine. (I still need a good reason to install V6 and can't find one; I
have a number of good reasons NOT to install it... )

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: Two compiler on same PC ???

- **From:** Herwig Huener <Herwig.Huener@fujitsu-siemens.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-11T12:39:10+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BC576CE.ABA4BD51@fujitsu-siemens.com>`
- **References:** `<9q0ed1$2g5$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
2001-10-11 12:22:21 MESZ

"William M. Klein" schrieb:
> 
> I am curious.  Has anyone in CLC ever SUCCESSFULLY installed *both* Fujitsu
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> am running Windows98 - if that makes any difference)

You talk about the windows environment, I guess.

With contemporary Disk-Size, the solution of choice is quite a bunch
of operating systems rather than a bunch of compilers.

1) Install a Windows and a Linux. The Linux Installation should
   have a few dozen GigaBytes available. Also install
   the Linux LoadManager (LILO) which gives you the choice of
   booting either Windows or Linux.

2) Boot Linux. Save your whole Windows-Partition (using dd ...)
   as a file somewhere into the Linux-system. That is the virgin
   Windows-Installation which you might need now and then.

Now, for all Cobol-compilers your want to installate:

3) Boot Linux.

4) dd the virgin Windows-Installation-file to the Windows-partition

5) Boot Windows.

6) Install Cobol-Compiler (or whatever).

7) Boot Linux

8) dd the Windows-partition to a new Windows-Installation-file.

Repeat that as often as desired.

One of the advantages is that you can avoid windows-installation-orgies
if you bomb the window-installation - which may happen now and then in
SW-Development. dd-ing the windows back is much faster than a new
windows re-installation.

LILO allows for more than Windows-Installation - so, for trying a few
Cobol-Compilers in parallel, a re-boot is all you need. It's just
a matter of careful Disk-Layout-Design.

But you said that you not even wish to reboot, which is understandable.
So the next step would be to try VMWARE - this should allow running
a few Windows installations at the same time - but I myself never
tried VMWARE so I cannot say anything for sure. Maybe someone else
is familiar with VMWARE.

Further advantages of this approach: If you destroy a
windows-installation,
you can go Linux, mount the damaged Windows-partition as well as the
disk-image of that windows (Linux can do such tricks), and make a
recursive
diff. That may be a tool to find out what went wrong.

Excuse all typos and English Language Errors.

Herwig
```

- **References:** `<9q0ed1$2g5$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3BC576CE.ABA4BD51@fujitsu-siemens.com> <3bcb11a8.170510216@192.168.2.2>`

```
After I do a FULL backup, I do plan on trying this (the "simple" solution).
The only "problem" that someone has raised (that sounds reasonable to me) is
what "program" Windows uses (as a default) to open files with .CBL or .COB
extensions.

Besides the "full backup" - I also plan on doing a "registry" backup.
Clearly, my worry is/was that both products would try and use identically
named registry entries.  It sounds like this isn't a real problem - but I'll
believe it when I try it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Two compiler on same PC ???

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-10-12T13:00:29+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Both of these reservations sound real to me, Bill.

(Especially the file association; it would a real pain to have to keep
switching associations depending on the compiler you wish to use... I
wouldn't mind betting that you end up with .COB for one and .CBL for the
other. This will probably cause problems with default COPY book extensions,
so they may have to be configured as .CPY1 and CPY2 or somesuch... I just
don't believe that it all works "flawlessly" without some manual
reconfiguring, but I hope I'm wrong...)

Please let us know how you get on.

Pete.
```

```
are going to load from a specific Project 'Open Files' dialog.

> Besides the "full backup" - I also plan on doing a "registry" backup.
> Clearly, my worry is/was that both products would try and use identically
> named registry entries.  It sounds like this isn't a real problem - but I'll

As a long shot - both could conceivably use a common dll. If there is a common
one, reflect when you Uninstall the 'Un-installation' tells you it is leaving
files in place "....because they are shared and may be required by something
else...".

Anyway as you say - suck it and see.

The simple thing I would like to be able to do is say install N/E University
Edition at the same time as N/E - but jeez am I leery. I don't need the hassle
of partitioning etc.
Real pity I can't install both without the possibility of conflict, or one
changing the settings for the other, or 'University' screwing up my Access Key
!!!  (Why University Edition - I just want to take a bloody look, seeing as they
sent it to me as a freebie !).

Jimmy
```

```
Ah, I see you have seen this response John <G>

You are absolutely right in that both my suggestion and Herwig's are
probably above and beyond the actual topic...

However, if you hang around CLC for a while you will see that getting a
response which is on-topic is even rarer than getting a response which is
actually helpful...<G>

Nevertheless, a lot of useful information is exchanged here and that can't
be a bad thing can it?

Besides, Bill Klein is certainly computer literate enough to draw his own
conclusions... it isn't like we were overwhelming some Newbie with a mass of
jargon.

As for "getting a grip" might I respectfully suggest that you loosen your
own a little bit...<G>

No harm has been done by thoroughly exploring the topic...

Having said all of that, I agree that installing two compilers SHOULD be a
"non-problem" as you put it.

I guess after you get burned a few times with "non-problems", you become a
bit cynical...<G>

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Two compiler on same PC ???

- **From:** Herwig Huener <Herwig.Huener@fujitsu-siemens.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-12T11:35:45+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BC6B971.766B8D5A@fujitsu-siemens.com>`
- **References:** `<9q0ed1$2g5$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3BC576CE.ABA4BD51@fujitsu-siemens.com> <3bcb11a8.170510216@192.168.2.2>`

```
2001-10-12 11:33:55 MEST

John Andersen schrieb:
> 
> ...
> 
> Where did you get the impression Bill wanted to run Linux?

I did not, and I have answered that already in email.

> It is not necesssary to time warp into a parallel universe
> to run two different compilers!!

Ah - back to my speciality, Thanks!

Actually, if you decide on installing this or that
COBOL-Compiler, the very process of thinking is a
computational process in a higly entropy-generating
environment (our brains). So the decission to
install this compiler or to install that compiler
is made, each one in quite a bunch of universes
which have been split by this thinking process.

Unfortunately, the different instances of ourselves
can never rejoin in order to compare notes and
experiences.

But see the bright side of that: There are also
universes where BASIC is the primary programming
language for economic applications rather than
COBOL. These branches of reality have split from
our reality decades ago and need not bother us.

Herwig
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Two compiler on same PC ???

- **From:** Herwig Huener <Herwig.Huener@fujitsu-siemens.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-12T11:24:32+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BC6B6D0.BB13CFAB@fujitsu-siemens.com>`
- **References:** `<9q0ed1$2g5$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3BC576CE.ABA4BD51@fujitsu-siemens.com> <3bc63110_3@news.newsgroups.com>`

```
2001-10-12 11:22:33 MEST

"Peter E. C. Dashwood" schrieb:
> 
> Fascinating Herwig!

Uhh???

> Earlier in this thread I suggested multiple partitioning and John up in
> Juneau reckoned I was shooting flies with a cannon...
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> (sorry, that's actually 2 questions, but you catch my drift...<G>)

dd does a copy from device to device - and, as you might know, in Unix
about everything is a file. So, in this case, the idea is taking the
whole windows-installation as one file and backup it somewhere!

You might call this "shooting flies with a cannon" - but I think it is
not - copying "a single file", although a large one, is a simple act.
Compare that with a re-installation of windows, if that
windows-installation
gets bombes by some applications placing DLLs in the same "official"
directories and such.

Herwig
```

```
On Fri, 12 Oct 2001 12:45:06 +1300, "Peter E. C. Dashwood"
<dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

 >Fascinating Herwig!
 >
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
 >Pete.
  
Yup, pretty close to a Nuke I'd say.
But it seems to be a catchy disease, A few posts after this someone
who shall remain nameless but who's initials are Steve Thompson
suggested installing Linux on the mainframe to transfer files.
I suspect he had his tongue firmly tucked in his cheek.

But the fact you had to ask what dd is reveales the scope
of the problem imposed by Herwig's solution, which may
well be what you were trying to get at with your "Drift".

The learning curve is great if you want to do anything more
than surf the web with a new OS.
I think it takes about 3 months just to start finding
you way around in a linux box if you come from the
Windows or IBM mainframe environment.  I know it took
me that long, perhaps I'm just dumb.  

Having been thru about 6 different Linux Distros 
(Setteling for the time being on Suse) and more than a few
BSD releases, I must say I love the 'nix OSs and find
windows a bit of a pain, but thats where the money
is these days.  

Still, IMHO (and anything I post is
to be taken as just My Opinion, even if you, Peter, 
read it as if I were issuing an edict of some sort),
installing a new os to accomplish a simple task will
always take more time than just learning the tricks
of the existing os. 

John Andersen
NORCOM
http://www.SCREENIO.com/
Juneau, Alaska
```

## Thread: Problem about ACCEPT — 6 msgs
