### Problem about ACCEPT

- **From:** "Benjamin Wu" <chase@gnuchina.org>
- **Date:** 2001-10-10T15:38:34+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9q0tu9$kpo0t$1@ID-54723.news.dfncis.de>`

```
I have some problem about ACCEPT,
I define a alphanumeric variable like
01 THE-NAME PIC X(10).
and I use command 'ACCEPT THE-NAME PIC X(10)'
but when I input data, I must input total ten alphabetic characters then it
accept input, I try it several time, it is the same.
       @OPTIONS MAIN
       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. BAD01.
       AUTHOR.
       DATE-WRITTEN.     Oct, 2001.
       DATE-COMPILED.
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       CONFIGURATION SECTION.
       SOURCE-COMPUTER.
       OBJECT-COMPUTER.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.
       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01 THE-NAME PICTURE IS X(10).
       01 FIRST-NUMBER  PICTURE IS 99.
       01 SECOND-NUMBER PICTURE IS 99.
       01 THE-RESULT    PICTURE IS 999.
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       MAIN.
            DISPLAY "ENTER SOMEONE'S NAME.".
            ACCEPT THE-NAME.
            DISPLAY "HELLO " THE-NAME.
       PROGRAM-END.
           STOP RUN.
```

#### ↳ Re: Creating Excel files in Mainframes

- **From:** dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Peter E. C. Dashwood)
- **Date:** 2001-10-10T19:39:02-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3638c46.0110101839.5de4257@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<4aca0d77.0110101031.1eabfb1b@posting.google.com>`

```
bvasanth123@rediffmail.com (Vasanth) wrote in message news:<4aca0d77.0110101031.1eabfb1b@posting.google.com>...
> Hi
> Are there tools/applications available to generate MS-Excel files in
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks

I don't know of any tools to do this (that doesn't mean there aren't
any...<G>) but if I had to do it, I would simply produce a CSV file on
the mainframe, FTP it to a server (or use whatever the installation
standard tool is for downloading and uploading between the Network and
the mainframe - this should give you the EBCDIC to ASCII conversion
you require), and import it to Excel.

CSV files are "Comma Separated Variable" files. Each field is
separated from the others by a comma (except the last one);
alphanumeric fields should be in quotes, and Numeric fields in
external decimal, with a point if required. Like this:
"pete",123.45,"hello you sexy thing",123

This can be easily written to a standard sequential file on the
mainframe and will be "converted" to a Line Sequential PC file when it
is downloaded to the Network. (Remember to keep the mainframe "record
size" to less than 256 characters; it helps when you convert to PC
file format.)

You can easily write a module to create CSV files in COBOL (hint: use
STRING...) on the mainframe.

Hope this helps,

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Creating Excel files in Mainframes

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-10-11T18:07:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Cdlx7.394$Pr5.262967@paloalto-snr2.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<4aca0d77.0110101031.1eabfb1b@posting.google.com> <b3638c46.0110101839.5de4257@posting.google.com> <4aca0d77.0110110736.24e9b0ed@posting.google.com>`

```
Vasanth <bvasanth123@rediffmail.com> wrote in message
news:4aca0d77.0110110736.24e9b0ed@posting.google.com...
> We are already doing this CSV process..As we are producing thousands
> of reports, it takes lot of time..
> To reduce this this turnover time, We want to create excel files in
> mainframes and download excel files instead of csv files
>

A. Have you looked at your application IN GENERAL to see where your time is
going?  That is, is the bottleneck on the mainframe producing the QSAM
files, or in the communications (file transfer), or in the Excel "import"
phase?

B. Instead of all those STRING operations used to create CSV with COBOL/390,
have you tried creating the files in fixed-record-size format and letting
Excel import that?

C. Have you looked at the C-Cubed products? I have not used them, but from
their web page it certainly appears that you should take a look.
www.c-cubed.net


MCM
```

```
usage either could be loaded what saves the time ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Creating Excel files in Mainframes

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-14T23:03:49-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9qdne801rkk@enews3.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<4aca0d77.0110101031.1eabfb1b@posting.google.com> <b3638c46.0110101839.5de4257@posting.google.com> <4aca0d77.0110110736.24e9b0ed@posting.google.com> <3BC69343.1B5069B@Azonic.co.nz> <D2C4B0504C765DA5.1CA10E6B4BAA953D.EC118B50FBBCF717@lp.airnews.net> <tGry7.2812$uO2.501360@news2.news.adelphia.net>`

```
"Dan Bartoli" <dbartoli@adelphia.net> wrote:

> I have a similar problem.  The time involved with using csv
files comes when
> the user has to adjust column widths, formatting, etc.

Data type coercion can also trash incoming data--it's a luxury
to be able to send typing information with your dataset.
Unfortunately, CSV doesn't really have this, except though
implication, and CSV parsers have grown interpretive because of
it.

> If anyone can come up with the formatting for an xls file
format, it would
> be most helpful.

A reasonable approach would be to go after WKS output.  It's
less complicated and better documented than XLS files.  (I'm not
sure if it's proprietary as well?)  At any rate, it looks as if
it has enough in its spec to do data formats and basic
formatting--formulas as well.

Here's a link to XLS and WKS specifications:
http://www.wotsit.org/search.asp?page=2&s=database
```

```
using Excel in the current Version, for ever.

They certainly won't publish the internal formats in a timely way. Think
about it...

Pete.
```

```
The Binary File Format (BIFF,) which is the standard for XLS
files, has been stable since Win 3.11.  But BIFF is really a
meta-format, so the details of what information needs to be
stored in that format, in order to be an Excel spreadsheet, has
perhaps changed.
```

#### ↳ Re: Creating Excel files in Mainframes

- **From:** "Lucas, Todd" <Todd.Lucas@C-Cubed.net>
- **Date:** 2001-10-11T08:45:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<O_cx7.52182$kf1.17003165@news1.rdc1.ne.home.com>`
- **References:** `<4aca0d77.0110101031.1eabfb1b@posting.google.com>`

```
You also might want to take a look at a product from C-Cubed, called MVS
Direct API.  It allows you to transfer and populate the spreadsheet from
within an Excel VBA macro - ie no external process to retrieve the Mainframe
file (FTP, IND$File, NDM, etc.).  It can transfer QSAM flat files, PDS
members, or even VSAM records directly into the spreadsheet.  Check out
www.c-cubed.net if you're interested ...

HTH - TL
```

- **From:** PhilOnline@pgrahams.com
- **Date:** 2001-10-11T17:53:39+00:00

```
|A CSV file would be the best bet but you can create an excel file
|directly. Not easy but it can be done.
|http://www.myfileformats.com/

The file would have to be output in ASCII with proper-endian numerics
and downloaded without any conversion.  Conversion of an unformatted
binary file such as .XLS would be (almost) impossible.

I still don't understand the problem with CVS.  I get the impression
the writer thinks that .XLS might be smaller file!  Doubtfull.

Phil
```

```
news:3bc5dbd3.15713179@news.cadiz1.ky.home.com...
```

#### ↳ Re: Creating Excel files in Mainframes

- **From:** Charles <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-11T19:58:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6Smx7.25032$ev2.34027@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<4aca0d77.0110101031.1eabfb1b@posting.google.com>`

```
On 10 Oct 2001 11:31:58 -0700, in article
<4aca0d77.0110101031.1eabfb1b@posting.google.com>, Vasanth stated 
>
>Hi
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thanks

We use an application called Decision Analyzer that makes ,downloadable to the
PC, fully functional Excel OR ".WK3" spreadsheets.  It can also make ".dbf"
database files that can be imported into Access.  It requires some set up and
administration on the Mainframe end, but it is very accessable from the PC with
interactive Screens and Mouse Clicks.  It works with databases on the mainframe
also.  Sometimes we use it to make Comma Delimited Address Files to merge with
Microsoft Word.  Decision Analyzer is made by Decision Technology Inc. (DTI).
In Princeton, NJ, off of route 1.  That is a traffic jam waiting to happen!
They are located in pretty area to visit if you need a business trip; HEE HEE.
They have a website at www.Decision-Technology.com.  I think they can do a Web-X
presentation or send you a demonstration CD.  They actually help us solve
problems when we call them for help; won't get that from Microsoft.

If you can write a program to insert commas inbetween elements in a file,
surrounds text with quotes, and converts packed numbers to decimal, then  you
can make files that can be imported into Excel.  A smart person could probably
write an application to do that, but a windows style application that lets you
highlight fields and files and sort options would be a lot easier.  This
application uses Assembly, Fortran, and I think VB, and EHLLAPI in the Windows
client.

I think EZTREIVE can make files from Databases like Peoplesoft.
```

#### ↳ Re: Creating Excel files in Mainframes

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompson_2@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-12T15:52:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BC710E9.784B2586@neo.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<4aca0d77.0110101031.1eabfb1b@posting.google.com>`

```
Vasanth wrote:
> 
> Hi
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks

Yes you can. Let us look at a few methods.

Get a copy of Linux for S/390 and install it. Now run WINE.
From your PC you can attach to the mainframe and run Excel.
All of this, except for the display on your PC, can be
driven on the mainframe. You can then send files (data)
between the other mainframe operating systems and the Linux
system on the mainframe, and let your code process that
before finally downloading to your PC.

You can write code that runs under OS/390 USS (Unix System
Services). Possibly WINE will run here (not sure of this).
This will allow you to do things w/o having to install
Linux.

You can write code (COBOL, PL/1, etc.) that will do the job
on the mainframe and then download it to your PC. If you
know the Micros**t format for the XL files, you can write
this type of file out. However, you will probably want to
output CSV format, which COBOL is quite capable of doing (I
think this is the type of thing most of the other posters
have been talking about).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Creating Excel files in Mainframes

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-10-13T09:24:09+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BC7FA29.54A8353@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<4aca0d77.0110101031.1eabfb1b@posting.google.com> <3BC710E9.784B2586@neo.rr.com>`

```
Steve Thompson wrote:
>
> Yes you can. Let us look at a few methods.
> 
> Get a copy of Linux for S/390 and install it. Now run WINE.

Linux on S/390 is running native IBM instructions set.  WINE is not an
an emulator (hint: W.I.N.an E.) and only runs on Intel 80x86 instruction
set, as does any Windows application.

> From your PC you can attach to the mainframe and run Excel.
> All of this, except for the display on your PC, can be
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> before finally downloading to your PC.

Even if this could be done, it doesn't necessarily help in saving time. 
You are expecting someone to sit at the terminal and laod and save and
transfer files using mouse clicks and keyboard.  This is probably all
that is wrong with the original problem.

It seems to me that what 'is taking too long' is having to have someone
sit at a keyboard and do the task.  What (IMHO) is required is to
_eliminate_entirely_ any and all manual intervention.

As the actual task has not been defined adequately nor has the 'too
long' been identified then I doubt that much could be done to provide
useful advice.  It is quite probable that spreadsheets are being
distributed within the company and that someone is sitting down and
loading, changing and saving new versions using data from the mainframe.

It would seem to be having the mainframe write the .XLS might be a
solution, except that it would need to work on an existing framework
.XLS as a template into which the data has to be merged.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Creating Excel files in Mainframes

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompson_2@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-14T03:39:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BC90838.832346FC@neo.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<4aca0d77.0110101031.1eabfb1b@posting.google.com> <3BC710E9.784B2586@neo.rr.com> <3BC7FA29.54A8353@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston wrote:
<snip>
> Linux on S/390 is running native IBM instructions set.  WINE is not an
> an emulator (hint: W.I.N.an E.) and only runs on Intel 80x86 instruction
> set, as does any Windows application.
> 
<snip>
If I understand correctly, the port to Linux was done using
a "standard" Linux compiler. This then allows one to use the
G5 or later chipset of the S/390 or z/Architecture by Linux
(IEEE Floating Point wherein 16 Registers are available, not
emulated). I have come to this understanding from some
things learned at IBM classes and www.winehq.com. At this
point, I do not have a mainframe available to me so that I
can attempt to "compile" and execute WINE on an z/series
machine (of any kind). However, the testing that I have done
with Linux on a mainframe suggested that it would be very
fast (compared to my AMD K6-2 350MHz based server).

Also, with the current changes made to the S/390 and
especially to the z/Architecture, UNICODE, ASCII and EBCDIC
are all "natively" supported, along with instructions for
handling the little-endian and big-endian situations.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Creating Excel files in Mainframes

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-10-15T07:57:45+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BCA88E9.CD6AC2E3@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<4aca0d77.0110101031.1eabfb1b@posting.google.com> <3BC710E9.784B2586@neo.rr.com> <3BC7FA29.54A8353@Azonic.co.nz> <3BC90838.832346FC@neo.rr.com>`

```
Steve Thompson wrote:
> 
> Richard Plinston wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> a "standard" Linux compiler. 

The standard Linux compiler, gcc, can emit a wide range of different
machine code sets.  This does not mean that programs can run on
different machines.

> This then allows one to use the
> G5 or later chipset of the S/390 or z/Architecture by Linux
> (IEEE Floating Point wherein 16 Registers are available, not
> emulated). I have come to this understanding from some
> things learned at IBM classes and www.winehq.com. 

Did you learn at winehq that it only claimed compatability with '16 and
32 bit x86' ?

> At this
> point, I do not have a mainframe available to me so that I
> can attempt to "compile" and execute WINE on an z/series
> machine (of any kind). 

It may well be that WINE itself would recompile to other machine codes,
though it probably hasn't been written with this in mind and there may
be issues that require code changes.  But WINE itself is just a series
of DLLs that provide an environment to run Windows programs.  Windows
programs are 80x86 machine code (a handful are also MIPS and Alpha when
these processors were supported).

> However, the testing that I have done
> with Linux on a mainframe suggested that it would be very
> fast (compared to my AMD K6-2 350MHz based server).

You should be able to get a reasonable performance out of a 80x86
machine code interpreter that you would need to write.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Creating Excel files in Mainframes

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompson_2@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-15T22:40:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BCB6520.32709F15@neo.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<4aca0d77.0110101031.1eabfb1b@posting.google.com> <3BC710E9.784B2586@neo.rr.com> <3BC7FA29.54A8353@Azonic.co.nz> <3BC90838.832346FC@neo.rr.com> <3BCA88E9.CD6AC2E3@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston wrote:
<snip>
> 
> You should be able to get a reasonable performance out of a 80x86
> machine code interpreter that you would need to write.

I stand corrected. For some reason (fuzzy logic induced by
too much ...) I was thinking back to yet another project
where a certain company was attempting to port "NT" to
S/370-XA level code. Your point about the 80x86 kept failing
to dawn on me until sometime this AM when I suddenly
realized a "small" set of problems. WINE can only emulate
the API, but the other code taken from the Intel arena would
have to be cross-compiled to the S/3x0 instruction set in
order for it to work... No small task. 

Steve Thompson
OSP Consulting
330/335-9907 office
```

#### ↳ Re: Creating Excel files in Mainframes

- **From:** jraben@cascinc.com (Jeff Raben)

```
if your many spreadsheets are fixed(?) in length you can do what I
did.
1) Build a prototype on the PC with formulae to pick up data.
2) build a CSV file.
3) execute - a little copy and "special" paste and BAM.

If you need more, between excel macros (I'm not very experienced in
that) and Visual Basic you can "merge" the csv and the predefined
sheets into valid output a number of ways.  
I have been very successful in building the equivalent of 1 page
sheets of over 300 variations exported in csv.

Beat a sharp stick but not by much.

Jeff

and stir with a Runcible spoon...
```

<a id="t-bd6cbef1cc"></a>
## Thread: Generated paragraph names in LE/COBOL-390 — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Generated paragraph names in LE/COBOL-390

- **From:** Martin@pi-sysprog.de (Martin Truebner)
- **Date:** 2001-10-12T00:27:00-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5e288797.0110112326.7caa676f@posting.google.com>`

```
I have developed code that uses the hooks in the TGT to create what
was once FLOW (display the last n executed lines).

When I added support for paragraph-names, I found that COBOL generates
virtuell names. These do clobber my trace table- Any way to suppress
them?

to illustrate:  100  PARA1. 
                101      IF A = B 
                102         MOVE C TO D
                103      END-IF
                104      MOVE X TO Y

The trace looks like this  100 PARA1
                           101
                           103 ????
                           104

The 103 entry is a paragraph-entry with a generated name, but no
internal table has the name(of course).

One alternative is to do the analysis at creation. But this is way to
much overhead. Right now I only analyse the trace-table in case of an
abend.

Is there a way to convince COBOL (in OS or VSE) not to generate this
useless code.

Martin
```

#### ↳ Re: Generated paragraph names in LE/COBOL-390

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-10-12T10:41:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BC6C815.3977801F@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<5e288797.0110112326.7caa676f@posting.google.com>`

```
Since the procedure division map is basically the resulting assembler
code the virtual paragraph names are assembler tags needed so that the
assembler can be read and in the rare case of a bug, verified.

Martin Truebner wrote:
> 
> I have developed code that uses the hooks in the TGT to create what
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> Martin
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Generated paragraph names in LE/COBOL-390

- **From:** martin@pi-sysprog.de (Martin Truebner)
- **Date:** 2001-10-14T06:05:56-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<52635377.0110140505.382baebe@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<5e288797.0110112326.7caa676f@posting.google.com> <3BC6C815.3977801F@istar.ca>`

```
Clark,

>> ...virtual paragraph names are assembler tags needed so that the
assembler can be read and in the rare case ....<<

I understand the need for it and it is also nice for human readers
that they are indicated in the assembler-listing of the procedure
division.

I doubt that there is any use for the BAL generated to the hook in the
TGT.

or again with a sample 

   100 LABEL   generates a BAL R14,LABELHOOK
   101         generates a BAL R14,INSTRHOOK
   103 GN02    generates a BAL R14,LABELHOOK      


Martin
```

#### ↳ Re: Generated paragraph names in LE/COBOL-390

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-12T07:54:32-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9q6p7r$20r$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<5e288797.0110112326.7caa676f@posting.google.com>`

```
If you want a "READY TRACE" facility - why not use what IBM documents as the
way to do this?

See:
 http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igymg202/4.1.5?

and go down to the section called,
  "READY TRACE and RESET TRACE statements"

  ***

As far as "last n statements" - I assume you don't have a decent debugger -
so you have to "play" with TGT, etc.  This does mean that you will probably
have to modify your "analysis" tool every release or two - as IBM *does*
change their code generation about that often.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Generated paragraph names in LE/COBOL-390

- **From:** martin@pi-sysprog.de (Martin Truebner)
- **Date:** 2001-10-14T06:09:26-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<52635377.0110140509.64433ae9@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<5e288797.0110112326.7caa676f@posting.google.com> <9q6p7r$20r$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Bill,

>> If you want a "READY TRACE" facility ...<<

>> ...debugger ..<<

I do not want either. I want a substitute for what was FLOW=nn. Not as
expensive (pathlengthwise) as READY TRACE and therefor usable for
production-programs.

Martin
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Generated paragraph names in LE/COBOL-390

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2001-10-14T13:44:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tFgy7.292$or2.78481571@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<5e288797.0110112326.7caa676f@posting.google.com> <9q6p7r$20r$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <52635377.0110140509.64433ae9@posting.google.com>`

```
    I include my own home-grown version of a trace table in most new
programs these days.  It stores the most recent paragraph names and upon
encountering an abend condition, I display this trace table.  In the event
of an unexpected abend, the trace table can be located in the dump.  I can
e-mail the code to you if you like.
```

```
email to:

The following is Appendix B.5 - Changes to create Version 3.01

frequent comp.lang.cobol submitters).

where available

CobCy

Windows/98, and Windows/2000.
```

<a id="t-016f248dab"></a>
## Thread: How is computing equipment with FBI? — 15 msgs

### How is computing equipment with FBI?

- **From:** Herwig Huener <Herwig.Huener@fujitsu-siemens.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-12T15:04:27+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BC6EA5B.AB8A3D@fujitsu-siemens.com>`

```
2001-10-12 15:15:15 MEST

Subject seems to be OffTopic but it is not.

Yesterday there was a Broadcast in German Television
about the computing equipment of the BKA. BKA is
"BundesKriminalAmt", and it is the equivalence of
the United States FBI.

The BKA uses computing equipment and Databases
installed in 1972. Complaint was expressed that the
vendor (Siemens) is not anymore capable of
maintaining this old system because most programmers
retired or even died.

The Systems networking capability: one. Compatibility
with modern databases: none. Power and real estate
consumption: quite impressive.

Data-Exchange with the other departments of the
police in Germany has to be done by reading from one
terminal and typing into another terminal.

An interview with somebody of the ministry of the
interior (BundesMinisterium des Innern) was broadcast
which showed that the ministry of the interior
has no idea in what bad state the computing
equipment of the BKA actually is in.

Considering this, it is evident that terrorists
may move quite freely in this country, and we all
hope that the US do not consider us as
"harbouring terrorists" because Germany has such
insufficient resources. After all, we do not want
to be air-raided.

As for the relevance to this newsgroup: I do
not know whether this old system under consideration
has been implemented in COBOL - maybe there is no
one left who knows. Also, this might be top
secret information. But I would like to know whether
the computing equipment run by the FBI is in similar
bad condition as the one run by the BKA.

After all, there is an opportunity for a new,
interesting and challenging project.

The information presented in this posting has been
made public by the German Television and may be
considered as non-confidental:

http://www.ndrtv.de/panorama/index.html

http://www.ndrtv.de/panorama/sendung/index.html

http://www.ndrtv.de/panorama/sendung/computer_presseerklaerung.html

Excuse me where my English obscures meaning.

Herwig
```

#### ↳ Re: How is computing equipment with FBI?

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-12T12:28:53-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<A69284EF2FF6B169.49E2C40D16ADAC81.44AB834DE56D509C@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<3BC6EA5B.AB8A3D@fujitsu-siemens.com>`

```
On Fri, 12 Oct 2001 15:04:27 +0200, Herwig Huener
<Herwig.Huener@fujitsu-siemens.com> enlightened us:

>2001-10-12 15:15:15 MEST
>
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
>

While I don't know specifically and I'm not sure if anyone outside of
the FBI's MIS department would know, it seems I remember reading that
all of the security agencies in the U.S. government (FBI, CIA, NSA
etc) have been upgrading both hardware and software for the last 10-15
years.  The last two major agencies that I am aware of that upgraded
was the Internal Revenue Service and the Social Security
Administration.  They all began upgrading in the 1980's as I recall
and was probably the reason that Y2K came off as well as it did.

As for the U.S. bombing Germany, that is highly unlikely.  The German
police in Hamburg have done a marvelous job rounding up suspects and
supplying information.  I don't think you need to worry.

>After all, there is an opportunity for a new,
>interesting and challenging project.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>Herwig

Excuse me where my English obscures meaning. :)

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

When I was born, I was so surprised I couldn't 
talk for a year and a half.

~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

```
Herwig Huener wrote:
>

> 

interesting.
I met some "forensic" PC people (legal types) and they collect all sorts
of antiquated PC's and operating systems (for data recovery and
evidence); they also stated that they keep abreast of the new
technologies.  

Someone (somewhere) knows how that system works.

I imagine that this is a case where management thought it was too
expensive to modernize, until it became really too expensive not to
modernize.

	Susan
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How is computing equipment with FBI?

- **From:** Herwig Huener & Josella Simone Playton <webmaster@Herwig-Huener.de>
- **Date:** 2001-10-14T20:00:41+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BC9D2C9.6C173BC3@Herwig-Huener.de>`
- **References:** `<3BC6EA5B.AB8A3D@fujitsu-siemens.com> <3BC72C1B.72007995@boeing.com>`

```
2001-10-14 20:00:00 MESZ

Susan A Allen wrote:
> 
> ...
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> modernize.

I see a different problem there, and I have seen this
more than once: the reliability of certain Legacy systems.
Good news as this may be - if there is no need to repair
for decades, then there is nobody around anymore who
understands the system. After all, what vendor could
aford paying a team of programmers for that they do
nothing else than keep themselves familiar with that
system which is in no need of attention?

Someone relying on such systems may be in for a surprise,
when software-environment changes, or the system does
not anymore meet the requirements.

Yes: Then it is expensive not to modernize, and it is
expensive to modernize.

Herwig
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How is computing equipment with FBI?

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-10-15T17:10:22+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3bca6302_6@news.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<3BC6EA5B.AB8A3D@fujitsu-siemens.com> <3BC72C1B.72007995@boeing.com> <3BC9D2C9.6C173BC3@Herwig-Huener.de>`

```
This is an excellent point, Herwig, and one I had not realised until I read
your post.

It is indeed unlikely that people will be retained to maintain systems that
don't need maintaining.

(Of course, most existing Legacy systems DO need constant maintenance and
that's why we have teams of COBOL people on site. But as new development
moves to other platforms and new functionality communicates with Legacy
through an interface, these systems will become more and more "sidelined"
until the scenario you describe comes about. Only the "core" of the Legacy
system is still in use and it DOESN'T need maintenance because all the
enhanced functionality is elsewhere...)

I believe this will be another factor towards speeding the eventual
"leverage" of Legacy code to the new environments which I see emerging in
the next 2 decades. (See my post under "New Tech. was 'Good COBOL Tutorial'
" in this forum.)

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: How is computing equipment with FBI?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-12T17:55:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BC72FFB.1789ED0F@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3BC6EA5B.AB8A3D@fujitsu-siemens.com>`

```
Herwig,

For starters, don't get concerned about German intelligence services -
they have a reputation, along with the Israelis, as being considered
first-class in the Western World. (The current US problem - no agents
that speak Pushtan).

For leisure, I'm currently reading Tom Clancy's 'The Bear and the
Dragon". You know his style of novel with all the high-tech
paraphernalia. Reading Clancy and what the FBI, CIA, and God knows who
else, and what they can do with their marvelous computer systems - the
WTC should never have happened - but it did.

Computerizing intelligence is no more than making it an automative
process. It speeds things up when having selected criteria, you can use
number-crunchers to make comparisons. Consider tracing funding of El
Qada - which international transactions do you want to query, over $10K,
or EVERYTHING - the system would just choke !

It boils down to the information stored in human minds and their ability
to assimilate and put pieces together in the puzzle. Sometimes it is
pure luck - as when the US broke the Japanese codes prior to Pearl
Harbour, or when the British got their hands on an Enigma machine. At
best, computers, regardless of how modern, play a very secondary
function. (Consider the DEA - billions tied up in fighting the drug war
- results, zippo !)

Jimmy

Herwig Huener wrote:

> 2001-10-12 15:15:15 MEST
>
…[57 more quoted lines elided]…
> Herwig
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: INPOL-failure, ...

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-10-17T14:07:32+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3bccdb2e_2@news.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<3BC6EA5B.AB8A3D@fujitsu-siemens.com> <3BCC3568.B6120BFB@fujitsu-siemens.com> <3BCC3949.4ABEC107@fujitsu-siemens.com>`

```
I had a look at the site, Herwig. (My German is a bit rusty, but I think I
got the gist of it)

It is a promotion for Object International. They are quoting sites and
Applications which utilise their OO product. ("Reference" sites...)

As the proposed INPOL system has to run across many countries, they are
saying that the use of components, developed using their product, will
guarantee platform independence, reuse and all the other benefits of
component based technology.

Here's an explanation of the terms you (and they) quoted:

>     Java, C++, CORBA, DBMS Oracle 8x, Application Server Weblogic
>     BEA, OS/2, SUN Solaris, HP Unix,
>     Swing, Java Beans, LDAP

Components will be developed in Java and C++ (note OO version of C). Some
components will be developed as Java Beans. All components will utilise the
Java Swing Class (a Java specific GUI Class) to provide a standard look and
feel, irrespective of the platform they are running on.

The server side will be handled by BEA's Weblogic Application Server. (BEA
are an American company who are rapidly establishing a reputation for
e-business expertise...). This supports Windows, UNIX, and just about
anything else, but it is primarily oriented towards Java based applications.
(Makes sense as they have chosen Java as the primary application development
language, with C++ also being utilised. (Note that the language in which the
components are developed is not really important, although they are
favouring Java... This bears out what I have been saying for over a year
now...<G>)). You can find out the whole story on this server architecture
at: http://www.bea.com/products/weblogic/server/index.shtml

The target OSes are OS/2, Sun Solaris, and HP Unix. This is simply a matter
of documentation because the components don't care what OS they are running
under. It has NO bearing on the development; that's why they are using
components.

CORBA is the Common Object Request Broker which allows the components to be
remotely served and shared. In effect, it defines a component interface or
wrapper which means that all OSes can deal with the component. (This
includes the capability to broker MS COM objects nowadays, although it looks
like the proposed system is studiously avoiding Windows...)

DBMS Oracle 8 is the database software. It is not clear whether they will
use intrinsic Oracle capability for access and reporting or will use JDBC. I
hope they don't tie it directly to Oracle, but as they seem emphatic about
platform independence it is more likely they will use SQL and Java Database
Connectivity (JDBC).

Finally, LDAP is the Lightweight Directory Access Protocol which utilises
TCP/IP stacks rather than OSI. This means the system will communicate with
the TCP/IP protocol which is used on the Web. As its name implies it is a
lighter weight, and therefore less resource hungry, protocol and it permits
an OS independent Directory structure view.

All-in-all, I think this is impressive. They have to meet a European (maybe
World) wide requirement for a system that can run on a number of platforms
and exchange data instantly. I note that they are planning for 20,000
clients.

They have opted, quite logically, for a component based, platform
independent, solution.

Makes sense to me...

final comments below...

"Herwig Huener" <Herwig.Huener@fujitsu-siemens.com> wrote in message
news:3BCC3949.4ABEC107@fujitsu-siemens.com...
> 2001-10-16 15:36 57 MESZ
>
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>
You are quite right when you are talking about traditional development, for
example, in COBOL. Obviously, the more diverse the application and the more
Languages and OSes involved, the higher the probability of failure.
(Certainly the degree of difficulty increases, and therefore the chance of
success is diminished.)

Having managed projects where mainframes and networks were involved, using
COBOL, PL/1 and VB, I endorse your observation. (The plus side is that when
you DO get it in, it is immensely satisfying, BECAUSE it was
difficult...<G>)

However, in this case they are using ONE Language (Java primarily.... C++
will be for the stuff that is REALLY close to the machine) and it is ideally
suited for the task they are attempting. By opting for the best technology
that is currently available for platform independent networking (the BEA
server and the use of components) I believe they are maximising the chance
of success.

You can expect many future projects to go the same way. (See my article,
"Meet the Future" in the April edition of www.aboutlegacycoding.com  (check
the "archive"))

Hope the above explanation helps,

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: How is computing equipment with FBI?

- **From:** "Edwin Earl Ross" <EdwinEarl@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-18T00:21:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9gpz7.437$Cs7.258569367@newssvr11.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<3BC6EA5B.AB8A3D@fujitsu-siemens.com>`

```
The FBI isn't likely to answer this question. However, having worked on some
US military systems, here is my estimate.

US Military purchasing is a complex and time consuming process. Getting a
critical item may take six months or a year, unless it is already in a
warehouse. Getting a new computer system (except for PCs) may take several
years. Some military systems use computers that are 15 years old or more,
especially special purpose systems. These old systems are well maintained
(as the B52s which are about 40 years old), and serve their purpose.
However, they aren't GUI systems with all the bells and whistles.

PCs are handled differently. Since the cost is fairly low, getting a PC is
much easier. Consequently, the replacement cycle is probably much less than
15 years, but it is probably longer than the industry standard of 2-3 years.

Being a government organization, the FBI suffers from constraints similar to
those of the US Military.

I suspect the FBI has both old and new systems--about the same as the US
Military. That is, mainframe systems up to 15 years old and PC systems that
are relatively new, both hardware and off-the-shelf software. They must have
an staff of computer professionals who continually maintain and enhance
their proprietary systems. As is the case with private business, mission
critical projects will be done and others will be delayed.

"Herwig Huener" <Herwig.Huener@fujitsu-siemens.com> wrote in message
news:3BC6EA5B.AB8A3D@fujitsu-siemens.com...
> 2001-10-12 15:15:15 MEST
...
> As for the relevance to this newsgroup: I do
> not know whether this old system under consideration
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> bad condition as the one run by the BKA.
...
> Herwig
```

```
    I can't speculate about the FBI, but I do have some experience
with the US Army.  In 1980, They were still using (some) the old FADAC,
Field Artillery Digital Automatic Computer.  It did not have one single
integrated circuit whatsoever, used an 8K fixed head disk as main memory,
and stored programs on paper tape.  The paper tape was very fast though.

    The "new" computer was the TACFIRE, which has magnetic core
memory like the IBM 360 UGA ripped out in 1976.  Hundreds and hundreds of
little circuit boards, each with 6 ic's, that were about the size of a SIMM.
```

## Thread: Structure Chart for cobol program — 11 msgs

### Structure Chart for cobol program

- **From:** tc_yohannan@hotmail.com (Chuck Leviton)
- **Date:** 2001-10-12T14:21:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,bit.listserv.ibm-main
- **Message-ID:** `<3bc6fbb9.263235532@News.CIS.DFN.DE>`

```
Is there a compiler option or some other means when working on MVS
COBOL to generate a structure chart of the way in which paragraphs are
PERFORMed?

I am looking for essentially a list of para names with indentations to
show which para PERFORMs which other para
```

#### ↳ Re: Structure Chart for cobol program

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-14T04:50:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,bit.listserv.ibm-main
- **Message-ID:** `<AQ8y7.28263$ev2.35704@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<3bc6fbb9.263235532@News.CIS.DFN.DE>`

```
Hi TC,

You're using MVS COBOL so I assume that you also use ISPF. If so, you can get
into edit for the source of the module you're interested in and do the
following:

On the cmd line enter: x all;f proc 8;f all p'^' 8;del all nx
next cmd           : create yrname;can

The member "yrname" will contain all the pnames in the pgm. You'll have to
indent them manually or you could add another "f all perform" and collect all
the "perform pgraph" stmts.

It's not fancy, but the price is right.

Regards, Jack



In article <3bc6fbb9.263235532@News.CIS.DFN.DE>, Chuck Leviton says...
>
>Is there a compiler option or some other means when working on MVS
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>show which para PERFORMs which other para
```

```
a numeric value p'#' 8 works better because it skips all of the comments.  
p"^" 8 looks for non blanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: Structure Chart for cobol program

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-14T20:23:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,bit.listserv.ibm-main
- **Message-ID:** `<jvmy7.28793$ev2.35729@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<3bc6fbb9.263235532@News.CIS.DFN.DE>`

```
Hi Rufio,

The P'^' means any non-blank chars.

I guess you could code "f all ' exit'" to identify the exit pgraphs.
BTW, I forgot to mention that you can create a PF key to do this stuff if you
use it often. As a mater of fact, I created a PDS member (PFKEYS) to keep all
this kind of stuff. Then I can cut & paste it into the PF keys when I need it. I
include comments, because I always forget which one I used for what. Sometimes,
even w/the comments I don't remember what I used them for. 

A sample is below. Regards, Jack

Sorry Chuck, for the salutation of my prev post. Didn't see your name till it
was too late.

***********************************************************************
***************  USE TO DELETE one of 2 paired lines    ***************
***************  saves the line w/"cont" in it and del  ***************
***************  etes the previous line.                ***************
***********************************************************************
f cont; f ' ' 1 prev;f ' ' 1 prev;:d;f p'=' 72
*********************************************************************   
***************  USE TO FIRE UP 8 ISPF WINDOWS AFTER    *************   
***************  ISPF STARTUP                           *************   
*********************************************************************   
SCRNAME SDSF1 PERM;SPLIT NEW;SCRNAME COMPILE PERM                       
SPLIT NEW;SCRNAME JOBJCL PERM;SPLIT NEW;SCRNAME PGMSRC PERM;            
SPLIT NEW;SCRNAME CPYBKS PERM;SPLIT NEW;SCRNAME MISC1 PERM              
SPLIT NEW;SCRNAME MISC2 PERM;SPLIT NEW;SCRNAME MISC3 PERM;;;            

*********************************************************************   
***************        USE TO FIRE UP A NEW WINDOW      *************   
*********************************************************************   
SPLIT NEW;SCRNAME                                                       
*********************************************************************   
***************      USE TO LIST ALL ACTIVE WINDOWS     *************   
*********************************************************************   
SWAP LIST
********************************************************************* 
***************   USE TO EXIT A WINDOW. ENTER REPEATEDLY   ********** 
***************   AT EOD UNTIL DELETE LOG DS PANEL APPEARS ********** 
********************************************************************* 
;=X
***************  USE TO bracket cobol paragraph names   ***************
***************  with '*****'                           ***************
***********************************************************************
Manually: x all;f all p'a####' 8 then use the following pf keys for 
Each paragraph name.
x all -exit;top;f p'a####' 8 nx;:r2;:.a;f nx a 8;f a 8;:.b
c all .a .a p'�' '*';c all .b .b p'�' '*';x all .a .b




In article <3bc6fbb9.263235532@News.CIS.DFN.DE>, Chuck Leviton says...
>
>Is there a compiler option or some other means when working on MVS
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>show which para PERFORMs which other para
```

```
John, 
Thanks for the useful input.  It takes me a step in the right
direction. But what I really need is a way to know which para Performs
which other para w/ appropriate indentations.

Couple of other things...
- The non blank character is different on different emulators.  On
mine I have to enter the [ key and it shows up as the non blank ï¿½ .  I
have copied and pasted it here in my posting but am not sure if it
will show up in everbody's newsreader/browser.  I guess it is ASCII
150 or something like that and so you can get it by depressing the ALT
key and some 3 digit number!

- By doing an "x all '*' 7", we can eliminate the comment lines too if
we find they clutter up the output.

I like your idea of storing PF Key settings in a PDS member.  If you
know you will be working in one shop for a long while, you can also
explore the keyboard macros that most emulators provide and can be
used most effectively (in case you are not doing so already!)

Thanks again
```

```
or would you be happy with simply
knowing for a particular paragraph who calls it?

program cross references.  The result
is an HTML page equivalent of the source
code with hot links for each name
```

#### ↳ Re: Structure Chart for cobol program

- **From:** Charles <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-16T17:09:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,bit.listserv.ibm-main
- **Message-ID:** `<OQZy7.31496$ev2.38016@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<3bc6fbb9.263235532@News.CIS.DFN.DE>`

```
On Fri, 12 Oct 2001 14:21:16 GMT, in article
<3bc6fbb9.263235532@News.CIS.DFN.DE>, Chuck Leviton stated 
>
>Is there a compiler option or some other means when working on MVS
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>show which para PERFORMs which other para
There is a Compiler option that shows everywhere a reference is in the program
for a paragraph or procedure name whether it is a go to or perform statement in
the reference section of the compile.
I use IBM COMPILER FOR VSE/ESA 1.1.1 (COBOL LE) WITH MAP AND CROSS REFERENCE
(FULL).

You could possible create a program that gave all of the perform statements a
level and make some sort of tree diagram, simply by reading the program and
listing every perform executed in each perform and then list everywhere each
perform was executed from.  In some cases certain papagraphs could be referenced
from multiple levels in a program.

The Problem is when dealing with external calls what would you want to do then?
CICS and on-line, interactive programs would be completely differrent if they
can call each other.
```

<a id="t-125f6b341d"></a>
## Thread: Structure of ISM files — 5 msgs

### Structure of ISM files

- **From:** Bryan Lawson <lawsonb@optushome.com.au>
- **Date:** 2001-10-13T17:20:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BC87777.85DEC58F@optushome.com.au>`

```
Hi all,

I need to extract data from some .ISM files. I am using Delphi.

Is it possible to interpret the structure from the header record or does
one need the cobol source?

I have searched high and low and can't even find any links on this
topic...

TIA,

Bryan
```

#### ↳ Re: Structure of ISM files

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-13T19:05:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BC891F4.CC200DFF@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3BC87777.85DEC58F@optushome.com.au>`

```


Bryan Lawson wrote:

> Hi all,
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Bryan

Bryan,

Did you mean to write ISAM (Indexed Sequential Access Mode) COBOL files ? If
that's the case, then you really do need the COBOL source of the indexed
file format. You referred to the 'header record' - Micro Focus ? All this
has is the 'housekeeping' that the run-time needs; it doesn't spell out the
record layout. .

The long way - if you have programs which print from this particular file -
is to do a print report and try and figure it out - but if numeric fields
are held as Comp-3 or other forms of binary, then you really do have a
problem.

If you can establish the file format, output a Sequential
Ascii-Delimited/CSV file, all fields separated by commas and additionally
text fields surrounded by quotes. Then Delphi will be able to read it in.

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: Structure of ISM files

- **From:** "JerryP" <news@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-13T23:01:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_I3y7.729425$NK1.66656502@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<3BC87777.85DEC58F@optushome.com.au>`

```

"Bryan Lawson" <lawsonb@optushome.com.au> wrote in message
news:3BC87777.85DEC58F@optushome.com.au...
> Hi all,
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> one need the cobol source?

If you mean ISAM, it is not possible to interpret the structure from either
the header record OR the COBOL source. Each vendor of COBOL has their own
proprietary ISAM format. You will need:

1. The original COBOL compiler and the original COBOL source to write a
COBOL program to reformat the existing file to a flat or text file; or

2. PERHAPS some utility that came with the COBOL compiler package that can
extract the data within the ISAM file; or

3. PERHAPS a third-party utility can do this if you know the COBOL compiler,
version, and platform on which the offending file was created.

Think of an ISAM file as a linked-list on steroids. Imbedded within the file
are data, linkage structures, and positioning pointers. It is a huge chore
to separate the data from the control information.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Structure of ISM files

- **From:** Bryan Lawson <lawsonb@optushome.com.au>
- **Date:** 2001-10-14T03:27:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BC905B5.BCCD2EB4@optushome.com.au>`
- **References:** `<3BC87777.85DEC58F@optushome.com.au> <_I3y7.729425$NK1.66656502@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
JerryP wrote:
> 
> "Bryan Lawson" <lawsonb@optushome.com.au> wrote in message
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> to separate the data from the control information.

James and Jerry,

thanks for the comments.

I believe they are ISAM files, it's just that the extension is .ISM and
the index file extension is .IDX

They are PC based hence the 3 letter extension.

Come to think of it, looking at the IDX file, I gather they are not
ISAM....

James, yes I get the feeling that they may be created by Micro Focus as
my searches have only come up with their name against ISM. 
There is definitely a header record. I can determine the individual
records using a hex viewer and the header record stands out clearly.

I have the contact details of one of the original programmers so maybe
he can knock up an export to flat file for us.

Thanks again,

Bryan
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Structure of ISM files

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-14T03:38:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BC90A15.5514D220@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3BC87777.85DEC58F@optushome.com.au> <_I3y7.729425$NK1.66656502@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3BC905B5.BCCD2EB4@optushome.com.au>`

```


Bryan Lawson wrote:

>
> I believe they are ISAM files, it's just that the extension is .ISM and
> the index file extension is .IDX

Bryan - yes that does look like Micro Focus with the 'idx" and that's a 'must
have' to identify the index records. The "ISM" you quoted, which is used for the
actual "Data" file, that can be whatever the originator wants to call it "dat",
"doc", "txt" or whatever - but MUST BE "idx" for the index file.

Yep if your contact can knock out a CSV for you, you are home and dry. Make sure
he gives you a description of the fields and sizes though, so you can setup Delphi
correctly.

Jimmy, Calgary AB.
```

```
Thanx
```

#### ↳ Re: newbie:copy"strecke"

- **From:** Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-13T20:04:40+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8tvgstst302tfhngvu3i2686ob9p8npc4f@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<9q9ts3$1n3$1@wrath.news.nacamar.de>`

```
Note to the group:  I reply in German to help this guy out

Hallo Thorsten, ein Copy-Strecke (denglisch) heisst auch oft copy-buch
(auch denglisch) oder copy-member (fuer PDS)

(alle fuerchterliches Deutsch)

Was es ist?  In C heisste es #include, in PLI %include.  Es ist
einfach ein Teil eines COBOL Programmes, der irgendwo abgespeichert
it.  Meistens handelt es sich um eine Satzbeschreibung

Man hat also eine Date mit Namen LGHSATZ (fuer Lohn und Gehalt Satz).
Diese mag den folgenden Inhalt haben

    02 LGH-Personal-Nr   PIC X(7).
    02 LGH-Name          PIC X(30).
    02 LGH-Gehalt        PIC S9(7)V99 COMP-3.
    ....

Im Programm LGH0001 steht dann etwa

WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
01 LGH-DATA.
   COPY LGHSATZ.

Damit wird dann beim Kompilieren des Programmes dies Copy-Strecke
LGH-Satz automatisch ins Programm LGH0001 eingefuegt und die folgende
Befehlsfolge funktioniert:

IF LGH-Name = 'Volker Bandke'
   MOVE 1000000 TO LGH-Gehalt
ELSE 
   MOVE 100000  TO LGH-Gehalt
END-IF

                                                            
     With kind Regards            |\      _,,,---,,_        
                            ZZZzz /,`.-'`'    -.  ;-;;,     
     Volker Bandke               |,4-  ) )-,_. ,\ (  `'-'   
      (BSP GmbH)                '---''(_/--'  `-'\_)        
                                                            
      "I saw _Lassie_. It took me four shows to figure out why the hairy kid never spoke. I mean, he could roll over and all that, but did that deserve a series?"  - the alien guy, in _Explorers_
      
        (Another Wisdom from my fortune cookie jar)
```


###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: newbie:copy"strecke"

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-10-15T03:18:07+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3bc9a073_1@news.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<9q9ts3$1n3$1@wrath.news.nacamar.de> <8tvgstst302tfhngvu3i2686ob9p8npc4f@4ax.com> <3bc8a379_7@news.newsgroups.com> <bleist8qesk2fult3082jgcghcnb23iph9@4ax.com>`

```
Volker,

I'm sure you are worth every penny (though maybe not QUITE as much as you
quoted in the code <G>)

I have to admit that there are days when I don't earn what they pay me; but
there are other days where I provide value far beyond what they pay me. On
the whole, I believe it works out fair for all concerned. (I also offer a
money back guarantee in cases where I have full project responsibility. So
far no one has claimed it, but I have sweated it on at least one
occasion...<G>)

Many years ago when I was a Company man, I wrote a program for a company in
Auckland which saved them over $200,000 the first time they ran it  and
about $30,000 each time they ran it after that (around every 4 months). (In
those days, that was a LOT of money...my annual salary at the time was under
$3000!) Nobody asked me to write it or specified it and I was a junior
programmer at the time. I just had an idea and did it.

My Boss called me into his office and told me that the (multi-millionaire)
owner of the Company said to say thanks and to increase my salary by $5 a
week.  After tax I received around $3.30. I was young, impetuous, and newly
married, so I told him where to put it and quit on the spot...<G>. I
immediately went freelance and nearly starved (there was virtually no
contract market in New Zealand in those days)... but it was better than
taking corporate charity.

Now, with hindsight and as a "grown-up" I can see both sides of the
argument; why would he pay more than he should, or indeed, pay ANYTHING, I
was just doing my job...?

(However, given the same situation, I would do (and have done when an IBM
Manager in England went back on his word over money) exactly the same again.
No discussion or argument; get your coat and head for the door... You either
have value or you don't. If the people you are working for can't see it or
are reluctant to recognise it, don't waste time on them, find someone else
who WILL recognise it. If you can't do this, then consider another line of
work...<G>)

I've relayed this anecdote here simply because many of us undervalue what we
do, others settle for whatever they are offered because they need work close
to home or whatever, and a very few realise that ALL they have going for
them is what they know and what they can do, and simply refuse to undersell
it. (These latter are usually freelance and mobile.)

Volker says: "very few people earn what they deserve"...

I say it is up to you to show what you deserve, negotiate a better deal, and
if you can't, then move.

Pete.
```

```
<excellent post snipped to save bandwidth>

Pete, 

In German we say:

Wer verdient schon was er verdient?

And it can be translated both ways, and both ways are intended to be
implied:

Who ever earns (the salary) he deserves

and

Who ever deserves (the salary) that he earns (= gets)


and both questions are supposed to be answered: no one


Works best in German where there is only one word for EARN and DESERVE


I once read (many winters ago) that French has 400000 words, Germans
has 600000, and English has 1000000, and I think I can believe this


>Volker,
>
>I'm sure you are worth every penny (though maybe not QUITE as much as you
>quoted in the code <G>)


>
>I have to admit that there are days when I don't earn what they pay me; but
```

- **From:** "Edwin Earl Ross" <EdwinEarl@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-17T23:48:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7Noz7.432$bE6.255173777@newssvr11.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<9qef5m$bqh$1@usenet.otenet.gr>`

```
Susan A. Allen gave some very good advice.

If you do not have a degree, jobs are harder to find. Many companies insist
on a degree, and will not hire people without one.

If you decide not to take a class, you will need a COBOL compiler with
associated documentation and a COBOL tutorial. The machine and operating
system you use will partly determine your choice of compiler and
documentation. Thus, you should post an email to comp.lang.cobol asking for
advice about which COBOL compiler to get for your computer and operating
system.
```

<a id="t-6c39a323a2"></a>
## Thread: Leap year logic — 34 msgs

_Topics: [`Date and calendar processing`](topics.md#dates)_

### Leap year logic

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-15T20:50:32-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9qg3lo$bps$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
As a follow up on another thread.

Yes, it is true that you ONLY need to handle "divide-by-4" to get the
correct leap year for 1901 thru 2099.  (You do not need to "worry about"
divide by 100 or divide by 400.)

HOWEVER, if you are dealing with people who might have been born before 1901
or might die after 2099, then you DO need to remember AT LEAST the 100 year
rule - and once you add that, you need the 400 year rule to get 2000
correct.

Certainly, we are not YET into the world of "30 year mortgages" - or other
COMMON "forward looking" business rules that need to deal with 2100 - but I
(personally) think that anyone who has ANY "business rules" that need to
know about leap years - SHOULD get it right - at least up to the "4000 year"
problem.

NOTE WELL: The question of dates being stored in items that will fail in
2034 (or whatever that year is) - is something that I would (again
personally) start "documenting" and finding out how your SYSTEM (OS) handles
sooner rather than latter.  30-year mortgages (and similar "look-ahead"
dates) are going to start running into that pretty SOON. If you are storing
"dates" as an integer from some "arbitrary" start date - and your field that
stores it will fail in 2004 (for 30-year in advance dates) then NOW seems
like the time to start fixing such problems - not 2003.
```

#### ↳ Re: Leap year logic

- **From:** "Tim Scrivens" <tim.scrivens@nz.eds.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-16T15:18:18+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9qg5db$fuu$1@hermes.nz.eds.com>`

```

year"

entitlements, but any one with birthdates, needs to go up to the 4000 rule
(which, if I understand it, is as far as you can go, because the 4000 rule
is not yet formulated).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Leap year logic

- **From:** Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-10-16T10:33:49-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BCC535C.3F406748@mb.sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<9qg3lo$bps$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <9qg5db$fuu$1@hermes.nz.eds.com>`

```
A question and a prediction:

- does anyone know if it's been settled whether 10,000 AD will be a leap year or
not??

(Let's plan for eternity.  9-character date fields from now on!  Seriously: I
picked up a date routine sometime around 1985 which allowed for the 4000-year
calculation.  Can't remember off-hand how it's handled but obviously the
question has been around for a long time).

- I predict that the next "millennial" problem will be not 3000AD but 2100AD.
Around the middle of this century (if programming remains a human function, if
new applications are being written - add all the usual caveats) people will
mutter "Why bother with the century?  This application won't be running that
long".  Human nature doesn't change much!  It's a pity that none of us will be
around to jeer and reminisce!  I do think, though, that it would be worth-while
to set up a specific archive of all the Y2K material, both for historical and
reference purposes.  Some readers may be surprised to find out that as long ago
as the early 1980's there were warnings of the problem; I recall one article (in
Computerworld) titled something like "The ticking time bomb in databases"; it
turned out to be a Y2K warning but I was disappointed because it wasn't
something new and exciting!

PL
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Leap year logic

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2001-10-16T15:57:23-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0110161457.5b4e3aca@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<9qg3lo$bps$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <9qg5db$fuu$1@hermes.nz.eds.com> <3BCC535C.3F406748@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
It is not possible to accurately predict the application of leap days
and seconds in the long term future, because of periodic and long term
variations in the earth's rotation and orbit.  Leap seconds cannot
even be accurately predicted for more than a few months in advance.

I think that the reason that SQL allows for two leap seconds per
minute is because the designers considered that they might be
necessary.  I also think that the reason that DB2 stores timestamps in
YYYYMMDD.HHMMSS.SSSSSS format, rather than as number of seconds or
days from a fixed reference point, is because the designers consider
the vagaries of the application of leap seconds and days cause too
many long term problems for long lived computer systems developed for
serious applications dealing with long term record keeping as could be
necessary for life assurance, mortgages, government records, etc. 
Provided that timestamps are held in a form that correctly identifies
the current date and time, routines to perform the calculations to
determine the durations between them can be relatively easily adapted
to accommodate any changes in the relationships between SI (UTC)
seconds, mean solar days and tropical years.

It is highly probable that the leap year rule would be sufficient to
get us to the year 4000 A.D., but thereafter the application of rules
have to accommodate the gradual increase in day length and decreases
in year length arising from long term one way trends caused by tidal
friction.  Even short term climatic variations affect the rate of
rotation, for example, more water in the atmosphere means that more
weight is distributed further from the earth's centre and the laws of
inertia result in a slower rate of rotation to conserve momentum.  A
warmer climate allows the atmosphere to hold more moisture.  There are
also the effects of glaciation and the Milankovitch cycles to
consider. plus the possible effects of events such as even relatively
minor asteroid impacts and very large volcanic eruptions such as that
of Krakatoa and its global climatic impact.

My own view is that we can not reasonably do better than use four
digit year numbers and eschew date recording mechanisms based upon
counts of seconds or days from a fixed reference point.  Scientists
will still have valid uses for such recording mechanisms, but they are
not suitable for the administrative calendar.  Then there would have
to be another massive reworking some time in advance of 10,000 A.D.,
when we will be long since gone and it will probably be done
automatically in carefully planned stages with no obvious impact on
ordinary day to day activities.

I have written a lot more on the subject, much of which can be seen at
www.jones0086.freeserve.co.uk under "calendars and computers", however
readers should be warned that it is incomplete and no warranty is
given that it is all correct.

Robert

Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote in message news:<3BCC535C.3F406748@mb.sympatico.ca>...
> A question and a prediction:
> 
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> PL
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Leap year logic

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-16T18:19:22-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9qif8h$bir$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<9qg3lo$bps$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <9qg5db$fuu$1@hermes.nz.eds.com> <3BCC535C.3F406748@mb.sympatico.ca> <fcd09c56.0110161457.5b4e3aca@posting.google.com>`

```
The question of Leap SECONDS (rather than leap DAYS) is truly an interesting
(and to me FRUSTRATING) one.

The draft of the next Standard introduces a new feature that it is HOPED
will allow for implementations (and operating systems) that do and do not
"know about" specific leap seconds (that may be either ADDED or SUBTRACTED -
from 60 seconds per minute).

While J4 (and WG4) were doing research into this, I think we found the
"international group" that is responsible for
 - declaring a specific leap second (or two - plus or minus)
 - "forecasting" when leap seconds will occur (in the next few years ONLY)

However, unlike leap days, there is DEFINITELY no way to "predict" when a
leap second (plus or minus) will occur at a "random" time in the future.  It
is based (as I understand it) on the ACTUAL calculated orbit of the Earth
around the sun - and how much "slippage" has and has not occurred.

Bottom-Line (IMHO)
 - You can/should be able to "calculate" leap days - in the past and future
using the divide by 4, 100, and 400 rules
 - You  can (but probably don't need to) have a "table" of leap seconds in
the past and immediate (1-3 years) future
 - There is no (currently available) way to handle leap seconds at a random
date in the future
 - If you have a "time" validation routine, then you PROBABLY could allow
for 58 to 62 seconds per minute - but would need to allow for non-60 values
for specific ("hard-coded") values for when the leap second occurs.
(Actually, there are specific days of the year when such seconds may be
declared - what varies is which of those days are "selected")
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Leap year logic

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org>
- **Date:** 2001-10-17T05:33:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BCD181A.D60D0F93@acm.org>`
- **References:** `<9qg3lo$bps$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <9qg5db$fuu$1@hermes.nz.eds.com> <3BCC535C.3F406748@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```


Peter Lacey wrote:
> 
> A question and a prediction:
> 
> - does anyone know if it's been settled whether 10,000 AD will be a leap year or
> not??

Not settled at this point.
A Gregorian date routine that allows for a "4000" leap year correction
is in error.  No such standard has been established.  Should the
rotation and orbit of the earth remain sufficiently constant, the
multiple of 400 at which the 4-100-400 rule needs another nudge to keep
it within 0.5 days is closest to a 3200 year cycle, so at this point the
most arithmetically correct proposal would actually be a 4-100-400-3200
year rule, not a 4-100-400-4000 rule.  But, since date systems are
created by political bodies and nothing has been officially adopted, any
comments on leap years after about 3200 are pure speculation (save for a
natural disaster, I think the built-in inertia of the existing systems
should protect us from any changes for leap years prior to 3200). 
Perhaps by 3200 the earth's rotation will have been changed by an
asteroid hit and the old leap year rule completely invalidated; or
perhaps the growing error will just be tolerated until it becomes too
great and several days have to be cut out of one year to get things back
in sync (perhaps in the year 10,000 as part of the massive Y10K 5-digit
year problem solution).  Hey, at the earliest we still have at least
1189 years left before 3190 A.D., when we have to get serious and make a
decision and get started on changing leap year code. NO PROBLEM!! :)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Leap year logic

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-10-18T06:56:28+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BCE6F0C.5FF6E845@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<9qg3lo$bps$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <9qg5db$fuu$1@hermes.nz.eds.com> <3BCC535C.3F406748@mb.sympatico.ca> <3BCD181A.D60D0F93@acm.org>`

```
Joel C. Ewing wrote:

> 1189 years left before 3190 A.D., when we have to get serious and make a
> decision and get started on changing leap year code. NO PROBLEM!! :)

While it is a 3200 year 'cycle' (approx), it is not zero based.  1584 is
the year which had 'correct' alignment. So if 3200 is decided as the
cyclic correction then it will be half a day out by 3184.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Dweebiest argument (was Re: Leap year logic)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-10-18T09:49:10-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BCEEBE5.67F95EBA@mb.sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<9qg3lo$bps$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <9qg5db$fuu$1@hermes.nz.eds.com> <3BCC535C.3F406748@mb.sympatico.ca> <3BCD181A.D60D0F93@acm.org> <3BCE6F0C.5FF6E845@Azonic.co.nz> <Tnlz7.202$wQ1.38468@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Michael Mattias wrote:

>
> Ok, bouys and gales, time for an instant poll:"What is dweebiest?"
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well, all programmers belong to one of those three groups!

IMHO: "endless discussions of leap-year logic ..." is the LEAST dweeby since it
is theoretical and interesting.  When I posted my original remark (about
10,000AD) I wasn't aware of the need to consider the rotation of the earth
(although it will be some considerable time before the leap seconds etc. add up
to anything) so I have learned something.  The other three are truly stupid
since they reflect personal styles and consequently are amenable to rational
debate.

What should be condemned (instead of having fun poked at, as here) is the urging
of restrictive programming styles, forbidding use of various elements of the
language, simply because the urger is used to doing it that way.  ANY style is
permissible, as long as the code lives up to "understandable and easy to
maintain".  (By "easy to maintain", I mean maintainable by anybody conversant
with COBOL, regardless of his/her preferred style).

PL
```

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-10-19T17:49:31+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3bcfb178_1@news.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<9qg3lo$bps$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <9qg5db$fuu$1@hermes.nz.eds.com> <3BCC535C.3F406748@mb.sympatico.ca> <3BCD181A.D60D0F93@acm.org> <3BCE6F0C.5FF6E845@Azonic.co.nz> <Tnlz7.202$wQ1.38468@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <3BCEEBE5.67F95EBA@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
Sorry Peter,

I strongly disagree with your post.

See impassioned (and deadly serious) plea below...

Pete.

"Peter Lacey" <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:3BCEEBE5.67F95EBA@mb.sympatico.ca...

> What should be condemned (instead of having fun poked at, as here) is

ABSOLUTELY EVERYTHING that goes on here CAN and SHOULD have fun poked at it
ON SOME OCCASIONS!

It is ONLY computer programming, for Chrissake! It is NOT life and death.

I cannot condone the humourless comdemnation of the viewpoints I don't
happen to agree with. I would be sad if people reacted to my own arguments
in this way (I'd rather have the piss taken out of me than have some pompous
prat launch into a tirade defending passionately his entrenched position, as
if his life depended on it.)

Humour is NECESSARY. For our well being.

There is a time for serious discussion, and yes, we can all learn something
at times, but the poking of fun restores balance and lightens up what could
easily be a boring, dry, soulless forum.

People of a humourless disposition are going to have a hard time here...
(Certainly as long as I'm contributing...)

Many of the suggested solutions to things make ME smile; sometimes I realise
when I read my own suggestions that they are comical and I re-phrase or
re-formulate them before posting (OK, so a few slip through anyway...deal
with it! <G>

What we do is important. (It is for people who are NOT computer programmers,
too). But don't let's get so carried away with our own importance that we
become pompous, arrogant , and humourless.

"Those without a sense of humour are at the mercy of the rest of us..."

Pete.
```

```
are over 50,000 women beaten by their boyfriends or husbands every year?"

She hit me! That's exactly what she did! She hit me. Right on the head.
```

#### ↳ Metric calendar, Was: Leap year logic

- **From:** Herwig Huener <Herwig.Huener@fujitsu-siemens.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-16T13:02:10+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BCC13B2.F4D7F7C@fujitsu-siemens.com>`
- **References:** `<9qg3lo$bps$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
2001-10-16 13:00:00 MEST

"William M. Klein" schrieb:
> 
> As a follow up on another thread.

> ...

Decades ago I thought about making the calendar metric
- as a test-case, we could try a metric calendar in
only parts of the world. This might be possible,
as the example of the US shows, where inches
and miles and such things are still in use.

Maybe economic wellfare would be improved by the
absence of leap-years?

Programming languages should easily adapt to any
calendar. However, talking serious, we have already
a metric calendar: It is the so-called
"Julian Date", invented by astronomers long ago.
With Julian Date, there will be no leap-year nor
leap-day for the rest of eternity.

If you want to look at the Julian date, here is how:
Install emacs. Although an editor, it knows
*all* kinds of calendars.

Today at noon UTM is JD=2452199.

Herwig
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Metric calendar, Was: Leap year logic

- **From:** "JerryP" <news@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-16T12:29:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LKVy7.512422$Lw3.31320020@news2.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<9qg3lo$bps$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <3BCC13B2.F4D7F7C@fujitsu-siemens.com>`

```
We could go back to the Jewish calendar. It doesn't add a day every so often
except every now and again.

Three times every eighteen years it adds a whole month.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Metric calendar, Was: Leap year logic

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-10-16T10:08:25-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<U6Xy7.8997$7Y4.1415092@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<9qg3lo$bps$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <3BCC13B2.F4D7F7C@fujitsu-siemens.com> <LKVy7.512422$Lw3.31320020@news2.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
I like the idea of 13 months, 28 days each, plus "new years day" added as
the last day of the year.  New years day would not be a monday, tuesday,
etc., but stand alone and be a breakout party day. On leap years, we would
make a "leap years day" and extent the holiday to two days.
That way, the 1st, 8th, 15th, and 22nd would always be a monday, the 2nd a
tuesday, etc.  Holidays would always fall on the same day of the week, and
we would all get 24 extra days off each century.

"JerryP" <news@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:LKVy7.512422$Lw3.31320020@news2.aus1.giganews.com...
> We could go back to the Jewish calendar. It doesn't add a day every so
often
> except every now and again.

…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Metric calendar, Was: Leap year logic

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-17T21:50:16-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ihlrstouo2i1c9m66lu8mjk75vji311iho@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<9qg3lo$bps$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <3BCC13B2.F4D7F7C@fujitsu-siemens.com> <LKVy7.512422$Lw3.31320020@news2.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
On Tue, 16 Oct 2001 12:29:31 GMT "JerryP" <news@bisusa.com> wrote:

:>We could go back to the Jewish calendar. It doesn't add a day every so often
:>except every now and again.

:>Three times every eighteen years it adds a whole month.

I don't have the entire calendar cycle in front of me, but here is how it
works. 10 months are always the same number of days (either 29 or 30).
Cheshvan and Kislev can be either 29 or 30 days. The "standard" years has
Cheshvan with 29 and Kislev with 30. The "short" year has both at 29 and the
"long" years both at 30. This calendar adjustment, sometimes together with the
leap month (which is always 30 days) allows the court to avoid holidays
occurring on inconvenient days (such as Yom Kippur on Sunday or Friday). This
adjustment is not a problem in Jewish law as unlike Saturday which is the
seventh day and cannot be altered, the month required the court to declare it.
And the month starts whenever the court declares it, even if the court
deliberately errs.

As a side point there us a calendar creep in the Jewish calendar as well. The
prayers for rain outside Israel start 60 days after the "Tekufa" when fall
starts. The calculated calendar has the four year rule but not the century
rule - thus it is creeping later every century (except Y2K, naturally) and
eventually will be close to Passover which is illogical. Obviously the Messiah
will come and the calendar will return to observation before then.
```
