```
"SkippyPB" <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com>
>
> After the Messiah comes, I don't believe there will be a need for a
> calendar.
>

It depends on which calendar you use:

If you use the Gregorian calendar, the (Christian) Messiah will take
everyone (or a lot, anyway) to heaven.

If you're using the Hebrew calendar, the (Jewish) Messiah (actually, there
are two) will institute God's law on earth and do away with haggling over
the calendar (plus some other stuff).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Metric calendar, Was: Leap year logic

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-18T20:14:53-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9qnv1n01qj7@enews3.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<9qg3lo$bps$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <3BCC13B2.F4D7F7C@fujitsu-siemens.com> <LKVy7.512422$Lw3.31320020@news2.aus1.giganews.com> <ihlrstouo2i1c9m66lu8mjk75vji311iho@4ax.com>`

```

"Binyamin Dissen" <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote in
message news:ihlrstouo2i1c9m66lu8mjk75vji311iho@4ax.com...
> ....                        This calendar adjustment,
sometimes together with the
> leap month (which is always 30 days) allows the court to avoid
holidays
> occurring on inconvenient days (such as Yom Kippur on Sunday
or Friday). This
> adjustment is not a problem in Jewish law as unlike Saturday
which is the
> seventh day and cannot be altered, the month required the
court to declare it.
> And the month starts whenever the court declares it, even if
the court
> deliberately errs.

There are provisions for pushing back the first day of the year.
if the molad tishri (first new moon of the new year) hits after
6PM (probably in Israel) the first day of the year is put off to
the next day.

> As a side point there us a calendar creep in the Jewish
calendar as well.

Perhaps the moladic period has a bit of imprecision, but the
system is based upon the lunar cycle.  So in that sense, at
lease, the calendar should not creep.
```

<a id="t-657d4652c1"></a>
## Thread: Job advice for old hack, please? — 23 msgs

_Topics: [`Jobs, careers, recruiting, salary`](topics.md#jobs)_

### Job advice for old hack, please?

- **From:** Jim Smith <longusername@NOSPAMhotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-16T14:51:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9qhhhd$o0tfh$8@ID-34145.news.dfncis.de>`

```
Hello,

I need advice about getting a job. I have five year's experience COBOL 
programming. So why do I need advice, right? Because it was ten years ago, 
my experience, that is. I'm basically pretty despondent about the hope of 
getting a job in the field again. I emailed an agent on Sunday with a link 
to my old resume and he didn't even reply.

I was learning a whole bunch of stuff to do with the web, including 
programming in Perl, PHP and Java and then I noticed there are just about 
as many Cobol as Java jobs going in dublin, Ireland, right now. I live in 
Northern Ireland (aha). But much of the time folks are asking for degrees 
now. I don't have one of those. I quit Cobol programming not because I was 
no good, but because I wanted to do something else. I don't expect anyone 
to take my word for it, but oh well.

So what do you folks think are the honest to god chances for an old hack 
like me. (now 40). This is starting to get serious. I may have to , like, 
wash dishes or something, at this rate. Also, I got a copy of COBOL 
Unleashed, first edition and then discovered from archived posts from this 
group that the Fujitsu compiler doesn't work for anybody else either. Could 
anybody ftp me the trial version from edition 2, per chance? Just a 
thought. I ask for a lot, don't I. :-)

Would be grateful for some help/encouragement/harsh truth on the first 
question, though.

Thanks,

Jim Smith
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
> Jim Smith

I had a somewhat similar experience. I was out of
COBOL for about a year and
a half and when I wanted to get back in I ran into
a some wall. The initial interest
was there because I worked at several large well
know firms but it died when they
discovered I hadn't coded COBOL for a year and a
half. I was fortunate enough to
find a firm that was willing to over look this and
give me a chance. It's not the best
pay or benefits but I'm back in and that's what
matters right now. In addition I have a
four year degree out of the University of Texas so
it did help open up some doors.

All I can say is good luck and keep plugging.

Jeff...
```

#### ↳ Re: Job advice for old hack, please?

- **From:** Charles <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-16T15:57:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VNYy7.31394$ev2.37841@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<9qhhhd$o0tfh$8@ID-34145.news.dfncis.de>`

```
On 16 Oct 2001 14:51:26 GMT, in article
<9qhhhd$o0tfh$8@ID-34145.news.dfncis.de>, Jim Smith stated 
>
When I was 37, I went back to school because I was laid off from the factory and
went to school to get into the computer field.  I now work for a Community
College as a COBOL programmer.

Knowing COBOL and JAVA may be useful.  Some of the new COBOL compilers for the
PC use OOP.  I would guess a lot of jobs go to contractors and developers.  Look
for some of the local developers and see if they have temporarty contract jobs
available.  If you knew CICS and some Database system that would be a plus
anywhere.

Try banking, investment firms, the government, and almost every company in your
area.  Find a local job specialist who knows how to evaluate your resume.  There
are still COBOL jobs out there, but it could take a few months to find them.  If
you get rejected ask why, in a nice way, and then ask for leads on jobs for
other companies in your area.  Not all jobs are advertised.  Follow up on the
resume and ask for further leads.

If you cant find anything right away, taking some classes couldn't hurt.  Just
about all big corporations want people with experience, but they can't always
find someone with experience; never assume a very talented person with
experience is going to apply.  Many places have to accept people with less than
a Bachelor's Degree.  Every country is different, in the US sometimes we have
govt programs for layed off workers to get further training; if something like
that is available it might be worth it.  You could always go into networking.  A
few courses in Oracle might lead to a certificate program.

Maybe you are not just a COBOL programmer, but a systems analyst or a software
developer.  That is about the same thing, some people like fancier names.


>Hello,
>
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>Jim Smith
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Job advice for old hack, please?

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-10-18T12:20:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1Ozz7.70$6R6.11916@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<9qhhhd$o0tfh$8@ID-34145.news.dfncis.de> <VNYy7.31394$ev2.37841@www.newsranger.com>`

```
Charles <nospam@newsranger.com> wrote in message
news:VNYy7.31394$ev2.37841@www.newsranger.com...
>
> Maybe you are not just a COBOL programmer, but a systems analyst or a
software
> developer.  That is about the same thing, some people like fancier names.


Sure, many people throw around terms like "programmer" or
"programmer/analyst" or "developer"  without much regard for consistency in
meaning, but I'd be real careful  about saying "[they are] about the same
thing."

I'll hazard a guess that most of the independent consultants here can tell
stories of clients who have staff  "programmers" who can program and program
well, but who are not able to to the design, development and system
integration. That's why consultants have any work at all: because there are
times when staff resources are inadequate to the task, and outside help is
needed.

Shakespeare had a point when he said, "That which we call a rose would by
any other name smell as sweet;" but so did my grandfather when he opined,
"You can put a sow in an evening gown and makeup and call her Monique, but
she'll still be a pig."

MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: Job advice for old hack, please?

- **From:** Herwig Huener <Herwig.Huener@fujitsu-siemens.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-16T18:43:18+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BCC63A6.EED1FE30@fujitsu-siemens.com>`
- **References:** `<9qhhhd$o0tfh$8@ID-34145.news.dfncis.de>`

```
2001-10-16 18:37:56 MESZ

Jim Smith schrieb:
> 
> ...

Why don't you sell what you did in your ten years of no-Cobol?
That is also a matter of fancier names - "forrestry comission
lumber agent" might be better than "lumberjack", "local
traffic optimizing management" might be better than "taxi-driving",
"food preparation specialist" might be better than "cook", or
"on her majesty's service" might be better than "on the dole".

I do not know what sounds good because that needs much more
familiarity with your language than I have - but the principles
of impressing management are the same everywhere.

Also, 40 is no age.

Herwig (50)
```

#### ↳ Re: Job advice for old hack, please?

- **From:** WDS@WDS.WDS.5 (WDS)
- **Date:** 2001-10-17T23:00:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3bce0d9a.27980468@news1.frb.gov>`
- **References:** `<9qhhhd$o0tfh$8@ID-34145.news.dfncis.de>`

```
On 16 Oct 2001 14:51:26 GMT, Jim Smith wrote:

>I need advice about getting a job. I have five year's experience COBOL 
>programming. 
[snippage]
>                        But much of the time folks are asking for degrees 
>now. I don't have one of those. 

Try applying anyway.  I have, on several occasions, seen situations
where an employer *wants* a college degree, specific certifications,
certain experience/skills/whatever; they don't always get exactly what
they want.

Good luck on your search.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Job advice for old hack, please?

- **From:** "Rufio" <davecawdell@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-17T23:14:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Ehoz7.13723$B72.2750673@news1.rdc1.az.home.com>`
- **References:** `<9qhhhd$o0tfh$8@ID-34145.news.dfncis.de> <3bce0d9a.27980468@news1.frb.gov>`

```

"WDS" <WDS@WDS.WDS.5> wrote in message
news:3bce0d9a.27980468@news1.frb.gov...
> On 16 Oct 2001 14:51:26 GMT, Jim Smith wrote:
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I defy anyone to prove that someone with years of experience and a college
degree is any better than someone with the same experience & no degree.
Anytime I see a job, I don't care if they say they need a degree - if 14
years full-time COBOL ain't good enough, they can end up hiring the "senior"
p/a with 2 years experience & a degree instead.

The only time a degree tells you anything is in juniors, where it is
reasonable evidence of a minimum of experience. Of course if it's a US
B.Sc., it may only be equivalent to a UK Associate Degree, and a US B.Sc.
hons may only be equivalent to a UK B.Sc. etc. So even having a degree isn't
the perfect evidence.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Job advice for old hack, please?

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@paralynx.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-17T18:48:38-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BCE34F6.1C33@paralynx.com>`
- **References:** `<9qhhhd$o0tfh$8@ID-34145.news.dfncis.de> <3bce0d9a.27980468@news1.frb.gov> <Ehoz7.13723$B72.2750673@news1.rdc1.az.home.com>`

```
Rufio wrote:
> 
> "WDS" <WDS@WDS.WDS.5> wrote in message
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> the perfect evidence.

That will depend upon the school.  Some US universities have VERY good computing science 
programs.  Some UK schools might have poor ones. When I worked in the UK, there was one 
private school, the F***H INSTITUTE OF DATA PROCESSING (name changed to protect me from 
getting sued by the guilty) which talked a great story and charged high fees.  Nobody I 
knew in the industry would ever touch one of their graduates.

As a former government IT manager I can tell you that the first winnowing of the 100 
resumes which typically come in for a given job is designed to reduce the pile as 
quickly as possible.  Anybody without decent paper qualifications didn't make it to the 
second round.  OK, I might have missed some non-documented gold nugget, but I didn't 
have the time to dig.
```

```
been through vocational training and are better than grads -
others........aren't.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Job advice for old hack, please?

```
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I would consider anyone working in the field of programming or database design
for 5 years to have the equivilent of a Bachelor's Degree.  Most of what I learn
is school is just a bunch of useless terms like name the 6 characteristics of a
database.

However it has to be real experience, not warming your rear end on a chair as a
help-desk technician.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Job advice for old hack, please?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-18T17:40:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BCF15A6.5F5EFF2A@home.com>`
- **References:** `<9qhhhd$o0tfh$8@ID-34145.news.dfncis.de> <3bce0d9a.27980468@news1.frb.gov> <Ehoz7.13723$B72.2750673@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <3BCE34F6.1C33@paralynx.com>`

```


Ed Guy wrote:

>
> As a former government IT manager I can tell you that the first winnowing of the 100
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> have the time to dig.

Which means of course, yours truly with only Oxford School Certificate (1949) , some 10
years in systems analysis and self taught in COBOL, would NEVER have made it to your short
list. No wonder I gave up even trying here in Calgary !

In my capacity as a Systems Manager I reviewed all applications from analysts. To be fair in
commerce, I wasn't inundated with reams of applicants - but I did look at them all - short
listing them on expertise, not academic qualifications. And EVERYBODY got an acknowledgment
- not the pig ignorant way things are handled today !

I had one gnome like figure, qualified as a Chartered Secretary, (corporate law/accounting),
arrive for an interview - I'm afraid he was 'dead in the water' as soon as I looked at him -
regardless of qualifications, he just didn't have the appearance of somebody you would want
to throw at end users, with the ability to create an aura of credibility.

Programmer recruitment I left to my Chief Programmer - and naturally he was looking for
specific skills, ideally ICL mainframes and COBOL, but he didn't ignore COBOL from other
environments.

PS: Way back then (1949) - out of a class of 30, perhaps 3 made it to university - assuming
their parents could afford to keep them there. Mind you the 'old school tie' worked - I
could have articled as a CA, (with one of the 'old boys' from college), with my parents
paying up front for articling and getting back a stipend of a shilling a week, (12
pennies),  for 5 years slave labour.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Job advice for old hack, please?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@paralynx.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-18T13:47:37-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BCF3FE9.31B1@paralynx.com>`
- **References:** `<9qhhhd$o0tfh$8@ID-34145.news.dfncis.de> <3bce0d9a.27980468@news1.frb.gov> <Ehoz7.13723$B72.2750673@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <3BCE34F6.1C33@paralynx.com> <3BCF15A6.5F5EFF2A@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> 
> Ed Guy wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

When I was hiring in Calgary (in the 1970s) I preferred to hire people with the SAIT 
diploma over U of C graduates.

U of C noticed this trend and a bunch of us were invited to meet with U of C faculty to 
explain why.  We told them that a SAIT graduate could be put to work immediately, even 
before they found their way to the washroom.  The university lot needed three month's 
training.  At that time Calgary was almost exclusively a COBOL town and yet the U of C 
curriculum included NO COBOL.

Actually, at one time I even had a secretary with a SAIT computer diploma - I knew we 
had a programming vacancy coming up in three months and I wanted her on hand for it!
```

## Thread: Install Fujitsu COBOL 4 on Windows 2000 — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Install Fujitsu COBOL 4 on Windows 2000

- **From:** "Stevan P. Knapp, MCSE, MCP+I" <steve@bloonin.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-16T17:56:29-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9qk8pr$ap6$1@ins22.netins.net>`

```
Anybody have step-by-step instructions to install Fujitsu COBOL 4 on a
Windows 2000 machine? It tells me that I need the NT service pack 3 or
higher and quits.
```

#### ↳ Re: Install Fujitsu COBOL 4 on Windows 2000

- **From:** "mkozelka" <m_kozelka@ameritech.net>
- **Date:** 2001-10-17T17:26:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fbjz7.1861$Rw2.1131303@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<9qk8pr$ap6$1@ins22.netins.net>`

```
Per the Fujitsu Website:

Fujitsu COBOL V4 will not install on the Microsoft Windows 2000 operating
system. If you attempt to do this you will receive the following message:
"NT Service Pack 3 must be installed before Fujitsu can be installed"


Go to http://msdn.microsoft.com/compatibility/ where you should be able to
find the "Application Compatibility Toolkit".


Download the Toolkit and go from there. The file at time of writing is
named act.exe. After installing it, it will likely enable you to run several
programs that may not otherwise be compatible with Win 2000.

If this does not work, you will have to purchase Version 6.1. If you are a
student, there are academic licenses available.
```

- **References:** `<9qk8pr$ap6$1@ins22.netins.net>`


## Thread: Converting Cobol to C — 12 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### Converting Cobol to C

- **From:** "Peter Nolan" <peternolan9@eircom.net>
- **Date:** 2001-10-17T13:47:13+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<z9fz7.7747$w5.48598@news.indigo.ie>`

```
Hi All,
this is possibly not a 'popular' place to ask this question..

I have a bunch of cobol with imbedded SQL statements doing simple things
like read a file and update/insert into tables etc.  I want to convert it to
C. (No flames please).

I was wondering if anyone attached to this newsgroup had experience at cobol
to C conversions for batch processing style programs.

If so, could you let me know directly?  I'd be interested to talk with
anyone who has done this before.

Thanks

Peter Nolan
```

#### ↳ Re: Converting Cobol to C

- **From:** "Peter Nolan" <peternolan9@eircom.net>
- **Date:** 2001-10-17T18:38:42+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Rqjz7.71$8s4.247@news.indigo.ie>`
- **References:** `<z9fz7.7747$w5.48598@news.indigo.ie>`

```
Hi PaulR,
I got your email but when I have tried to email you directly it bounces with
a message saying an error in remote mailer.  I have seen this happen before.
You you have another email address? (Rather than put a long post here)
Thanks
Peter
```

#### ↳ Re: Converting Cobol to C

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-10-18T07:11:39+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BCE729B.4310CE1C@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<z9fz7.7747$w5.48598@news.indigo.ie>`

```
Peter Nolan wrote:
> 
> I have a bunch of cobol with imbedded SQL statements doing simple things
> like read a file and update/insert into tables etc.  I want to convert it to
> C. (No flames please).

This is not a flame, but just asks why would you want to do this.

It won't be any faster in C than the Cobol speed because this is going
to be a factor of the disk system.

It won't be more maintainable (probably worse).  There was an argument
raised a decade or so ago where a survey had found that there was a
higher proportion of Cobol programmers doing 'maintenance' than C
programmers.  The bogus conclusion was that if the Cobol code was
converted to C the programmers would do less maintenance and more new
development.

This was a completely incompetent conclusion because 'Cobol maintenance'
is primarily to keep the system reflecting changes in the business model
and is a result of the types of applications that Cobol is useful for. 
The majority of C programs tend not to need changing due to changes in
business and 'maintenence' is primarily bug-fixing.

Change an application from Cobol to C and it still requires maintenece
to the business model _and_ it requires bug-fixing.

Also, if it is a convertion, rather than a complete rewrite, you just
get Cobol programs written in the C language and you do not get C
programs.  The advantages of Cobol are lost in the convertion but any
advantages that C has (localisation, modularity, conciseness,
reusability through libraries, etc) are not gained.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Converting Cobol to C

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-10-17T19:56:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Tnlz7.201$wQ1.38654@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<z9fz7.7747$w5.48598@news.indigo.ie> <3BCE729B.4310CE1C@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3BCE729B.4310CE1C@Azonic.co.nz...
> Peter Nolan wrote:
> >
> > I have a bunch of cobol with imbedded SQL statements doing simple things
> > like read a file and update/insert into tables etc.  I want to convert
it to
> > C. (No flames please).
>
> This is not a flame, but just asks why would you want to do this.

I'm obviously not the original poster, but the legitimate possibilities
include...

1. Migrating to a platform where "C" is available and COBOL is not.
2. Migrating to "C" due to personnel changes: COBOL people have left, C
people have arrived.
3. Bad outside consultant trying to justify his existence; this consultant
is, coincidentally, the owner's brother.

MCM
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Converting Cobol to C

- **From:** "Peter Nolan" <peternolan9@eircom.net>
- **Date:** 2001-10-17T22:01:48+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gpmz7.193$8s4.1153@news.indigo.ie>`
- **References:** `<z9fz7.7747$w5.48598@news.indigo.ie> <3BCE729B.4310CE1C@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Richard,
no reason so sinister....

Simply put, very few (if any) people under the age of 30 speak cobol on unix
boxes nowadays.  If I want to use this code again and have it developed or
maintained by grads with <5 years experience then it is going to have to be
in C.....

I personally understand all the arguements about both, and have used cobol
on unix many times......but I think it is time to use C on unix/NT.

I agree, all I will finish up with is cobol programs written in C.  After
all, the programs are just file processing programs....what cobol was always
good at.

Peter
```

<a id="t-ad60eed299"></a>
## Thread: Need Help VA COBOL Compiler — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Need Help VA COBOL Compiler

- **From:** "Denis A. Chesnokov" <ches@tercom.ru>
- **Date:** 2001-10-17T17:05:42+03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9qjvmv$2atl$1@news.spbu.ru>`

```
Hello all. How change working directory from current to MyDirectory for VA
Cobol compiler (cob2.exe) if i work from command-line?
Denis
```

#### ↳ Re: Need Help VA COBOL Compiler

- **From:** Gary Mazo <mazoi@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-17T15:25:43-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BCE0567.89ED0C7C@yahoo.com>`
- **References:** `<9qjvmv$2atl$1@news.spbu.ru>`

```
Denis,
It depends on what you mean by "working directory".  There are
many files in many directories used by cob2.  Location of some
can be controlled by environment variables, some cannot be controlled at all.
The compiler listing (starting with Version 3 of the compiler) can be
controlled
by COBLSTDIR variable.  Object file goes into the current directory and there
is no way to
control that. Location for the executable file could be controlled with the
/OUT option
for the linker.
Compiler work files go into the path specified in the TEMP
environment variable or the current directory if TEMP is not specified or is
invalid.

The best way to do what you want
is to cd into MyDirectory and execute the cob2 from there.

Uspehov - Good luck.

Gary

"Denis A. Chesnokov" wrote:

> Hello all. How change working directory from current to MyDirectory for VA
> Cobol compiler (cob2.exe) if i work from command-line?
> Denis
```

## Thread: HELP: Conversion — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### HELP: Conversion

- **From:** Trimegisto <trimegisto@rocketmail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-17T15:25:58+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lt4rst8f83ocj99u3a1g8kfls3dgf4fgfq@4ax.com>`

```
Hi,

My company's former programmer has retired and left some of our
applications writen in some form of Cobol. 

The data files don't have any extension but they all have a file with
the same name but with the .idx extension.

What kind of format is this? Are the .idx files indexes?

I want to reuse some this old data but don't even know where to start.

Can someone point me in the right direction?

Thank you!
```

#### ↳ Re: HELP: Conversion

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-17T17:14:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BCDBDF0.E350F34F@home.com>`
- **References:** `<lt4rst8f83ocj99u3a1g8kfls3dgf4fgfq@4ax.com>`

```


Trimegisto wrote:

> Hi,
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thank you!

With an extension of "idx" it's likely you are talking about Micro Focus
COBOL (???). Yes, the 'idx' file is an index of the records in the file
you have without a suffix

    - Customers       = data file
    - Customers.idx = index to Customers' data

Records are written to the first file, Sequentially, 'adding to the tail'.
To get key order, that's where the index file comes in.

You will need to look at his source programs to find the format for the
data file. Look at the top of the programs for file definitions - he may
have used :-

    copy "customer.cpy" - in which case the copy file will give you the
data format.

You don't say how you want to re-use it. In something other than COBOL ?
If so you will need to write COBOL programs to create CSV files which you
can read into another language or a Database. If your installation doesn't
know COBOL, then you are going to need to sub-contract to get those COBOL
CSVs written.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: HELP: Conversion

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@paralynx.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-17T11:25:05-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BCDCD01.16CB@paralynx.com>`
- **References:** `<lt4rst8f83ocj99u3a1g8kfls3dgf4fgfq@4ax.com> <3BCDBDF0.E350F34F@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> 
> Trimegisto wrote:
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
> CSVs written.

Or else you can use a generic parser like ParseRat (http://www.parserat.com/) set in 
"generic binary" mode. It (and its competitors) can take the file and put out CSV, DBF, 
XML etc. and also handle conversion of any packed or binary numbers if needed.
```

```
Hi!

Regards
Karl Jonsson
"Trimegisto" <trimegisto@rocketmail.com> skrev i meddelandet
news:lt4rst8f83ocj99u3a1g8kfls3dgf4fgfq@4ax.com...
> Hi,
```

#### ↳ Re: HELP: Conversion

- **From:** roblec <roblec@cinci-rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-19T13:40:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BD02D56.A4B5D5B0@cinci-rr.com>`
- **References:** `<lt4rst8f83ocj99u3a1g8kfls3dgf4fgfq@4ax.com>`

```
It certainly sounds like these are MicroFocus files.  If you do not have
access to the source files (or the file definition libraries) you can
possibly figure out the layout of these data files yourself.  It is
difficult but not impossible.

Dismiss the idx files.  All of the data is in the files without
extensions.  Each data file has a 128 byte header. After you've stripped
off the header, the data records themselves are PROBABLY in fixed-length
record format.  Most programmers will choose fixed-length if it is at
all possible due to ease of use and having an eye on future conversions
(which happens quite often).

Assuming fixed length, use a good file viewer (I rely heavily on Vern
Buerg's LIST.COM) and try various values for the record length until you
can see obvious repetitive columns of like data.  Generally you can
recognize fields such as dates, names & addresses, etc.  Some may be
stored as binary and will appear as extended character symbols.  These
will require further analysis and conversion later on.

Each record begins with a two-byte header. Use this record header to
identify the start of each record.  If the file uses variable-length
records the job becomes much more difficult.  In that case, the record
header is four bytes, with the second word (bytes 3+4) being the binary
value of each individual record's length, and remember the bytes may be
swapped (little-endian or whatever).

It is also possible that the data file is compressed, which would make
deciphering the format nearly impossible.  Fortunately, MicroFocus
introduced record compression around the same time in history that
larger disk drives became available, and again with an eye on future
conversion many programmers opted to NOT use such compression.

If you still have the original compiler available, there should be a
rebuild utility laying around.  It is named REBUILD.EXE and can be used
to identify the file layout using rebuild/i, and also can be used to
convert the data to flat files for import.  If you do not have Rebuild
available, some folks here in this group might be willing to provide you
with different versions until you find one the same rev as your data
files.  If you know when the original programs were first written that
would help also.

Good Luck!
-Rob


Trimegisto wrote:
> 
> Hi,
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thank you!
```

```
Gibt's hier keinen!

In dieses forum bitte, auf Englisch posten...Wir haben hier viele leute vom
ganzen Welt, und die meistens Englisch verstehen...niemand werde dein
Englisch kritisieren. (Mein Deutsch ist schrecklich, aber das macht
nichts... dein Englisch, gleichfalls!..<G>)

Web Enablement in COBOL can currently be achieved by using CGI code or ASAPI
code.

Fujitsu are saying that they will provide a NETCobol product and this will
generate .NET intermediate code, just like any other product aimed at .NET.

This product will not be available publicly before 2002 (Beta versions are
currently being tested.)

If you wish to find short courses in German for Web Enablement in COBOL, try
posting to German Language COBOL forums.

If you have specific problems, post here and we will help...

Good Luck!

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Suche einen deutschen "Schnellkurs" für COBOL im Web! o.T.

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-10-23T03:33:46+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3bd42f6f_2@news.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<9qm57c$p6f4f$1@ID-109843.news.dfncis.de> <3bceaf9b_1@news.newsgroups.com> <fhhvst49o6n0fv46fhs7ieaaf1ikmpes4t@4ax.com>`

```
Volker,

I posted the following on the 19th. According to my system it was sent, but
my Newsreader shows no such message.

========= Begin quote from the 19th ==============
Thanks Volker.

Pity you didn't post sooner. I did the best I could for him. It looks like I
misinterpreted his request. "COBOL im Web" is ambiguous... although reading
it again now, I see what he meant.

Hopefully he will see your response.

Pete.
============= End quote from the19th ===================

"COBOL in dem Web" is ambiguous to a native English speaker (maybe not to a
native  German speaker) as "COBOL in the WEB" (at least to us) indicates the
Web use of COBOL. I notice that in your response you have worded it so as to
remove all doubt. (Excellent!)

I realise now he was looking for a course in COBOL, on the Web. (Note
different preposition).

It seems that James has raised the same point.

While this may very well be a flaw in English, like the illogical nature of
so many English prepositions, it is one which we understand.

Obviously, it is far better if a Native German (or Whatever) speaker
responds to posts of this nature, but there isn't always a guarantee that
one will be monitoring the forum.

The real solution is for all of us to be precise in what we say (no matter
which language we are using).

Pete.
```

<a id="t-8e23539eb7"></a>
## Thread: program-id obsolete? — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL's future, legacy, and obsolescence`](topics.md#future)_

### program-id obsolete?

- **From:** "Wolf Grossi" <X-news@magro-soft.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-18T15:00:50+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1003406406.293126@hagakure.utanet.at>`

```
Hi folks,
    on a UNIX/Linux and MF-Cobol4.1 I find the following situation:

I am calling cobol-programs out of an extended rts using the c-function
"cobcall".
After returning from cobcall, the program is logically cancelled by
"cobcancel".
Works fine, no problem.
For technical reasons I have to link certain programs to another name, e.g.
ln -s CVFILE01.int CVFILE01R.int

Problem:
1:   Program CVFILE01.CBL containing a PROGRAM-ID: CVFILE01.
If I _do_not_ "cobcancel" CVFILE01 and the next call is to to the linked
CVFILE01R, I get the error
119 Name is not unique (Symbol 'CVFILE01')

2:   Program CVFILE01.CBL _does_not_ contain a PROGRAM-ID: CVFILE01.
If I _do_not_ "cobcancel" CVFILE01 and the next call is to to the linked
CVFILE01R, it works OK.

Apparently it's ok to skip the PROGRAM-ID, I just wonder if there are
side-effects I possibly have to consider.

Thanks for reading and comments
Wolf
```

#### ↳ Re: program-id obsolete?

- **From:** "Rick Price" <rick@hpdsoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-18T16:45:15+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9qmu59$oc41u$1@ID-39799.news.dfncis.de>`
- **References:** `<1003406406.293126@hagakure.utanet.at>`

```
I've got an idea that MF Cobol (for historical reasons) only stores up to 8
characters internally as program name.  I don't know if that can be
increased.

So although it recognises the file name as different when loading, it uses
the first 8 characters to check if  something of the same name has already
been loaded.
```

## Thread: Fatal error: "buffer not empty" in a Micro Focus Unix application — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fatal error: "buffer not empty" in a Micro Focus Unix application

- **From:** "Panos Zotos" <pzotos@syntax.gr>
- **Date:** 2001-10-18T16:28:13+03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9qmlct$hvt$1@usenet.otenet.gr>`

```
Hi all,

We are using Micro Focus OCDS 4.0 to develop some Unix applications on SCO
OpenServer 5.05.

One of these applications is installed on a 5.05 server and there are about
50 users that make use of it. Lately we keep getting some unexpected runtime
errors, where the only message we get is

"fatal error : buffer not empty"

and the application stops. We then restart it and it works correctly until
the error appears again, in a completely unexpected manner. We are getting 5
to 10 of these errors per day, and we have never faced a data corruption
problem, although the application stops abnormally.

Does anybody have seen this problem before ?

Thanks in advance for your reply.

Panos Zotos
Athens, Greece
```

- **References:** `<9qmlct$hvt$1@usenet.otenet.gr>`

```

Wild guess - maybe you have a SCSI configuration problem?  Check in your
/usr/adm/messages file to see if anything is logged in there.


Regards,

Greg
```

<a id="t-fdba7dce83"></a>
## Thread: Fillers???? — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Fillers????

- **From:** JONESEY <mrjones_101@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-18T20:28:40+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tabust8jr54qdntsttce91hlvd4hg6bn5o@4ax.com>`

```
So this is probably a stupid question....but could someone explain fillers to me in sequential files...why do we need them and how do you know how
many to put in??

Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: Fillers????

- **From:** "Ron" <Ron@NoSpamForMe.Com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-18T15:48:35-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9qnf77$sef$1@suaar1ac.prod.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<tabust8jr54qdntsttce91hlvd4hg6bn5o@4ax.com>`

```
FILLER is any part of the record description that is undefined
or any part of the record description you do not intend to use in
your program. FILLER *DOES NOT* necessarily mean the data
contains SPACES.  RTM.

=================================================================

"JONESEY" <mrjones_101@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:tabust8jr54qdntsttce91hlvd4hg6bn5o@4ax.com...
> So this is probably a stupid question....but could someone explain fillers
to me in sequential files...why do we need them and how do you know how
> many to put in??
>
> Thanks
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fillers????

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-18T22:34:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BCF5A70.C995AE0D@home.com>`
- **References:** `<tabust8jr54qdntsttce91hlvd4hg6bn5o@4ax.com> <9qnf77$sef$1@suaar1ac.prod.compuserve.com>`

```


Ron wrote:

> FILLER is any part of the record description that is undefined
> or any part of the record description you do not intend to use in
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Thanks

Well that tells you what a FILLER is but doesn't answer your 'Why".

Firstly they don't apply JUST to sequential files, but all - Relative, ISAM and
Sequential. You design a system :-

FD Customerfile. (the "Old' file)
01 CustomerRecord.
 05 Cus-PrimeKey                pic x(05).
 05 Cus-Address.
   10 Cus-AddressLine1        pic x....
   10 Cus-Address Line 2 etc.....

Having run the system for say eighteen months, somebody suggests, 'It would be
nice to have the Customer's Current Balance in the record". You are stumped -
you don't have any spare space.

To get over the above you now have to create a "New"file :-

FD OldCustomerfile. (the "Old' file)
01 OldCustomerRecord.
 05 OldCus-PrimeKey                pic x(05).
 05 OldCus-Address.
   10 OldCus-AddressLine1        pic x....
   10 OldCus-Address Line 2 etc.....

FD Customerfile. (the "New' file)
01 CustomerRecord.
 05 Cus-PrimeKey                pic x(05).
 05 Cus-Address.
   10 Cus-AddressLine1        pic x....
   10 Cus-Address Line 2 etc.....
 05 Cus-Balance                    pic s9(06)v9(02).

1. Read in Old file
2. Move fields to "New" record
3. Initialize Cus-Balance
4. write Newfile record

Assuming some 30 programs use this file format - you now have 30 re-compiles.

The other alternative is take the "Old" record and anticipate you "might" be
asked to add information as the system progresses. So your 'Old' record looks
like :-

   10 OldCus-AddressLine1        pic x....
   10 OldCus-Address Line 2 etc.....
    10 filler                                    pic x(20).

and in the "New" record you show :-

   10 Cus-Address Line 2 etc.....
 05 Cus-Balance                     pic 9(06)v9(02)    = 8 chars.
 05 filler                                   pic x(12).

You still have to go through the conversion routine above, ensuring you
initialize Cus-Balance. However, if ONLY two of your programs access that
Cus-Balance field, it is only those two programs that immediately need to be
recompiled. For peace of mind of course, you make sure all get recompiled.

FILLERS are much more likely to be used with "historical" or Master files,
primarily ISAMs, but can occur in sequentials if needed.  How much FILLER space
do you need - depends upon the complexity of the system - you might get away
with pic x(20) or somebody else could allocate pic x(100). But whichever you do,
change the record format and you still have to go through the conversion
routines above.

I assume you are a student - so there are two possibilities.

a) Your instructor has given you a test data file to use as input :-

123456 Smith          John J.            30.00
234679 Brown         Michael, B.    29.00

and in the record format he has included FILLERS so that you can easily see the
fields.

b) You are being asked to display or prepare a print line :-

01 DisplayPrintRecord.
    05 IdNumber                     pic x(06).
    05 filler                               pic x(03).
    05 LastName                     pic x(20).
    05 filler                               pic x(01).
    05 FirstNames                    pic x(10).
    05 filler                               pic x(03).
    05 HourlyRate                    pic zz.99  blank when zero.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Fillers????

- **From:** Charles <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-23T16:06:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<RzgB7.39697$ev2.46783@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<tabust8jr54qdntsttce91hlvd4hg6bn5o@4ax.com>`

```
On Thu, 18 Oct 2001 20:28:40 +0100, in article
<tabust8jr54qdntsttce91hlvd4hg6bn5o@4ax.com>, JONESEY stated 
>
>So this is probably a stupid question....but could someone explain fillers to me in sequential files...why do we need them and how do you know how
>many to put in??
>
>Thanks

In a file you may want a filler to reserve some unused space for future use,
or/and as a work area.  

A second reason for a filler is if you have an area used for more than one thing
and it may have a redfine statement.  

Sometimes a file may have more than one record type and for one or more record
types there may be space that is unused.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fillers????

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-10-23T14:16:39-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bpiB7.11201$v21.1901265@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<tabust8jr54qdntsttce91hlvd4hg6bn5o@4ax.com> <RzgB7.39697$ev2.46783@www.newsranger.com>`

```
Filler is data space that is never referenced by a name.  It may be used by
other programs, it may be reserved for future use, or it may be someone
elses file and you do not have a clue what it is because you were never
told.  Often, it is obsolete data. It could also be referenced by a
different method ... a higher level data name or a redefines clause.

You need them because there is something in the field at that spot, and if
you do not put something in your record description, the data you are using
will not be alligned in the buffer correctly.

Often, with output, you fill a line with spaces, then populate the
non-filler portions of the record with data.  The filler remains as blank,
so that you can read it ... IE-spaces between the data column on successive
lines of output.

"Charles" <nospam@newsranger.com> wrote in message
news:RzgB7.39697$ev2.46783@www.newsranger.com...
> On Thu, 18 Oct 2001 20:28:40 +0100, in article
> <tabust8jr54qdntsttce91hlvd4hg6bn5o@4ax.com>, JONESEY stated
> >
> >So this is probably a stupid question....but could someone explain
fillers to me in sequential files...why do we need them and how do you know
how
> >many to put in??
> >
> >Thanks
>
> In a file you may want a filler to reserve some unused space for future
use,
> or/and as a work area.
>
> A second reason for a filler is if you have an area used for more than one
thing
> and it may have a redfine statement.
>
> Sometimes a file may have more than one record type and for one or more
record
> types there may be space that is unused.
>
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fillers????

- **From:** "Editor" <Editor@aboutlegacycoding.com>

### Re: Leap year logic

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-18T20:07:04-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9qnuj601q3q@enews3.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<9qg3lo$bps$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <9qg5db$fuu$1@hermes.nz.eds.com> <3BCC535C.3F406748@mb.sympatico.ca> <3BCD181A.D60D0F93@acm.org> <3BCE6F0C.5FF6E845@Azonic.co.nz> <Xns913E900C8177Cjacampbellacslinknet@203.134.67.67>`

```
I thought the Gregorian calendar was put into place (in some
parts of the world) in the 16th century?  Later in the most of
the remainder of the world.

"James Campbell" <jacampbell@acslink.net.nospam.au> wrote in
message
news:Xns913E900C8177Cjacampbellacslinknet@203.134.67.67...
> Actually, the Gregorian Calender was designed to re-synch with
324AD -
> that's why a number of days had to be dropped when switching
from the
> Julian; and, because the leap days in 100, 200 and 300 are
part of the
> Gregorian Calender, Cristmas is now three days later that the
shortest day
> of the year (at least for the folks "up there"), although is
was timed to
> co-incide with the shortest day.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in
<3BCE6F0C.5FF6E845
> @Azonic.co.nz>:
>
> >Joel C. Ewing wrote:
> >
> >> 1189 years left before 3190 A.D., when we have to get
serious and make a
> >> decision and get started on changing leap year code. NO
PROBLEM!! :)
> >
> >While it is a 3200 year 'cycle' (approx), it is not zero
based.  1584 is
> >the year which had 'correct' alignment. So if 3200 is decided
as the
> >cyclic correction then it will be half a day out by 3184.
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Leap year logic

- **From:** "JerryP" <news@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-19T21:26:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
"Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com> wrote in message
news:9qnuj601q3q@enews3.newsguy.com...
> I thought the Gregorian calendar was put into place (in some
> parts of the world) in the 16th century?  Later in the most of
> the remainder of the world.
>

Yeah, it wasn't until 1752 that the US and UK adopted the Gregorian
calendar. They had to drop the eleven days then.

Of course the Orthodox Church holds that anything the Roman Church does is
automatically wrong, so the Orthodox Church doesn't use the Gregorian
calendar. That's why the October Revolution occurred in November and
Orthodox Christmas is in January.
```

```
Howard Brazee wrote:

> On 19-Oct-2001, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> all.

Howard,

I deliberately used "dustbin" - I honestly don't know what the equivalent is
down in your part of the world or up here. We don't use 'bins' but plastic bags.
Could it be garbage "pail" or "bucket" or "container" ? Canucks - please supply
Canuck 'name'.

As a footnote - when I left UK we were just starting to use a metal container,
with 'metal ribs' not totally cylindrically metal like a dustbin, which
contained a plastic bag that the dust man "extracted" - not too useful if you
wanted to put coal fire embers into it !

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Leap year logic

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-10-22T16:43:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d1YA7.114665$%B.8927716@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<9qg3lo$bps$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <9qg5db$fuu$1@hermes.nz.eds.com> <3BCC535C.3F406748@mb.sympatico.ca> <3BCD181A.D60D0F93@acm.org> <3BCE6F0C.5FF6E845@Azonic.co.nz> <Xns913E900C8177Cjacampbellacslinknet@203.134.67.67> <9qnuj601q3q@enews3.newsguy.com> <xU0A7.1037552$ai2.78311211@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3BD0AA14.6B74@paralynx.com> <3BD0D6C8.F857CEB0@home.com> <9r1bnj$7cd$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3BD448EA.1CBC5567@home.com>`

```

On 22-Oct-2001, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:

> > It took me forever to realize what Brits were talking about when they
> > mentioned a dust man.  In America, dust and garbage aren't the same
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> you wanted to put coal fire embers into it !

I grew up calling the outside one a "garbage can", but accepted "trash can"
as a synonym.  Inside, they were waste baskets.   I bet there are regional
variations in our country that I haven't noticed.  But I would have noticed
if I had heard an American (US) say "dust bin".   I have used the word
"pail" used in this context.  I may have heard "bucket" as well, I'm not
sure.

Kitchens produce garbage, offices produce trash.

Recycle containers are often called "bins", for some reason.

P.S.  I don't like saying "America", when I can say "U.S.A." to be more
specific when it is not obvious that I am not including Canada or the rest
of North America or the Americas - but I can't say a "USian".   (I wrote
"nor" above, but think "or" expresses my meaning better).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Leap year logic

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-22T17:34:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BD45A4F.1FA0EE20@home.com>`
- **References:** `<9qg3lo$bps$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <9qg5db$fuu$1@hermes.nz.eds.com> <3BCC535C.3F406748@mb.sympatico.ca> <3BCD181A.D60D0F93@acm.org> <3BCE6F0C.5FF6E845@Azonic.co.nz> <Xns913E900C8177Cjacampbellacslinknet@203.134.67.67> <9qnuj601q3q@enews3.newsguy.com> <xU0A7.1037552$ai2.78311211@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3BD0AA14.6B74@paralynx.com> <3BD0D6C8.F857CEB0@home.com> <9r1bnj$7cd$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3BD448EA.1CBC5567@home.com> <d1YA7.114665$%B.8927716@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```


Howard Brazee wrote:

> On 22-Oct-2001, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:
>
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
> "nor" above, but think "or" expresses my meaning better).

You picked up on some interesting usage of words - consider the following UK
meanings :-

- "Jack and Jill went up; the hill to fetch a pail of water...."
- "There's a hole in my bucket, Eliza, Eliza....".
-  waste basket - in ye olden dayes, that's how they started out - they were
wicker baskets in offices (waste paper baskets), subsequently replaced by metal
and plastic.
- bin - dustbin and in a factory/hardware store a bin for holding different
loose items, nuts, bolts etc. (I think 'bin' also has a reference to data
processing ?)
- garbage - somebody makes a ridiculous statement, "That's a load of garbage";
the only other usage I can think of is GIGO - which we took from you some 40
years ago.
- trash - mostly used as "to trash something....", destroy or throw away.
- can - a can of 7oz Green Giant Mexicorn or slightly larger if you are
referring to commercial size Heinz tomato sauce/ketchup. Get beyond that and you
are into drums used for oil etc. I was intrigued for years that Americans(U.S.)
"canned" fruits/veggies. I *know* you do also "can" but generally you are
talking about boiling or sterilizing  in hot water, which in UK is 'bottling".
- container - a pretty generic word, but doesn't really relate to any of the
above..

Anybody from UK wish to clarify any of above, if I haven't covered current usage
?

PS: Much more interesting topic than current thread on "GO TO". Hitler !

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
Howard Brazee wrote:

> As a young adult, when I came across the word "midden" (in reference to
> coprolites), I had to look it up, as it was completely unfamiliar to me.

As you well knew - we'd have to look it up too <G>. Midden, "a dung hill or heap
of refuse". Coprolite, "petrified dung of extinct vertebrates". You learn
something new every day. You must have had some weird hobbies/interests as a
young adult.
My preference would have been checking the dictionary for words like 'fornicate'
<G>

Still I 'spose with all that cattle down there, Jerry's right to be using the
word midden in the Lone Star State.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Leap year logic

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>

```
You are making me feel provincal, James.  I knew exactly what a midden was.
You never had the joys of moving a two holer?

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
news:3BD5A007.18975902@home.com...
>
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> As you well knew - we'd have to look it up too <G>. Midden, "a dung hill
or heap
> of refuse". Coprolite, "petrified dung of extinct vertebrates". You learn
> something new every day. You must have had some weird hobbies/interests as
a
> young adult.
> My preference would have been checking the dictionary for words like
'fornicate'
> <G>
>
> Still I 'spose with all that cattle down there, Jerry's right to be using
the
> word midden in the Lone Star State.
>
> Jimmy
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Leap year logic

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-23T19:14:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BD5C32C.D3F5C400@home.com>`
- **References:** `<9qg3lo$bps$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <9qg5db$fuu$1@hermes.nz.eds.com> <3BCC535C.3F406748@mb.sympatico.ca> <3BCD181A.D60D0F93@acm.org> <3BCE6F0C.5FF6E845@Azonic.co.nz> <Xns913E900C8177Cjacampbellacslinknet@203.134.67.67> <9qnuj601q3q@enews3.newsguy.com> <xU0A7.1037552$ai2.78311211@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3BD0AA14.6B74@paralynx.com> <3BD0D6C8.F857CEB0@home.com> <9r1bnj$7cd$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3BD448EA.1CBC5567@home.com> <d1YA7.114665$%B.8927716@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <Jh0B7.117853$%B.9176868@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <9r3von$b8n$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3BD5A007.18975902@home.com> <e6iB7.11149$v21.1889347@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```


Donald Tees wrote:

> You are making me feel provincal, James.  I knew exactly what a midden was.
> You never had the joys of moving a two holer?

Assuming we are on the same wavelength - and I'm not sure, the nearest is
venturing into sunny Egypt. The 'toilet' facilities were referred to as a
thunder bucket, an oil drum hidden behind a lousy piece of hessian screening. We
also had honey wagons for collecting the 'deposits'.

Oh the joys, in one's youth, of defending King and Country in far off
places.....

PS: You're right; there's a lot of application of 'midden' in Calgary. And up
there in Drumheller, about an hour north-east, a gold mine for dinosaur hunters,
I'm sure they've found coprolites <G> Check out Tyrrell Museum on the web folks
- it is a magnificent museum. (The British Museum's Albertosaurus comes from
this part of the world).

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Leap year logic

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-23T08:56:09-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9r43uq$pvb$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3BCC535C.3F406748@mb.sympatico.ca> <3BCD181A.D60D0F93@acm.org> <3BCE6F0C.5FF6E845@Azonic.co.nz> <Xns913E900C8177Cjacampbellacslinknet@203.134.67.67> <9qnuj601q3q@enews3.newsguy.com> <xU0A7.1037552$ai2.78311211@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3BD0AA14.6B74@paralynx.com> <3BD0D6C8.F857CEB0@home.com> <9r1bnj$7cd$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3BD448EA.1CBC5567@home.com> <d1YA7.114665$%B.8927716@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <Jh0B7.117853$%B.9176868@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <5F2AC683158CAF4F.4270EE41B23D4C6C.F3E131C7582DE4F2@lp.airnews.net>`

```

"SkippyPB" <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote in message
news:5F2AC683158CAF4F.4270EE41B23D4C6C.F3E131C7582DE4F2@lp.airnews.net...
>
> Where in Texas?  I lived in Dallas for a while and never ever heard
> that term.  All my office mates and acquaintances used garbage cans,
> waste baskets and a pail was a bucket.

in response to Howard Brazee, who wrote, in part:
>
>In Texas we call it a 'Midden' (or "midden heap" or "midden container").


I echo the call for details.  I spent sixteen years in the Hill Country, two
years in Houston, four in Austin, and seventeen in San Antonio, and traveled
extensively for business and recreation over the eastern two-thirds of the
state (never spent much time in the Panhandle or west of Midland), and never
did I hear the term "midden heap" or "midden container".  I could envision
this usage in an area populated primarily from deep Appalachia, where many
archaisms like this survive from Middle English, but I am unaware of such
areas offhand in Texas.  What part of Texas is this term in common usage?

       -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Leap year logic

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **Date:** 2001-10-23T16:29:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0WgB7.1090328$ai2.82948007@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<3BCC535C.3F406748@mb.sympatico.ca> <3BCD181A.D60D0F93@acm.org> <3BCE6F0C.5FF6E845@Azonic.co.nz> <Xns913E900C8177Cjacampbellacslinknet@203.134.67.67> <9qnuj601q3q@enews3.newsguy.com> <xU0A7.1037552$ai2.78311211@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3BD0AA14.6B74@paralynx.com> <3BD0D6C8.F857CEB0@home.com> <9r1bnj$7cd$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3BD448EA.1CBC5567@home.com> <d1YA7.114665$%B.8927716@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <Jh0B7.117853$%B.9176868@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <5F2AC683158CAF4F.4270EE41B23D4C6C.F3E131C7582DE4F2@lp.airnews.net>`

```
"SkippyPB" <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com>
> >>
> >In Texas we call it a 'Midden' (or "midden heap" or "midden container").
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> waste baskets and a pail was a bucket.

Well, I'm in Houston.

It's well known here that folks from Dallas fall into one of two groups:
They either work at 7-11's or dine there. I think their word for 'garbage
can' is 'the street' (sometimes jockularly phrased as "Give it to the
city"). That probably explains the absence of the word in daily discourse. I
could be wrong, though.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Leap year logic

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-10-23T14:00:26-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Z9iB7.11161$v21.1891739@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<3BCC535C.3F406748@mb.sympatico.ca> <3BCD181A.D60D0F93@acm.org> <3BCE6F0C.5FF6E845@Azonic.co.nz> <Xns913E900C8177Cjacampbellacslinknet@203.134.67.67> <9qnuj601q3q@enews3.newsguy.com> <xU0A7.1037552$ai2.78311211@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3BD0AA14.6B74@paralynx.com> <3BD0D6C8.F857CEB0@home.com> <9r1bnj$7cd$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3BD448EA.1CBC5567@home.com> <d1YA7.114665$%B.8927716@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <Jh0B7.117853$%B.9176868@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <5F2AC683158CAF4F.4270EE41B23D4C6C.F3E131C7582DE4F2@lp.airnews.net>`

```
Skippy, it is not the bucket, it is what it is filled with that makes it a
midden.  Sort of like "it is not the program, it is the programmer".  I am
surprised that Texas cowboys pull plumbing along after them in those chuck
wagons ... almost as surprised as I am at Jimmy, living in Calgary for god's
sake ....


"SkippyPB" <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote in message
news:5F2AC683158CAF4F.4270EE41B23D4C6C.F3E131C7582DE4F2@lp.airnews.net...

> enlightened us:

> >> as a synonym.  Inside, they were waste baskets.   I bet there are
regional
> >> variations in our country that I haven't noticed.  But I would have
> >noticed
> >> if I had heard an American (US) say "dust bin".   I have used the word
> >> "pail" used in this context.  I may have heard "bucket" as well, I'm
not

…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> Steve
```

```
<swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote:
```

```
><robaireRemoveThis@sympatico.ca> enlightened us:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…

I should have mentioned that the french word used in Quï¿½bec is
"poubelle", from the family name of Eugï¿½ne Renï¿½ Poubelle,
prefect of the Seine area who ordered its compulsory use for
residential buildings in 1884. There is no mention of how he reacted
to his name being associated with the object.
I guess Thomas Crapper felt the same way about his invention.

Robaire
```

## Thread: Metric calendar, Was: Leap year logic — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Date and calendar processing`](topics.md#dates)_

### Re: Metric calendar, Was: Leap year logic

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-10-19T13:52:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9qpb3h$p73$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<9qg3lo$bps$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <3BCC13B2.F4D7F7C@fujitsu-siemens.com> <LKVy7.512422$Lw3.31320020@news2.aus1.giganews.com> <ihlrstouo2i1c9m66lu8mjk75vji311iho@4ax.com> <Xns913F78A155BB7jacampbellacslinknet@203.134.67.67>`

```

On 18-Oct-2001, jacampbell@acslink.net.nospam.au (James Campbell) wrote:

> Reminds me of what someone once told me about months in Saudi Arabia:
> - a day starts when, after the sun has gone down, you (or the local iman)
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> moon.


That's what my map says.  There are a few places that are 1/2 and even 1/4
off the standard hours.  China is under one time zone (that makes sense to
me - I hate switching clocks around - it would be better to switch schedules
around).   The USSR had its clocks wrong for decades, I don't know whether
Russia has corrected that.

Sometimes Arizona is the same time as California, other times it is the same
time as New Mexico.

While I would like the world to use Zulu time, I am intrigued by a concept
that in the future we could all go to something similar to the Saudis. 
Clocks get set remotely and adjust themselves each day to whatever daylight
standard is desired.  It could still be time zone based, but without
daylight savings time jumps.
```

### Re: Metric calendar, Was: Leap year logic

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bhc0tt8i6vvaghmum9lql2ttkqua5hanei@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<9qg3lo$bps$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <3BCC13B2.F4D7F7C@fujitsu-siemens.com> <LKVy7.512422$Lw3.31320020@news2.aus1.giganews.com> <ihlrstouo2i1c9m66lu8mjk75vji311iho@4ax.com> <Xns913F78A155BB7jacampbellacslinknet@203.134.67.67>`

```
On 19 Oct 2001 11:51:02 +1000 jacampbell@acslink.net.nospam.au (James
Campbell) wrote:

:>Reminds me of what someone once told me about months in Saudi Arabia:
:>- a day starts when, after the sun has gone down, you (or the local iman) 
:>can no longer tell the difference between a white thread and a black thread
:>- a month starts if, at the begining of the day, you can _see_ the new moon.

:>So, if the sky is cloudy at beginning of a day so you can't see the new 
:>moon, the old month continues.  If the clouds then clear, it doesn't make a 
:>difference - you didn't see the new moon at the beginning of the day!  So 
:>the next village over (where it wasn't cloudy) can be on a different month 
:>to you.

:>He told me it made programming the HR system "interesting".

Judaism does not have the problem as the declaration can only be made by the
Jerusalem court.

In the days the calendar was established by the declaration the outlying areas
would not know the day of the start of the month until the messengers got
there.

:>Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote in
:><ihlrstouo2i1c9m66lu8mjk75vji311iho@4ax.com>: 
:>
:>>This adjustment is not a problem in Jewish
:>>law as unlike Saturday which is the seventh day and cannot be altered,
:>>the month required the court to declare it. And the month starts
:>>whenever the court declares it, even if the court deliberately errs.
```

<a id="t-2b3928c6fb"></a>
## Thread: I'AM a student I NEED your help. — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### I'AM a student I NEED your help.

- **From:** Serge (tcccorp) <tcccorp@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-20T09:21:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20011020-112123-528906@foorum.com>`

```

Hello I have a project to my school. I have wrinting this code yesterday but I 
don't know if the syntaxe is correct. I don't have a Cobol compiler . Can you 
help me. Thank a lot


I can send you a email with this code if you want


SELECT AUTEUR ASSIGN TO DSK-AUTEUR
	ORGANISATION INDEXED
	ACCESS MODE SEQUENTIAL
	RECORD KEY NOMPRENOM.
SELECT TITRE ASSIGN TO DSK-TITRE
	ORGANISATION INDEXED
	ACCESS MODE SEQUENTIAL
	RECORD KEY TITROUV.
DATE DIVISION.
FILE SECTION.
FD AUTEUR
01 LAUTEUR
	02 NOMPRENOM.
		03 NOM	PIC X(20).
		03 PRENOM PIC X(15).
	02 NAISS	PIC S9(5) COMP-3.
	02 FILLER PIC X(3).
	02 TITRE_OUV PIC X(20).
FD TITRE
01 LETITRE
	02 TITROUV PIC X(20).
	02 FILLER PIC X(5).
	02 NBEXEM PIC S9(3) COMP-3.
	02 IDAUT PIC X(35).
	02 AUTTITRE PIC X(20).
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
77 CODE PIC X.
88 BONCODE VALUE "A" "S" "I" "N" "T" "Q".
77 TEMPO PIC X(35);
77 LETITRE PIC X(20).
77 NB PIC S9(3) COMP-3.
*
PROCEDURE DIVISION.
	OPEN I-O AUTEUR
		 TITRE.
MENU.
	DISPLAY "A) ARRIVEE D UN OUVRAGE".
	DISPLAY "S) SUPPRESSION D UN OUVRAGE".
	DISPLAY "I) IDENTIFICATION D UN AUTEUR".
	DISPLAY "N) RECHERCHE A PARTIR D UN NOM".
	DISPLAY "T) RECHERCHE A PARTIR D UN OUVRAGE".
	DISPLAY "Q) QUITTER CE PROGRAMME".
IF NOT BONCODE
	DISPLAY "CODE INCONNU"
	GO TO MENU
   END-IF.
IF CODE="Q" CLOSE AUTEUR
		  TITRE
		STOP RUN
   END-IF.
IF CODE="A" CLOSE ARROUV.
IF CODE="S" CLOSE SUPOUV.
IF CODE="I" CLOSE IDENTA.
IF CODE="N" CLOSE RECHNO.
GOTO RECHEROUV.
* CODE T
RECHEROUV.
	DISPLAY " ENTREZ LE TITRE DU LIVRE".
	ACCEPT TITROUV.
	READ TITRE IK
		DISPLAY "TITRE INEXISTANT"
		GOTO MENU
	   END READ
	DISPLAY "NOMBRE EXEMPLAIRE : " NBEXEM " AUTEUR :" IDAUT
	GO TO MENU
* CODE I
IDENTA.
	DISPLAY "VEUILLEZ RENTRER LE NOM".
	ACCEPT NOM.
	DISPLAY "VEUILLEZ RENTRER LE PRENOM".
	ACCEPT PRENOM.
	READ NOMPRENOM IK
		DISPLAY "AUTEUR INCONNU"
		GOTO MENU
	   END READ
	DISPLAY "DATE DE NAISSANCE : " NAISS 
	MOVE TITRE_OUV TO TITROUV
	READ TITRE.
	DISPLAY "TITRE :" TITROUV "NB EXEMPLAIRE :" NBEXEM 
	MOVE TITROUV TO TEMPO
	PERFORM UNTIL TEMPO NOT = AUTTITRE
		MOVE AUTTITRE TO TITROUV
		READ TITRE
		DISPLAY "TITRE :" TITROUV "NB EXEMPLAIRE :" NBEXEM 
	   END-PERFORM
	GO TO MENU
* CODE N
RECHNO.
	DISPLAY "VEUILLEZ RENTRER LE NOM".
	ACCEPT NOM.
	MOVE LOW-VALUE TO PRENOM
	START AUTEUR KEY > NOM IK
		DISPLAY "AUTEUR INCONNU"
		GOTO MENU
	  END-START
RET-REC.
* il y a forcement un titre sinon l'auteur n'est pas dans la biblio
	READ AUTEUR NEXT AT END.
		DISPLAY "FIN DE LA LISTE"
		GO TO MENU
	   END-READ.
	DISPLAY "DATE DE NAISSANCE : " NAISS 
	MOVE TITRE_OUV TO TITROUV
	READ TITRE.
	DISPLAY "TITRE :" TITROUV "NB EXEMPLAIRE :" NBEXEM 
	MOVE TITROUV TO TEMPO
	PERFORM UNTIL TEMPO NOT = AUTTITRE
		MOVE AUTTITRE to TITROUV
		READ TITRE
		DISPLAY "TITRE :" TITROUV "NB EXEMPLAIRE :" NBEXEM 
	   END-PERFORM
	GO TO RET-REC.
* CODE A
	DISPLAY "VEUILLEZ RENTRER LE TITRE".
	ACCEPT TITROUV. 
	DISPLAY "VEUILLEZ RENTRER LE NOMBRE D'EXEMPLAIRES".
	ACCEPT NB.
* si pas ce titre n'existe pas alors on fabrique le NOMPRENOM
	READ TITRE IK
		DISPLAY "VEUILLEZ RENTRER LE NOM DE L'AUTEUR DE " TITROUV
		ACCEPT NOM
		DISPLAY "VEUILLEZ RENTRER LE PRENOM DE L'AUTEUR DE " TITROUV
		ACCEPT PRENOM
* si pas cet auteur n'existe pas alors on fabrique le NAISS
	READ NOMPRENOM IK
		DISPLAY "VEUILLEZ LA DATE DE NAISSANCE DE " NOMPRENOM
		ACCEPT NAISS
		GO TO CREATION
	   END READ
*si auteur existe mais pas son titre alors on cherche le chainage
		GO TO CHERCHEC
        END-READ.

	ADD NBEXEM TO NB
	REWRITE TITRE.
RETOURA
	DISPLAY "NOMBRE D'EXEMPLAIRE POUR " TITROUV " : " NBEXEM
	GO TO MENU.
* creation d'un auteur
CREATION.
	MOVE  TITROUV TO TITRE_OUV.
	WRITE AUTEUR IK
		DISPLAY "FICHIER HS"
		GO TO MENU
	   END-WRITE.
	MOVE TITROUV TO AUTTITRE
	WRITE TITRE IK
		DISPLAY "FICHIER HS"
		GO TO MENU
	   END-WRITE.
	GO TO RETOURA.
CHERCHEC.
	MOVE TITROUV TO LETITRE.
	MOVE TITRE_OUV TO TITROUV.
	READ TITRE IK
	  END-READ.
	MOVE AUTTITRE TO TEMPO.
	MOVE LETITRE TO AUTTITRE
	REWRITE TITRE.
	MOVE LETITRE TO TITROUV
	MOVE TEMPO TO AUTTITRE
	WRITE TITRE IK
		DISPLAY "FICHIER HS"
		GO TO MENU
	   END-WRITE.
	GO TO RETOURA.
* code S
SUPOUV.
	DISPLAY "VEUILLEZ RENTRER LE TITRE".
	ACCEPT TITROUV. 
	READ TITRE IK
		DISPLAY "TITRE INEXISTANT"
		GO TO MENU
	  END-READ.

	DISPLAY "VEUILLEZ RENTRER LE NOMBRE D'EXEMPLAIRES".
	ACCEPT NB.
	SUBSTRACT NBEXEM TO NB.
	IF NBEXEM <1 
		MOVE IDAUT TO NOMPRENOM
		READ AUTEUR.
		IF TITROUV = AUTTITRE 
			DISPLAY "SUPPRESSION DE L'AUTEUR " NOM PRENOM
			DELETE AUTEUR.
			GOTO SUITES
		 END-IF.

		MOVE AUTTITRE TO LETITRE
		MOVE TITROUV TO TEMPO
		PERFORM TEST AFTER UNTIL AUTITRE=TEMPO
			MOVE AUTTITRE TO TITROUV
			READ TITRE
		END-PERFORM.
		MOVE LETITRE TO AUTTITRE.
		REWRITE TITRE.
		MOVE TITROUV TO TITRE_OUV.
		REWRITE AUTEUR.
		MOVE TEMPO TO TITROUV.
		GO TO SUITES
	 ELSE
		REWRITE TITRE.
		DISPLAY "IL RESTE " NBEXEM " DE " TITROUV
		GO TO MENU
	END-IF.	
SUITES.
	DISPLAY "SUPPRESSION DU TITRE : " TITROUV
	DELETE TITRE.
	GO TO MENU.
```

#### ↳ Re: I'AM a student I NEED your help.

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-20T20:00:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3BD1D960.D8684A63@home.com>`
- **References:** `<20011020-112123-528906@foorum.com>`

```


"Serge (tcccorp)" wrote:

> Hello I have a project to my school. I have wrinting this code yesterday but I
> don't know if the syntaxe is correct. I don't have a Cobol compiler . Can you
> help me. Thank a lot
>

Serge,

Part of the learning process is to put your source through a compiler to check for
syntax errors firstly, before your own logic checks. The program is well laid out,
but you will learn far more if you put it through a compiler first.

Some tips -

(a) you are missing important "." (periods/full stops), and have a semi-colon (:)
where you should have a period.

(b) CODE is a RESERVED word - use Un-CODE or le-CODE if your prefer

(c) You have specified 01 un-CODE with Level 88's and then you test :-

    if un-Code = "xxxxx"

try the following :-

01 Un-Code                pic x.
  88  ARRIVEE           value "A", "a".
  88  SUPPRESSE      value "S", "s".  *> Suppression is probably a reserved
                                                                    word
  88 IDENTA              value "I", "i".
  88 RECHERCHE     value "R", "r".
  88 QUITER              value "Q", "q".

Evaluate true
    when ARRIVEE perform.......
    when SUPPRESSE perform....
    when IDENTA perform.....
    when RECHERCHE  perform.....
    when QUITER  CLOSE AUTEUR
                                           TITRE
                              STOP RUN
    when other
                   DISPLAY "CODE INCONNU"
                   GO TO MENU
End-evaluate

- see - you don't reaslly need BonCode.

(d) You can't tell the program to CLOSE a PARAGRAPH  - you perform PARAGRAPHs or
GO TO PARAGRAPHs :-.

IF CODE = "A" CLOSE ARROUV.
*> I can't see a reference to ARROUV

(e) it is GO TO  and not GOTO

I could go on and on, but you need to put it through a compiler first.

Jimmy, Calgary AB

> I can send you a email with this code if you want
>
…[210 more quoted lines elided]…
> User of http://www.foorum.com/. The best tools for usenet searching.
```

#### ↳ Re: I'AM a student I NEED your help.

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-20T16:27:34-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<C67B25692F213B3C.E181A2717E8ABFE2.D59E50C20D726219@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<20011020-112123-528906@foorum.com>`

```
On 20 Oct 2001 09:21:23 GMT, Serge (tcccorp) <tcccorp@yahoo.com>
enlightened us:

>
>Hello I have a project to my school. I have wrinting this code yesterday but I 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You didn't say what platform this is for, so most can only tell you
generally what might be wrong.  I found a couple of things.  Look
below.

>
>SELECT AUTEUR ASSIGN TO DSK-AUTEUR
…[44 more quoted lines elided]…
>   END-IF.

You never read or retreived the code, so you are going to have an
endless loop.  At the very least you need:

ACCEPT CODE FROM CONSOLE.

Before IF NOT BONCODE



>IF CODE="Q" CLOSE AUTEUR
>		  TITRE
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>IF CODE="N" CLOSE RECHNO.

All of the above CLOSE will fail because you never defined those
files.


>GOTO RECHEROUV.
>* CODE T
>RECHEROUV.
>	DISPLAY " ENTREZ LE TITRE DU LIVRE".
>	ACCEPT TITROUV.

You are accepting the key which is defined in an FD.  While, depending
on platform, this may work, it is bad coding.  You should ACCEPT a
Working-Storage field, edit/validate it, then move it to the KEY field
if it is valid.

>	READ TITRE IK
>		DISPLAY "TITRE INEXISTANT"
>		GOTO MENU
>	   END READ

The above DISPLAY and GOTO (should be GO TO) will happen every time
the READ is done - I would guess this is not what you had in mind.

>	DISPLAY "NOMBRE EXEMPLAIRE : " NBEXEM " AUTEUR :" IDAUT
>	GO TO MENU
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>	ACCEPT PRENOM.

As before you are accepting key field defined in the FD.  Much better
to ACCEPT Working-Storage fields, edit/validate them, then move them
to the key fields.

>	READ NOMPRENOM IK
>		DISPLAY "AUTEUR INCONNU"
>		GOTO MENU
>	   END READ

The above DISPLAY and GOTO (should be GO TO) will happen very time you
read the file.  Is that what you want?  If so, why the following code?

>	DISPLAY "DATE DE NAISSANCE : " NAISS 
>	MOVE TITRE_OUV TO TITROUV
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>	ACCEPT NOM.

See previous comments about accepting key fields.

>	MOVE LOW-VALUE TO PRENOM
>	START AUTEUR KEY > NOM IK
>		DISPLAY "AUTEUR INCONNU"
>		GOTO MENU
                          Should be GO TO
>	  END-START
>RET-REC.
…[111 more quoted lines elided]…
>	GO TO MENU.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

If a motorist cuts you off, just turn the other cheek.
Nothing gets the message across like a good mooning. 

~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

<a id="t-faa6bb4dca"></a>
## Thread: GO TO questions — 31 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### GO TO questions

- **From:** "Jean-Ren��� Duval" <jrduval@videotron.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-10-21T11:23:27-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3OBA7.931$p64.161630@wagner.videotron.net>`

```
Hello.

I had a discussion with a colleage about that famous GO TO. He said no
matter what the circumstances never use any GO TO because this is bad
programming practices. In a perfect world, section coding should not exceed
2 pages in length and GO TOs are useless. But in a real world, I see so many
programs with 4 or more pages so if I don't use GOTOs, entire sections would
have to be rewritten. And even when I'm coding new ones, I'm using those
GOTO into my code. Is it a good pratice or should I use some kind of switch
instead ?

Another one. Do you know any places that I could find some examples on how
to use system variables (MVS) in a Cobol programs.

Thank you for your response.

Jean-Ren� Duval
jrduvalxxx@videotron.ca
```

#### ↳ Re: GO TO questions

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praul@isot.NOSPAM.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-21T11:22:46-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tt5tnq704epfb8@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<3OBA7.931$p64.161630@wagner.videotron.net>`

```
My opinion only:

   There is never a good reason to use a GOTO in the average COBOL program.
If the program seems to have sections so large that you *need* GOTO's,  the
program
was not designed correctly.

  Now the generic statement that GOTOs are bad is ridiculous too. There
*are* situations
where you need to use a GOTO - like when you are using COBOL to write stuff
that would be better written in assembler. <grin>

  Actually, the only acceptable situation where I allow GOTOs in my shop is
when it is in legacy
code; I've yet to see a need for it in any newly written code, and that
include CICS modules.
Under CICS, there are some arguments that make sense about using a GOTO in
exception
handling, but I personally have not seen it make enough CPU difference to
say that it is a
good idea.

  YMMV, and there are other people here whose opinions I respect who
disagree.

-Paul

"Jean-Ren� Duval" <jrduval@videotron.ca> wrote in message
news:3OBA7.931$p64.161630@wagner.videotron.net...
> Hello.
>
> I had a discussion with a colleage about that famous GO TO. He said no
> matter what the circumstances never use any GO TO because this is bad
> programming practices. In a perfect world, section coding should not
exceed
> 2 pages in length and GO TOs are useless. But in a real world, I see so
many
> programs with 4 or more pages so if I don't use GOTOs, entire sections
would
```

#### ↳ Re: GO TO questions

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-21T16:31:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tLCA7.37359$ev2.44194@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<3OBA7.931$p64.161630@wagner.videotron.net>`

```
Hi JR,

Here we go again. You've probably started another fire storm, but my view is
that GO TOs s/b (c/b) used to GO TO pgraph-exit but nowhere else. This
convention eliminates the need for some ugly nested IFs and also makes things
easier for the code reader in this respect:

If you code: IF WS-X = y
GO TO 100-EXIT
END-IF

IF WS-A = a
GO TO 100-EXIT
END-IF 
etc.

Code Readers can quickly see that this pgraph doesn't (or does) concern them,
and can procede fairly quickly. Without the GO TO they have to "keep their
place" through a complex IF to determine whether the pgraph is of interest.

Well, that's my take. I'm sure you'll get others. We report; you decide.

Re. your 2nd ques: mvshelp.com has an IBM Manual page. Click on the COBOL
manuals; then go to (go to?) the bottom of the page; the 3rd manual from the
bottom s/b the COBOL Ref Man; click there and then look in the manual index at
the bottom. You might try the COBOL Pgmmer's guide, too. It's 1 or 2 entries
above the REF Man.

Regards, Jack.    




In article <3OBA7.931$p64.161630@wagner.videotron.net>, Jean-Ren� Duval says...
>
>Hello.
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: GO TO questions

- **From:** "JerryP" <news@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-10-21T17:56:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2%DA7.1064745$ai2.80595301@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<3OBA7.931$p64.161630@wagner.videotron.net>`

```

"Jean-Ren� Duval" <jrduval@videotron.ca> wrote in message
news:3OBA7.931$p64.161630@wagner.videotron.net...
> Hello.
>
>  But in a real world, I see so many
> programs with 4 or more pages so if I don't use GOTOs, entire sections
would
> have to be rewritten.

And your dilemma is?

>And even when I'm coding new ones, I'm using those
> GOTO into my code. Is it a good pratice or should I use some kind of
switch
> instead ?

No switches. No GO TOs. No SECTIONS. Use PERFORMS, CALL, and INVOKE. Once
you get the hang of it (takes about a year), the thought processes become
automatic.

Here's the reason: While it is possible to write buggy, hard-to-follow, and
error-prone code in any language with any constructs, it's easy, really
easy, to make a mess with GOTOs; GOTO-less programming imposes a mental
discipline which minimizes these problems (or so many people believe).
```
